topics.title: Test on stress-strain characteristics of mild steel and Tor steel by conducting uniaxial tension test on rod specimens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL407 - PCCEL407 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Test on stress-strain characteristics of mild steel and Tor steel by conducting uniaxial tension test on rod specimens # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) The **Uniaxial Tension Test** is a fundamental destructive material testing experiment conducted on a Universal Testing Machine (UTM) to determine the mechanical response of ductile metallic rod specimens — specifically **Mild Steel (Fe 410 / MS Grade)** and **Tor Steel (High Yield Strength Deformed / HYSD bars — Fe 415, Fe 500, Fe 550)** — under a gradually increasing axial tensile load applied along a single principal axis until fracture occurs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The test characterizes four critical engineering properties: **(i) Proportional Limit, (ii) Elastic Limit / Yield Point, (iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS), and (iv) Fracture / Breaking Strength**, along with **Percentage Elongation** and **Percentage Reduction in Area** — all derived from a single load–elongation curve converted to a stress–strain plot. The governing Indian Standard codes are: - **IS 432 (Part 1)** – Specification for Mild Steel and Medium Tensile Steel Bars. - **IS 1786** – Specification for High Strength Deformed Steel Bars and Wires (Tor Steel). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Chewing Gum and the Rubber Band" Imagine stretching two different materials by hand: - **Mild Steel (Chewing Gum analogy before breaking):** When you pull it gently, it stretches elastically and returns to its original length. Pull a *little* more and it suddenly "gives way" — stretching dramatically without any extra effort (this is the **yield point**). Then it stretches enormously like chewing gum before finally snapping. Mild steel **warns** you before it breaks. - **Tor Steel (Bungee Cord analogy):** It also stretches, but unlike mild steel, it does **not** have a sharp "give." It resists the pull uniformly all the way, stretching slowly and progressively. You have to measure its yield *indirectly* using the **0.2% offset method** because the yield point is mathematically "smeared out" by the manufacturing process (cold twisting / thermo-mechanical treatment). > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Takeaway:** Both materials are *ductile* (they stretch before breaking), but their *internal micro-structure* — produced by different manufacturing routes — gives them distinct stress–strain "fingerprints." ## 1.3 The Two Specimens at a Glance | Property | Mild Steel (MS) | Tor Steel (HYSD) | |---|---|---| | **Carbon Content** | **0.15% – 0.25%** | **0.20% – 0.30%** + microalloying | | **Manufacturing** | Hot-rolled, no subsequent deformation | Cold-Twisted Deformed (CTD) / Thermo-Mechanically Treated (TMT) | | **Surface** | Plain / smooth round | Deformed with transverse ribs (lug pattern) for better concrete bond | | **Yield Behaviour** | **Sharp / Distinct yield point** (Upper & Lower) | **Gradual yielding** → 0.2% proof stress used | | **Typical Yield Stress** | **250 N/mm²** (Fe 410) | **415 / 500 / 550 N/mm²** | | **Typical UTS** | **410 – 530 N/mm²** | **500 – 600+ N/mm²** | | **Ductility (% Elongation)** | **20% – 30%** (very high) | **10% – 20%** (lower) | | **Failure Mode** | Cup-and-cone ductile fracture | Brittle-like shear / oblique fracture | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Units Used Throughout:** Force → **N / kN** · Area → **mm²** · Stress → **N/mm² = MPa** · Length → **mm** · Strain → **dimensionless (mm/mm)**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four Engineering Phases of a Ductile Metal Under Tension When a cylindrical rod is pulled, it passes through four distinct regions on the stress–strain plot: ### **Phase A — Elastic Region (Linear & Non-Linear)** - Stress (σ) is **directly proportional** to strain (ε). - Obeys **Hooke's Law**: σ = E · ε, where **E = Young's Modulus ≈ 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** for steel. - Strain is **fully recoverable** — the specimen returns to its original length on unloading. - Ends at the **Proportional Limit (A)**. - Microscopic event: atomic bonds stretch but do not slip. ### **Phase B — Yield Region (Mild Steel) / 0.2% Proof Stress (Tor Steel)** - For **Mild Steel:** The curve shows a *peak* (Upper Yield Point, B) followed by a *flat plateau* (Lower Yield Point, C). The material deforms plastically at *nearly constant stress*. This flatness is caused by **dislocation pinning by carbon / nitrogen atoms (Lüders bands propagation)**. - For **Tor Steel:** Cold working / TMT has already pre-strained the bar, eliminating the sharp yield. The 0.2% offset method is used. ### **Phase C — Strain Hardening Region (Plastic Plateau)** - Stress rises again due to **work hardening** — dislocations multiply and tangle, requiring more force to continue deformation. - Ends at the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (D)**, σ_u = P_max / A₀. ### **Phase D — Necking and Fracture** - Localised cross-sectional reduction ("neck") forms at the weakest point. - Engineering stress *appears* to drop (because A₀ is still used in the denominator), but **true stress continues to rise**. - Fracture occurs at point **E** with stress = P_fracture / A₀ (engineering fracture stress). ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Print-Ready Cheat Sheet) | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Original Cross-Sectional Area** | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d_0^2}{4}$ | d₀ = original mean diameter | mm² | | 2 | **Engineering Stress** | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | P = applied tensile load | N/mm² (MPa) | | 3 | **Engineering Strain** | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \dfrac{L - L_0}{L_0}$ | L₀ = original gauge length | dimensionless | | 4 | **Young's Modulus** | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{P L_0}{A_0 \, \Delta L}$ | Slope of elastic line | N/mm² | | 5 | **Yield Stress (Mild Steel)** | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{P_y}{A_0}$ | P_y = load at yield point | N/mm² | | 6 | **0.2% Proof Stress (Tor Steel)** | Read at ε = 0.002 offset from elastic line | — | N/mm² | | 7 | **Ultimate Tensile Stress** | $\sigma_u = \dfrac{P_{max}}{A_0}$ | P_max = maximum load reached | N/mm² | | 8 | **Breaking Stress** | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P_{br}}{A_0}$ | P_br = load at fracture | N/mm² | | 9 | **Percentage Elongation** | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | L_f = final gauge length after fracture | % | | 10 | **Percentage Reduction in Area** | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | A_f = final area at neck (cup-and-cone) | % | | 11 | **Modulus of Resilience** | $U_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | Energy absorbed up to yield | N·mm/mm³ | | 12 | **Factor of Safety (Working Stress)** | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{working}}$ | — | dimensionless | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** Engineering stress uses the **original area A₀**. True stress uses the **instantaneous area A_i**. For KTU board problems, the **engineering convention** is assumed unless explicitly stated. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mild Steel** is preferred for general structural work, nails, chains, and low-stress welded structures where high ductility is required (e.g., earthquake-resistant detailing needs plastic deformation capacity). - **Tor Steel (HYSD)** is the **backbone of modern RCC construction** in India — used as longitudinal reinforcement in beams, columns, slabs, and footings. Its high yield strength reduces the quantity of steel required, while its ribbed surface provides superior **bond strength with concrete** (IS 456:2000 mandates its use in seismic zones). - The **percentage elongation** value tells the design engineer how much *visible warning* the structure will give before failure — a critical factor in life-safety design. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Numerical Problems ## 3.1 Derivation of the 0.2% Proof Stress (Offset Method) for Tor Steel Since Tor steel exhibits a **gradual yielding** behaviour with no distinct yield point, the yield strength is **defined by convention** rather than observation. **Step 1 — Plot the Engineering Stress–Strain Curve** Draw the σ vs ε curve from raw data: P (load) and ΔL (elongation) from the UTM dial gauge or extensometer. **Step 2 — Construct the Offset Line** From a point ε = 0.002 (i.e., **0.2% strain**) on the x-axis, draw a straight line **parallel to the initial linear (elastic) portion** of the stress–strain curve. **Step 3 — Locate the Intersection** The intersection of this offset line with the stress–strain curve defines the **0.2% Proof Stress (σ₀.₂)**. Mathematically, the offset line equation is: $$ \sigma_{offset}(\varepsilon) = E \cdot (\varepsilon - 0.002) $$ **Step 4 — Equate to True Curve at Intersection** At the intersection point ε_p : $$ E \cdot (\varepsilon_p - 0.002) = \sigma(\varepsilon_p) $$ Solving iteratively (since σ(ε) is non-linear) — in practice, **read directly off the graph**. > [!NOTE] > **Convention Background:** The 0.2% offset corresponds to a permanent plastic strain of 0.2% if the load were removed — this is the engineering community's universally agreed compromise for a "yield strength" value in materials lacking a sharp yield point. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Numerical Problem (KTU Board Pattern) **Problem Statement:** A mild steel specimen of original diameter **d₀ = 20 mm** and gauge length **L₀ = 200 mm** is tested in a UTM. The following load–elongation readings are recorded near the elastic and yield regions: | Load P (kN) | Elongation ΔL (mm) | |---|---| | 20 | 0.032 | | 40 | 0.065 | | 60 | 0.097 | | 80 (Yield) | 0.130 | | 120 (Ultimate) | — | | 102 (Fracture) | — | After fracture, the final gauge length **L_f = 280 mm** and final diameter at neck **d_f = 14 mm**. Determine: 1. Young's Modulus (E) 2. Yield Stress (σ_y) 3. Ultimate Tensile Stress (σ_u) 4. Breaking Stress (σ_b) 5. Percentage Elongation (%EL) 6. Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA) ### **Step 1 — Original Cross-Sectional Area** $$ A_0 = \frac{\pi d_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (20)^2}{4} = \frac{400 \pi}{4} = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2 $$ We retain A₀ = **314.159 mm²** throughout for engineering stress calculations. ### **Step 2 — Young's Modulus from the Linear Elastic Region** Use the first three readings (load increasing linearly with elongation). Pick the 3rd reading for maximum accuracy (still in elastic zone). At P = 60 kN, ΔL = 0.097 mm: $$ A_0 = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2, \quad L_0 = 200 \text{ mm} $$ $$ \sigma = \frac{P}{A_0} = \frac{60{,}000}{314.159} = 190.99 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ $$ \varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{0.097}{200} = 0.000485 $$ $$ E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{190.99}{0.000485} = 393{,}793 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 3.94 \times 10^2 \text{ kN/mm}^2 $$ **Expected ideal value:** **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 kN/mm²**. The discrepancy here is because the test data is illustrative (a real KTU exam problem will give data matching standard values). ### **Step 3 — Yield Stress (at P_y = 80 kN)** $$ \sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{80{,}000}{314.159} = 254.65 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Rounded:** σ_y ≈ **254.6 N/mm²** (consistent with Fe 410 grade mild steel, nominal 250 N/mm²). ### **Step 4 — Ultimate Tensile Stress (at P_max = 120 kN)** $$ \sigma_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{120{,}000}{314.159} = 381.97 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Rounded:** σ_u ≈ **382.0 N/mm²**. ### **Step 5 — Breaking Stress (at P_br = 102 kN)** $$ \sigma_b = \frac{P_{br}}{A_0} = \frac{102{,}000}{314.159} = 324.68 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Rounded:** σ_b ≈ **324.7 N/mm²**. ### **Step 6 — Percentage Elongation** $$ \%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{280 - 200}{200} \times 100 = \frac{80}{200} \times 100 = 40.0\% $$ ### **Step 7 — Percentage Reduction in Area** First compute the final area at the neck: $$ A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (14)^2}{4} = \frac{196 \pi}{4} = 153.938 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ \%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.159 - 153.938}{314.159} \times 100 = \frac{160.221}{314.159} \times 100 $$ $$ \%RA = 51.0\% $$ ### **Final Tabulated Result** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | A₀ | 314.16 mm² | | Young's Modulus (E) | 1.93 × 10⁵ N/mm² (≈ 200 GPa) | | Yield Stress σ_y | 254.6 N/mm² | | Ultimate Stress σ_u | 382.0 N/mm² | | Breaking Stress σ_b | 324.7 N/mm² | | % Elongation | 40.0% | | % Reduction in Area | 51.0% | > [!NOTE] > **Verification of Ductility:** %EL = 40% >> 5% (the IS threshold for ductile failure) → the specimen is **highly ductile** — a characteristic signature of mild steel. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Standard Tensile Specimen Geometry (IS 1608 / ASTM E8) A schematic block-diagram mapping the geometry of a standard proportional round tensile specimen (the Mermaid block below abstracts the geometry into labelled functional zones). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[End Threaded Grip Section
Length 30 mm
Diameter 24 mm] --> B[Reduced Grip Section
Length 15 mm
Tapering Transition] B --> C[Parallel Gauge Section
Original Length L0 200 mm
Original Diameter d0 20 mm
Used for Elongation Measurement] C --> D[Reduced Grip Section
Length 15 mm
Tapering Transition] D --> E[End Threaded Grip Section
Length 30 mm
Diameter 24 mm] ``` **Key Dimensions (for KTU 20 mm dia. standard specimen):** - **Total Length:** ≈ 330 mm - **Gauge Length L₀:** 200 mm (5.65√A₀ per IS 1608 proportional specimen) - **Original Diameter d₀:** 20.0 mm - **Original Area A₀:** 314.16 mm² ## 4.2 Universal Testing Machine (UTM) — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD L1[Three Phase Power Supply 415V AC] --> M1[Oil Hydraulic Pump
Constant Pressure Source] M1 --> C1[Loading Cylinder
Piston Movement Creates Axial Tension] C1 --> S1[Upper Cross Head
Stationary Grip Chuck] C1 --> S2[Lower Cross Head
Movable Grip Chuck] S1 --> SP[Specimen Mounted Vertically
Threaded into Both Chucks] S2 --> SP SP --> D1[Load Dial Gauge
Reads P in kN] SP --> D2[Extensometer
Reads Delta L in mm] D1 --> R[Recorder Plots P vs Delta L Curve] D2 --> R R --> O[Converted to Stress vs Strain
Sigma N per mm2 and Epsilon] ``` ## 4.3 Comparison of Stress–Strain Behaviour — Mild Steel vs Tor Steel ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph MS[MILD STEEL STRESS-STRAIN CURVE] MSO[Origin] --> MSA[Linear Elastic Region
Slope E 2.0e5 N per mm2] MSA --> MSB[Upper Yield Point B
Sigma yU around 270 N per mm2] MSB --> MSC[Lower Yield Plateau C
Almost Constant Stress
Luders Band Propagation] MSC --> MSD[Strain Hardening Region
Stress Rises to Sigma U] MSD --> MSE[Necking Begins
Engineering Stress Drops] MSE --> MSF[Fracture Point E
Cup and Cone Ductile Failure] end subgraph TS[TOR STEEL HYSD STRESS-STRAIN CURVE] TSO[Origin] --> TSA[Linear Elastic Region
Slope E 2.0e5 N per mm2] TSA --> TSB[0.2 Percent Proof Stress
Offset Line from Epsilon 0.002] TSB --> TSC[Smooth Strain Hardening
No Yield Plateau] TSC --> TSD[Ultimate Stress Sigma U] TSD --> TSE[Limited Necking
Smaller % Elongation] TSE --> TSF[Fracture Point
Oblique Shear Failure] end ``` ## 4.4 Experimental Procedure Flowchart (For Lab Record / Viva) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Prepare Specimen
Measure d0 and L0 with Vernier
Mark Gauge Length with Centre Punch] --> B[Step 2: Mount in UTM
Grip Both Ends in Chucks
Align Axially to Avoid Bending] B --> C[Step 3: Attach Extensometer
Set Gauge to Zero
Start Hydraulic Pump] C --> D[Step 4: Apply Load Incrementally
Record P and Delta L in Tabular Form] D --> E[Step 5: Identify Yield Point
For MS observe Drop in Pointer
For TS use 0.2% Offset] E --> F[Step 6: Continue Loading till Fracture
Record Pmax and Pbr] F --> G[Step 7: Remove Broken Pieces
Rejoin Carefully and Measure Lf
Measure Final Neck Diameter df] G --> H[Step 8: Calculate E Sigma y Sigma U
Percent Elongation Percent RA
Plot Stress vs Strain Graph] H --> I[Step 9: Compare MS and TS Curves
Discuss Differences in Viva] ``` ## 4.5 Failure Mode Comparison — Cup-and-Cone vs Oblique Fracture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph F1[Cup and Cone Fracture of Mild Steel] F1A[Outer Surface Tension Driven
Forms Conical Lip Cup] --> F1B[Central Region Triaxial Stress
Forms Flat Conical Cone] F1A --> F1C[Highly Ductile
Large Plastic Zone] end subgraph F2[Oblique Shear Fracture of Tor Steel] F2A[Failure Plane at 45 degrees
to Specimen Axis] --> F2B[Maximum Shear Stress Plane
Governs Brittle like Failure] F2A --> F2C[Moderate Ductility
Less Plastic Zone] end ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **"Differentiate clearly between Mild Steel and Tor Steel with respect to their manufacturing process, yield behaviour, and typical applications in civil engineering."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Mild Steel (Fe 410) | Tor Steel (HYSD — Fe 415/500) | |---|---|---| | **Manufacturing** | Hot-rolled, no subsequent plastic deformation | Cold-twisted and deformed (CTD) or thermo-mechanically treated (TMT); ribs on surface | | **Yield Behaviour** | Distinct upper and lower yield points on the σ–ε curve (due to Lüders band formation) | No distinct yield point — gradual yielding; 0.2% proof stress is used | | **Yield Strength** | ~ 250 N/mm² | 415 / 500 / 550 N/mm² | | **Ductility** | Higher (20–30% elongation) | Lower (10–20% elongation) | | **Application** | General structural, rivets, bolts, low-stress welds | Primary reinforcement in RCC beams, columns, slabs (IS 456:2000) | --- ### **Question 2:** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **"What is meant by the 0.2% proof stress, and why is it necessary for Tor Steel but not for Mild Steel?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The 0.2% proof stress is the **stress value obtained by drawing a line parallel to the initial linear elastic portion of the stress–strain curve, offset by a strain of 0.002 (0.2%)**, and noting its intersection with the σ–ε curve. It represents the stress at which the material undergoes a **permanent plastic strain of 0.2%**. It is *necessary* for Tor Steel because the cold-twisting / TMT manufacturing process **eliminates the sharp yield point** (no Lüders band formation), making the "yield" value impossible to read directly. For Mild Steel, the **distinct yield plateau** makes the yield point directly observable on the curve, so no offset construction is required. [1 mark for definition + 1 mark for construction method + 1 mark for justification of why used for Tor Steel] --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **complete procedure** for conducting a uniaxial tension test on a mild steel rod specimen in a Universal Testing Machine. List the observations and calculations to be made. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** In a tension test on a mild steel specimen of diameter **18 mm** and gauge length **60 mm**, the following observations were recorded: - Load at yield point: **54 kN** - Maximum load: **82 kN** - Load at fracture: **66 kN** - Final gauge length after fracture: **76 mm** - Final diameter at neck: **12 mm** Calculate: **(i)** Yield stress, **(ii)** Ultimate tensile stress, **(iii)** Percentage elongation, **(iv)** Percentage reduction in area. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks** 1. **Specimen preparation (1 mark):** As per IS 1608, prepare a proportional round specimen. Measure original diameter d₀ to 0.01 mm accuracy using a Vernier at three points along the gauge length and take the mean. Mark a gauge length L₀ (≈ 5.65√A₀) with light centre-punch marks. 2. **UTM setup (1 mark):** Mount the specimen vertically in the upper and lower grip chucks. Ensure **perfect axial alignment** to prevent bending stresses. Attach an extensometer to the gauge length. 3. **Testing (2 marks):** Start the hydraulic pump, open the loading valve slowly, and apply **gradually increasing tensile load**. Record load (P) at regular intervals, noting the elongation (ΔL) from the extensometer. Note the **load at which the dial pointer first drops or becomes stationary** (yield point). Continue loading until fracture occurs. Record P_max and P_break. 4. **Post-test (1 mark):** Carefully remove the broken halves, rejoin them along the axis, and measure the **final gauge length L_f** and **final neck diameter d_f** (cup-and-cone measurement). 5. **Plotting & Calculations (2 marks):** Plot σ (= P/A₀) vs ε (= ΔL/L₀). Calculate E, σ_y, σ_u, σ_b, %EL, %RA. Identify type of fracture. #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks** **Given:** d₀ = 18 mm, L₀ = 60 mm, P_y = 54 kN, P_max = 82 kN, P_br = 66 kN, L_f = 76 mm, d_f = 12 mm. **Step 1 — Original Area:** $$ A_0 = \frac{\pi (18)^2}{4} = \frac{324 \pi}{4} = 254.469 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Yield Stress (1.75 Marks):** $$ \sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{54{,}000}{254.469} = 212.21 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Ultimate Tensile Stress (1.75 Marks):** $$ \sigma_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{82{,}000}{254.469} = 322.25 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Percentage Elongation (1.75 Marks):** $$ \%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{76 - 60}{60} \times 100 = \frac{16}{60} \times 100 = 26.67\% $$ **Step 5 — Percentage Reduction in Area (1.75 Marks):** $$ A_f = \frac{\pi (12)^2}{4} = \frac{144 \pi}{4} = 113.097 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ \%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{254.469 - 113.097}{254.469} \times 100 = \frac{141.372}{254.469} \times 100 = 55.55\% $$ #### **Final Answer for (b)** | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Yield Stress σ_y | **212.21 N/mm²** | | Ultimate Tensile Stress σ_u | **322.25 N/mm²** | | % Elongation | **26.67%** | | % Reduction in Area | **55.55%** | --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Draw a **neat labelled stress–strain diagram for Mild Steel**, marking all significant points (Proportional Limit, Elastic Limit, Upper Yield Point, Lower Yield Point, Ultimate Stress, Fracture Point). Explain the significance of each region. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **0.2% proof stress method** with a stress–strain diagram. Why is it used for Tor Steel? A Tor Steel bar of 16 mm diameter has a load at 0.2% proof stress of **70 kN**. Calculate the **0.2% proof stress value in N/mm²**. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks** The σ–ε curve for Mild Steel shows these critical points (1 mark for correct sketch; 1 mark for correct labels): 1. **Origin O** — Unstressed state. 2. **Proportional Limit A** — End of linear σ–ε relation; Hooke's Law strictly valid. 3. **Elastic Limit** — Maximum stress up to which strain is fully recoverable. 4. **Upper Yield Point B** — Stress at which dislocation unlocking begins (sudden drop). 5. **Lower Yield Point C** — Constant stress during Lüders band propagation across the gauge length. 6. **Ultimate Stress Point D (σ_u)** — Maximum load carrying capacity; onset of necking. 7. **Fracture Point E (σ_f)** — Final rupture; cup-and-cone formation. **Significance (4 marks — 1 mark each region):** - **Region OA:** Atomic bond stretching; fully recoverable. - **Region AB to C:** Plastic deformation begins; stress remains nearly constant; provides **visible warning** before failure. - **Region CD:** Strain hardening; dislocations multiply, requiring more stress to continue deformation. - **Region DE:** Necking; cross-section reduces locally, true stress rises but engineering stress appears to fall; final fracture. #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks** **0.2% Proof Stress Method (3 Marks):** The 0.2% proof stress is the **stress at which a permanent plastic strain of 0.2%** would remain after unloading. It is determined graphically: 1. Plot the σ–ε curve. 2. Draw a line from ε = 0.002 on the x-axis, **parallel to the initial linear elastic portion** (slope = E). 3. The intersection of this line with the σ–ε curve gives σ_0.2. **Why used for Tor Steel (2 Marks):** The cold-twisting / TMT process pre-strains the bar and **eliminates the sharp yield point**, producing a smooth σ–ε curve with no yield plateau. The proof stress method is a **standard engineering convention** to assign a yield value to such materials (per IS 1786). **Calculation (2 Marks):** Given d = 16 mm, P_0.2 = 70 kN. $$ A_0 = \frac{\pi (16)^2}{4} = \frac{256 \pi}{4} = 201.062 \text{ mm}^2 $$ $$ \sigma_{0.2} = \frac{P_{0.2}}{A_0} = \frac{70{,}000}{201.062} = 348.15 \text{ N/mm}^2 $$ **Answer:** σ_0.2 = **348.15 N/mm²** (≈ 348 MPa). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to divide by A₀** — Writing σ = P without computing original area first. **Mandatory: A₀ = πd₀²/4 always.** > 2. **Unit mismatch in load** — Load from the dial is in **kN**, but stress is in **N/mm²**. Forgetting to multiply by 1000 is the #1 calculation error. > 3. **Confusing %EL with %RA** — %EL uses **gauge length change**; %RA uses **area change at the neck**. They are not interchangeable. > 4. **Missing the 0.2% offset explanation** — In Tor Steel questions, simply stating "0.2% proof stress" without drawing the offset line costs 1–2 marks. > 5. **Forgetting to label axes / units** on the stress–strain graph — examiners explicitly allocate 1 mark for neatness and correct axis labels. > 6. **Writing A_f for the whole broken specimen** — A_f is the area **at the narrowest point of the neck**, not the area of a re-joined half. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ☐ The **Uniaxial Tension Test** on a UTM gives the **engineering stress–strain curve** for the material. - ☐ For **Mild Steel (Fe 410)**: σ_y ≈ **250 N/mm²**; sharp yield point; %EL ≈ **20–30%**; cup-and-cone fracture. - ☐ For **Tor Steel (HYSD Fe 415/500)**: σ_0.2 used in place of σ_y; smooth curve; %EL ≈ **10–20%**; oblique fracture. - ☐ **E for all steels ≈ 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa** (a board-exam constant). - ☐ Always convert: **load in kN → multiply by 1000** to get Newtons; stress in **N/mm² = MPa**. - ☐ The 4 key stresses to report: **σ_y (or σ_0.2), σ_u, σ_b, and E**. - ☐ The 2 ductility measures: **%EL** (gauge length) and **%RA** (cross-sectional area). - ☐ Governing codes: **IS 432** (Mild Steel), **IS 1786** (HYSD/Tor Steel), **IS 1608** (tensile testing), **IS 456:2000** (RCC design). - ☐ Higher **%EL** = more ductile = more **visible warning** before failure (life-safety advantage in seismic design). - ☐ Higher **σ_y** = stronger = less material required (economy advantage in RCC reinforcement). - ☐ In **Tor Steel**, the 0.2% proof stress is *not* a real physical event — it is a **convention** for design. - ☐ Necking is the localised cross-section reduction; true stress at fracture is *higher* than engineering stress σ_b. - ☐ The UTM dial indicator **sudden drop / pause** indicates the yield point for Mild Steel; for Tor Steel, the dial moves smoothly throughout.

topics.title: Shear test on mild steel rod (Compression Testing Machine and Shear Shackle)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL407 - PCCEL407
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Shear test on mild steel rod (Compression Testing Machine and Shear Shackle)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Shear Test on a Mild Steel Rod** is a standardized destructive laboratory experiment conducted to determine the **average ultimate shear strength** of a given cylindrical mild steel specimen. The test is performed by mounting the rod in a calibrated **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** — operating in compression mode — fitted with a specialized fixture called a **Shear Shackle (Guillotine-type shear attachment)**. The shackle applies two opposing, parallel, and nearly co-planar transverse forces on the specimen, causing it to fail along a defined shear plane.

The average shear strength ($\tau_u$) is mathematically defined as the ratio of the maximum shearing load ($P_{max}$) resisted by the specimen to its original cross-sectional area ($A_0$):

$$\tau_u = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCCEL407):** This experiment directly addresses **Course Outcome CO2 / CO3** of the lab — *"Determine mechanical properties of structural materials experimentally and interpret failure behavior."* The test bridges the theoretical concept of shear stress (from Strength of Materials) with the practical strength design of rivets, bolts, pins, and welded connections in steel structures.

## 1.2 Classification of Shear Failure Modes

Depending on the number of shear planes created, the test is classified as:

| Type | Number of Shear Planes | Effective Shear Area | Common Application |
|:----:|:----------------------:|:--------------------:|:-------------------|
| **Single Shear** | One | $A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$ | Lap riveted joints, single lap bolts |
| **Double Shear** | Two | $A_0 = 2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$ | Double-cover plate riveted joints, pin connections |

> [!NOTE]
> For this KTU experiment, the mild steel rod is typically tested in **single shear** using a standard shear shackle that cuts the rod at one cross-section.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are trying to **cut a piece of thick wire using a pair of scissors**. The two blades of the scissor create two opposing forces that try to "slide" one part of the wire over the other. The wire does not stretch (no axial tension) and does not crush (no compression) — it **slips** along a tiny cross-sectional plane.

This slipping failure is exactly what **shear failure** looks like. The mild steel rod in the shear shackle behaves the same way: the upper anvil pushes down while the lower stationary block resists, and the rod shears off cleanly across one section, much like a metal punch cutting a slot in a steel plate or the action of a **paper guillotine / paper cutter**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real Engineering Utility:** Shear strength values obtained from this test are used to design:
> - **Rivets** in old truss bridges
> - **Bolts** in beam-column connections
> - **Pins** in chain links, crane hooks, and clevises
> - **Welds** (the throat area resists shear)
> - **Cotter joints** and **knuckle joints** in mechanical assemblies

## 1.4 Material Profile: Mild Steel (MS)

**Mild Steel** is a low-carbon steel (Carbon content **0.15% – 0.25%**) conforming to **IS 432** (for plain bars used in general engineering) and **IS 2062** (for structural steel). Key properties relevant to this test:

- **Density:** **7850 kg/m³**
- **Young's Modulus (E):** **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm² (MPa)**
- **Poisson's Ratio (ν):** **0.30**
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength:** **410 N/mm² – 560 N/mm²**
- **Yield Strength:** **250 N/mm² (minimum, for Grade A)**
- **Elongation at failure:** **23% minimum (in 200 mm gauge length)**
- **Shear Modulus (G):** **~ 7.7 × 10⁴ N/mm²**

> [!WARNING]
> Mild steel is a **ductile** material. Under shear, it does not snap instantly; the rod deforms visibly before final separation, producing a characteristic **cup-and-cone / fibrous shear lip** at the fractured surface. Students often confuse this ductile shear failure with brittle fracture — observe carefully and record observations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Stress State in Pure Shear

When a body is subjected to two equal, opposite, and parallel forces ($P$) separated by a small distance, the internal resisting force per unit area on the section between the two forces is called **shear stress ($\tau$)**. Pure shear produces a state of stress in which the principal stresses act at **45°** to the shear plane.

The complementary shear stresses on orthogonal planes are always **equal in magnitude** — a principle established by the **Theorem of Complementary Shear Stresses** (from Strength of Materials / Solid Mechanics). This is the same reason a **bolt in single shear** is designed assuming the shear acts on a single cross-section.

## 2.2 The Mechanics of the Shear Shackle

The **Shear Shackle** (also called a **Guillotine Shear Attachment** or **Shear Tool Fixture**) consists of:

1. **Lower Fixed Block (Base Anvil):** A hardened steel block with a semicircular groove of known radius to support the rod.
2. **Upper Movable Blade (Cutting Punch):** A hardened tool-steel blade that descends vertically when the UTM crosshead moves down.
3. **Locating Pin / Collar:** Centers the rod precisely at the shear plane.
4. **Backing Plate:** Prevents the cut fragment from flying out and provides reaction support.

**Operational Logic — Step by Step:**

- The cylindrical MS rod is placed horizontally across the lower anvil groove.
- The upper blade is positioned so that its sharp cutting edge aligns exactly with the **mid-section** of the rod.
- The UTM is operated in **compression mode** — the moving crosshead pushes the upper blade downward.
- The reaction is taken by the rigid base of the UTM.
- The rod fails when the induced shear stress $\tau$ exceeds the **ultimate shear strength** of mild steel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Compression Testing Machine?** The KTU lab (PCCEL407) typically uses a 1000 kN or 2000 kN UTM. These machines are designed primarily for compression testing of concrete cubes/cylinders, but with a shear shackle fixture they can be adapted to perform tensile, bending, and shear tests. This multi-functionality is why it is called a *Universal* Testing Machine.

## 2.3 The Governing Equations

For a cylindrical mild steel rod of nominal diameter $d$ subjected to single shear failure load $P_u$:

$$\boxed{\tau_{u,\text{avg}} = \frac{P_u}{\frac{\pi}{4}d^2}}$$

For a rod in **double shear** (when tested in a double-shear shackle):

$$\boxed{\tau_{u,\text{avg}} = \frac{P_u}{2 \times \frac{\pi}{4}d^2} = \frac{2 P_u}{\pi d^2}}$$

The **shear strain** experienced by the rod just before failure is:

$$\gamma = \frac{\delta}{L_s}$$

where $\delta$ is the relative transverse displacement of the two halves and $L_s$ is the characteristic length over which shear is acting.

The **shear modulus of rigidity** is obtained from the linear elastic portion of the load–deformation curve:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

For isotropic materials, $G$ is related to Young's Modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ by:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$$

For mild steel: $G = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2(1+0.30)} \approx 7.69 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Revision Table

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Symbol | Unit |
|:-----:|:----------|:--------|:------:|:----:|
| 1 | Cross-sectional area (circular rod) | $A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$ | $d$ = diameter | mm² |
| 2 | Average shear stress (single shear) | $\tau = \frac{P}{A_0}$ | $P$ = applied load | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 3 | Average shear stress (double shear) | $\tau = \frac{P}{2A_0}$ | — | MPa |
| 4 | Shear strain | $\gamma = \frac{\delta}{L_s}$ | $\delta$ = slip | dimensionless (radians) |
| 5 | Shear Modulus | $G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$ | — | N/mm² or MPa |
| 6 | Relation of E, G, ν | $G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | — | MPa |
| 7 | Theoretical pure shear strength (Tresca) | $\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_u}{2}$ | $\sigma_u$ = UTS | MPa |
| 8 | Nominal diameter correction (IS 432) | $d_{\text{nom}} = \frac{\text{measured mass}}{\text{density} \times L}$ | $L$ = length | mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The **empirical ratio** for mild steel is $\frac{\tau_u}{\sigma_u} \approx 0.70$ to $0.80$ for rolled bars, and exactly **0.577** in pure shear according to the **Von Mises yielding criterion** for ductile metals. Memorize the Von Mises relation: $\tau_{y} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}$. This is a favorite 3-mark question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The shear test data is indispensable in:

- **Design of riveted/bolted joints** in steel trusses (bridges, roof structures, transmission towers).
- **Design of pin connections** in mechanical linkages (knuckle joints, cotter joints, bell-crank levers).
- **Failure analysis** of components that have failed in service (forensic engineering).
- **Quality control** of incoming mild steel stock in fabrication shops.
- **Calibration of FE (Finite Element) models** for shear-dominated connections.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Laboratory Procedure

### A. Specimen Preparation
1. Select a **mild steel rod** of nominal diameter 12 mm, 16 mm, or 20 mm (as per lab availability) and length approximately **80 mm – 100 mm**.
2. Measure the diameter at **three locations** (both ends and middle) using a **vernier caliper** (LC = 0.02 mm) or a **micrometer** (LC = 0.01 mm).
3. Record the average diameter $d$ and the cross-sectional area $A_0$.
4. Lightly file/deburr the ends so that the rod sits flat in the shackle.

### B. Machine and Fixture Setup
1. Switch ON the UTM. Ensure the load indicator is **zeroed**.
2. Select the appropriate **load range** on the UTM dial/display (e.g., 0 – 200 kN for a 16 mm MS rod).
3. Mount the **shear shackle** on the lower compression platen of the UTM. Tighten the holding bolts firmly.
4. Place the MS rod specimen centrally in the lower anvil groove of the shear shackle.
5. Align the upper movable blade so that its cutting edge is exactly **perpendicular** to the longitudinal axis of the rod and centered on the specimen's mid-length.

### C. Testing Operation
1. Set the loading rate on the UTM controller — typically **5 mm/min to 10 mm/min** of crosshead displacement (as per IS 1608 for mild steel).
2. Start the machine. The crosshead descends and applies load to the upper blade.
3. Observe the load dial/display continuously. The load will rise steadily, then may plateau (ductile yield), then drop suddenly at fracture.
4. **Record the maximum load ($P_u$)** at the instant of failure. If the UTM is fitted with a digital peak-hold indicator, note the value.
5. Also tabulate load values at regular intervals of deformation (every 0.5 mm of crosshead movement) to plot the **Load–Deformation curve**.

### D. Post-Test Observations
1. Carefully remove the fractured specimen.
2. Observe the failure surface: note whether it is **flat, fibrous, smooth, or jagged**.
3. Measure any **necking** or **lateral expansion** at the fracture plane.
4. Repeat the test for **two more specimens** of the same diameter to obtain a mean value.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Derivation of Average Shear Stress

We start from the fundamental definition of shear stress as a force per unit area on a shearing plane.

**Step 1 — Force Balance on the Shear Plane**

Consider a free-body diagram of the upper half of the rod after the shear plane has formed. The applied load $P$ acts downward through the upper blade. The reaction from the lower anvil supports the lower half. The internal shear force at the cross-section equals the applied load by equilibrium:

$$V = P$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Average Shear Stress**

By definition, average shear stress is the resultant shear force distributed uniformly over the cross-sectional area:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{A_0}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the Cross-Sectional Area of a Circular Rod**

For a circular cross-section of diameter $d$:

$$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$$

**Step 4 — Combining Steps 2 and 3**

Substituting $V = P$ and $A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi}{4}d^2} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

Therefore the **final working formula** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_u &= \frac{4P_u}{\pi d^2}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Notation Reminder:** The subscript $u$ stands for *ultimate* (failure) value, not *unconfined*. This convention follows IS 800:2007 and IS 1608:2005.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard 14-Mark Problem)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A mild steel rod of nominal diameter **20 mm** is tested in single shear using a shear shackle on a 1000 kN UTM. The maximum load recorded at failure is **142.5 kN**. Calculate:
> (a) The average ultimate shear strength of the rod.
> (b) The theoretical shear yield strength using the Von Mises criterion, given $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm².
> (c) The shear modulus if the measured slip at 50% of ultimate load is 0.06 mm over a characteristic length of 25 mm.
> (d) Comment on the failure mode.

### Part (a) — Average Ultimate Shear Strength

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d = 20$ mm
- Ultimate load $P_u = 142.5$ kN $= 142{,}500$ N
- Single shear configuration

**Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4} \times (20)^2 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4} \times 400 \\
&= 100\pi \\
&= 314.159 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_u &= \frac{P_u}{A_0} \\
&= \frac{142{,}500}{314.159} \\
&= 453.6 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 453.6 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\tau_u \approx 453.6$ MPa

### Part (b) — Theoretical Shear Yield Strength (Von Mises)

The **Von Mises yield criterion** for pure shear states:

$$\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}$$

Substituting $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_y &= \frac{250}{\sqrt{3}} \\
&= \frac{250}{1.7321} \\
&= 144.34 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $\tau_y \approx 144.34$ MPa

> **Comparison Insight:** The experimental $\tau_u = 453.6$ MPa is more than **3 times** the theoretical yield $\tau_y = 144.3$ MPa. This is expected because $\tau_y$ marks the *onset of yielding*, while $\tau_u$ corresponds to *final fracture*. Mild steel can sustain significant post-yield strain hardening.

### Part (c) — Shear Modulus (G)

**Given Data:**
- Load at 50% of ultimate: $P_{0.5} = 0.5 \times 142{,}500 = 71{,}250$ N
- Slip $\delta = 0.06$ mm
- Characteristic length $L_s = 25$ mm
- Area $A_0 = 314.159$ mm² (from Part a)

**Step 1 — Shear stress at 50% load:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{0.5} &= \frac{P_{0.5}}{A_0} \\
&= \frac{71{,}250}{314.159} \\
&= 226.8 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Shear strain:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\gamma &= \frac{\delta}{L_s} \\
&= \frac{0.06}{25} \\
&= 0.0024 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Shear Modulus:**

$$\begin{aligned}
G &= \frac{\tau_{0.5}}{\gamma} \\
&= \frac{226.8}{0.0024} \\
&= 94{,}500 \text{ MPa} \\
&= 94.5 \text{ GPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Result:** $G \approx 94.5$ GPa

> **Verification using isotropic relation:** $G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{200{,}000}{2(1+0.30)} = 76{,}923$ MPa ≈ **77 GPa**
> The measured $G$ of 94.5 GPa is higher than the theoretical 77 GPa because at 50% of ultimate load, the rod is already past the linear-elastic region and is undergoing **strain hardening**, so the slope of the secant is steeper. This is a beautiful example of how mild steel transitions from elastic to plastic behavior.

### Part (d) — Failure Mode Comment

The rod in mild steel under single shear fails in a **ductile manner** with the following characteristic features:

- A **rough, fibrous, dull grey** fracture surface indicating **microvoid coalescence**.
- Visible **lateral expansion (bulging)** at the shear plane due to Poisson's effect.
- A **45° inclined shear lip** is often observed, aligning with the direction of maximum principal tensile stress.
- No brittle cleavage facets are seen (which would indicate high-carbon or hardened steel).

## 3.4 Standard Data Recording Table (For Practical Record)

| S.No. | Specimen ID | Measured Diameter, $d$ (mm) | Cross-sectional Area, $A_0$ (mm²) | Ultimate Load, $P_u$ (kN) | Avg. Shear Strength, $\tau_u$ (MPa) |
|:-----:|:-----------:|:--------------------------:|:--------------------------------:|:-------------------------:|:------------------------------------:|
| 1 | MS-12-A | 11.98 | 112.71 | 51.20 | 454.20 |
| 2 | MS-12-B | 12.02 | 113.49 | 50.85 | 448.05 |
| 3 | MS-12-C | 12.00 | 113.10 | 51.00 | 450.93 |
| **Mean** | — | **12.00** | **113.10** | **51.02** | **451.06** |

## 3.5 Comparative Behavior: Mild Steel vs. Other Materials in Shear

| Material | Typical $\tau_u$ (MPa) | Failure Mode | Ductility |
|:---------|:----------------------:|:------------:|:---------:|
| **Mild Steel (this test)** | 300 – 450 | Ductile, fibrous | High |
| High-Carbon Steel | 470 – 700 | Semi-brittle | Medium |
| Cast Iron | 120 – 200 | Brittle, granular | Very Low |
| Aluminum Alloy | 100 – 250 | Ductile | High |
| Brass | 200 – 350 | Ductile | Medium-High |
| Concrete (plain) | 2 – 5 | Brittle | Negligible |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Shear Test Workflow

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete **functional architecture** of the test, mapping the interaction between the specimen, machine, fixture, and data acquisition system — all of which a KTU student must be able to draw or explain in the exam.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Test Procedure] --> B[Inspect MS Rod Specimen]
    B --> C[Measure Diameter d at 3 Locations using Vernier Caliper]
    C --> D[Compute Average Area A0 = pi x d square / 4]
    D --> E[Power ON Universal Testing Machine UTM]
    E --> F[Select Load Range 0 to 200 kN]
    F --> G[Mount Shear Shackle on Lower Compression Platen]
    G --> H[Place MS Rod Centrally in Lower Anvil Groove]
    H --> I[Align Upper Movable Blade at Mid Length of Rod]
    I --> J[Set Crosshead Speed 5 mm per min]
    J --> K[Apply Compression Load via Crosshead]
    K --> L{Monitor Load Dial Continuously}
    L --> M[Record Load and Deformation at Regular Intervals]
    M --> N{Load reaches Peak Pmax?}
    N -- No --> L
    N -- Yes --> O[Note Maximum Load Pu]
    O --> P[Stop UTM and Reset Crosshead]
    P --> Q[Remove Fractured Specimen Carefully]
    Q --> R[Inspect Fracture Surface for Ductile Features]
    R --> S[Compute Average Shear Strength tau u = Pu / A0]
    S --> T[Plot Load versus Deformation Curve]
    T --> U[Repeat for 2 More Specimens and Tabulate Mean]
    U --> V[End of Test Record Mean tau u in MPa]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Shear Shackle in Operation

The following block-level schematic illustrates the **force flow and component arrangement** during the test. Use this diagram when answering *"Draw a neat sketch of the shear shackle"* type questions in the KTU exam.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph UTM [Universal Testing Machine UTM in Compression Mode]
        A1[Upper Movable Crosshead] -->|Applies Load P downward| A2[Load Cell with Dial Indicator]
    end

    subgraph SHACKLE [Shear Shackle Fixture]
        B1[Upper Hardened Cutting Blade] -->|Transmits P| B2[MS Rod Specimen Horizontal]
        B2 -->|Resists| B3[Lower Fixed Anvil with Semi Circular Groove]
        B3 -->|Reaction R equals P| B4[Base Plate Bolted to UTM Platen]
    end

    A2 --> B1
    B4 --> A3[Lower Compression Platen of UTM]

    style UT M fill:#e1f0ff,stroke:#003366
    style SHACKLE fill:#fff4e1,stroke:#cc6600
```

## 4.3 Free-Body Diagram of the Shear Plane

This diagram represents the **internal stress state** at the critical cross-section, which is a frequent 7-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Applied Load P acting downward on Upper Half] --> B[Internal Shear Force V equals P on Shear Plane]
    B --> C[Shear Stress tau equals V divided by A0]
    C --> D[Principal Stresses act at 45 degrees to Shear Plane]
    D --> E[Maximum Principal Tensile Stress sigma 1 equals tau]
    D --> F[Maximum Principal Compressive Stress sigma 2 equals minus tau]
    E --> G[Fracture initiates along 45 degree Plane]
    F --> G
```

## 4.4 Typical Load–Deformation Behavior of Mild Steel in Shear

| Region | Load–Deformation Behavior | Physical Interpretation |
|:------:|:-------------------------|:------------------------|
| **OA** | Linear (steep slope) | Elastic region; reversible; slope ≈ $G \cdot A_0 / L_s$ |
| **AB** | Non-linear (curve bends) | Plastic yielding begins; microvoids nucleate |
| **BC** | Plateau (load ≈ constant) | Perfectly plastic flow; shear lips form |
| **CD** | Strain hardening (slight rise) | Work hardening; void growth |
| **DE** | Sudden drop | Final fracture; crack propagates at ~45° to axis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain the Load–Deformation curve," explicitly label **O, A, B, C, D, E** points and identify the **proportional limit**, **yield point**, **ultimate point**, and **fracture point** — just like in the tensile test.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Define "average ultimate shear strength" of a material. How is it different from the "theoretical shear yield strength" according to the Von Mises criterion?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Average ultimate shear strength** ($\tau_u$) is defined as the maximum shear load sustained by a specimen divided by its original cross-sectional area: $\tau_u = P_u / A_0$. **(1 mark)**
- It is a **practical/experimental** value obtained from destructive testing, representing the *fracture* resistance of the material. **(1 mark)**
- The **theoretical shear yield strength** ($\tau_y$) is calculated using the **Von Mises yield criterion** as $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$, representing the *onset of yielding* under pure shear. **(1 mark)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a shear shackle? Why is a UTM operated in "compression mode" used for performing a shear test on a mild steel rod?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **shear shackle** is a hardened tool-steel fixture consisting of a fixed lower anvil (with a semicircular groove) and a movable upper cutting blade; it is mounted on the UTM to convert a compressive load into a shearing action on the rod. **(1.5 marks)**
- A UTM is operated in **compression mode** because the load is applied by pushing the upper crosshead *downward* against the rigid lower platen, with the shear shackle transferring this compressive force into two opposing transverse shearing forces on the rod. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Describe with a neat sketch the procedure to determine the average ultimate shear strength of a 16 mm diameter mild steel rod using a 1000 kN Universal Testing Machine fitted with a shear shackle. Mention the relevant IS code. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In such a test, the maximum load recorded is **118.6 kN** for a rod of measured diameter **16.04 mm**. Calculate the average ultimate shear strength. If the same rod were tested in double shear in a suitable shackle, what load would cause the same average shear stress? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Procedure and Sketch **(7 marks)**

**1. Specimen Preparation (2 marks):**
- Cut a mild steel rod of nominal diameter 16 mm to a length of 80–100 mm.
- Measure the diameter at three locations (both ends and middle) using a vernier caliper (LC = 0.02 mm).
- Calculate the average cross-sectional area $A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}d^2$.

**2. Machine and Fixture Setup (2 marks):**
- Power ON the 1000 kN UTM, select the appropriate load range (0–200 kN), and zero the dial indicator.
- Mount the shear shackle firmly on the lower compression platen.
- Place the MS rod specimen horizontally in the lower anvil's semicircular groove, ensuring it is centered.
- Align the upper hardened blade so that its cutting edge is exactly perpendicular to the rod axis at its mid-length.

**3. Testing (2 marks):**
- Set the crosshead speed to 5 mm/min (per IS 1608).
- Apply the compressive load gradually; the upper blade descends and shears the rod.
- Record the load and crosshead deformation at regular intervals.
- Note the **maximum (peak) load** $P_u$ at the instant of failure.
- Stop the machine, reset, and remove the fractured specimen. Inspect the fracture surface.

**4. Relevant IS Code (1 mark):**
- **IS 1608:2005** — Metallic materials — Tensile testing at ambient temperature (also applicable to shear specimen preparation).
- **IS 432 (Part 1):1982** — Specification for mild steel and medium tensile steel bars.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution **(7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Ultimate load in single shear, $P_{u,1} = 118.6$ kN $= 118{,}600$ N
- Measured diameter $d = 16.04$ mm
- Mode: Single shear in part (a); double shear in part (b)

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4}d^2 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4}(16.04)^2 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4}(257.2816) \\
&= 202.07 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Average ultimate shear strength (single shear):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_u &= \frac{P_{u,1}}{A_0} \\
&= \frac{118{,}600}{202.07} \\
&= 586.96 \text{ N/mm}^2 \\
&\approx 587.0 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Load required in double shear for the same average stress:**

In double shear, the effective shear area is $2A_0$. For the same average stress $\tau_u$:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{u,2} &= \tau_u \times 2A_0 \\
&= 2 \times P_{u,1} \\
&= 2 \times 118.6 \text{ kN} \\
&= 237.2 \text{ kN}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final Answers:**
- Single-shear average ultimate shear strength: $\tau_u = 587.0$ MPa
- Double-shear equivalent load: $P_{u,2} = 237.2$ kN

---

### Question B (Internal Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Module 2]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the theoretical basis of the **Von Mises yield criterion** in pure shear. Show that for a ductile material like mild steel, $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mild steel rod of 18 mm diameter fails in single shear at a load of 145.2 kN. Calculate the average ultimate shear strength and compare it with the theoretical yield strength in shear, given $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa and $\sigma_u = 410$ MPa. Compute the **shear strength ratio** $\tau_u / \sigma_u$ and comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Von Mises Yield Criterion Derivation **(7 marks)**

**1. Statement (1 mark):**
The **Von Mises yield criterion** states that a ductile material begins to yield when the **second deviatoric stress invariant** $J_2$ reaches a critical value:

$$J_2 = k^2$$

where $k = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ is the **shear yield stress** in pure shear.

**2. Principal Stresses in Pure Shear (2 marks):**
For an element subjected to pure shear $\tau$ on its faces, the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

(These act on planes oriented at 45° to the shear plane.)

**3. Von Mises Equation (2 marks):**
The Von Mises equivalent stress in terms of principal stresses is:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2\right]}$$

**4. Substituting the Principal Stresses (1 mark):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{VM} &= \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(\tau - (-\tau))^2 + ((-\tau) - 0)^2 + (0 - \tau)^2\right]} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[(2\tau)^2 + (-\tau)^2 + (-\tau)^2\right]} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}\left[4\tau^2 + \tau^2 + \tau^2\right]} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}(6\tau^2)} \\
&= \sqrt{3\tau^2} \\
&= \tau\sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**5. Yielding Condition (1 mark):**
At the onset of yielding, $\sigma_{VM} = \sigma_y$:

$$\tau\sqrt{3} = \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}}$$

This completes the derivation.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation **(7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- Diameter $d = 18$ mm
- Single-shear failure load $P_u = 145.2$ kN $= 145{,}200$ N
- Yield strength $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa
- Ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_u = 410$ MPa

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \frac{\pi}{4}(18)^2 \\
&= \frac{\pi}{4}(324) \\
&= 254.47 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Average ultimate shear strength:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_u &= \frac{P_u}{A_0} \\
&= \frac{145{,}200}{254.47} \\
&= 570.6 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical shear yield strength (Von Mises):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_y &= \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \\
&= \frac{250}{1.7321} \\
&= 144.3 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Shear strength ratio:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\tau_u}{\sigma_u} &= \frac{570.6}{410} \\
&= 1.392
\end{aligned}$$

> **Comment on the result:**
> The experimentally observed $\tau_u$ is **larger** than the tensile UTS ($\sigma_u = 410$ MPa), giving a ratio of **1.39**. This is unusual — in conventional shear tests on round bars, the measured $\tau_u$ is typically **0.6 – 0.8 times** $\sigma_u$. The anomaly here is because:
> 1. The shear shackle induces **combined shear + bending** at the cut, so the measured $P_u$ is not pure shear.
> 2. Mild steel rod is a **rolled product** with directional grain; its shear strength along the rolling direction exceeds the true shear strength.
> 3. **Strain hardening** between yield and fracture raises the apparent shear strength.
> 4. The 18 mm rod may have higher actual carbon content than typical mild steel.
>
> In a properly designed pure-shear test (e.g., on a thin tubular specimen under torsion), the ratio $\tau_u / \sigma_u$ would be close to **0.70**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing single and double shear:** In double shear, the area is $2A_0$, not $A_0/2$. Many students divide by 2 incorrectly. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**
> 2. **Forgetting to convert kN to N:** Load in kN must be multiplied by 1000 before dividing by area in mm². **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 3. **Using radius instead of diameter:** $A_0 = \pi d^2 / 4$, not $\pi r^2$ where $r$ is incorrectly taken as $d$. **(Loses 1 mark)**
> 4. **Mixing up Von Mises and Tresca:** Von Mises gives $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$; Tresca gives $\tau_y = \sigma_y / 2$. Examiners specifically test this distinction. **(Loses 2 marks)**
> 5. **Not labeling the sketch:** The shear shackle sketch *must* show the lower anvil, upper blade, and the load direction arrows. Unlabeled sketches get partial credit only. **(Loses 1–2 marks)**
> 6. **Omitting the IS code reference:** Always mention **IS 1608:2005** and/or **IS 432** in procedure questions. **(Loses 0.5–1 mark)**
> 7. **Neglecting ductile failure observations:** Examiner expects mention of fibrous surface, shear lip, 45° fracture plane, and absence of brittle cleavage. **(Loses 1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2 Experiment**

- ✅ **Primary formula (single shear):** $\tau_u = \dfrac{4 P_u}{\pi d^2}$
- ✅ **Primary formula (double shear):** $\tau_u = \dfrac{2 P_u}{\pi d^2}$
- ✅ **Von Mises shear yield:** $\tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,\sigma_y$
- ✅ **Tresca shear yield:** $\tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2} = 0.50\,\sigma_y$
- ✅ **Relation between moduli:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$
- ✅ **Typical mild steel $\tau_u$ range:** **300 – 450 MPa** (rolled rods)
- ✅ **Typical mild steel $\tau_y$ range:** **140 – 160 MPa**
- ✅ **IS Code references:** IS 1608:2005 (testing), IS 432 (MS bars), IS 2062 (structural steel)
- ✅ **Loading rate (IS 1608):** Crosshead speed ≈ 5–10 mm/min for ductile metals
- ✅ **Failure mode of mild steel in shear:** Ductile, fibrous, 45° shear lip, microvoid coalescence
- ✅ **Shear shackle components:** Lower anvil (fixed, grooved) + Upper blade (hardened, movable)
- ✅ **Why a UTM is used in compression mode:** The compressive crosshead motion provides the two opposing shearing forces through the shackle fixture
- ✅ **Fracture plane orientation:** ≈ 45° to the rod axis (perpendicular to the direction of maximum principal tensile stress)
- ✅ **Specimen size:** Typically 12 mm, 16 mm, or 20 mm diameter; length 80–100 mm
- ✅ **Number of repetitions:** Minimum **3 specimens** to obtain a mean value (per IS guidelines)
- ✅ **Engineering applications of the test data:** Design of rivets, bolts, pins, cotter joints, knuckle joints, welded throat sections
- ✅ **Comparison mnemonic:** *Single shear = One scissor blade pair; Double shear = Two scissor blade pairs simultaneously*

> 🎯 **Final Exam Mantra for KTU 2024:**
> *"Know the formula, know the IS code, know the failure mode, know the Von Mises line, and you walk out with full marks."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexural behaviour of steel by conducting a bending test on rolled steel sections (I cross section)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL407 - PCCEL407
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flexural behaviour of steel by conducting a bending test on rolled steel sections (I cross section)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Flexural Behaviour

**Flexural behaviour** refers to the deformation and stress response of a structural member when subjected to **bending loads** (loads acting transverse to the longitudinal axis of the member, producing internal **bending moment** and **shear force**). When a rolled steel **I-section (also called I-beam, H-beam, or W-beam depending on the profile standard)** is loaded transversely, fibres above the **neutral axis (NA)** experience **compression** while fibres below the NA experience **tension**, and the **neutral axis itself carries zero longitudinal stress**.

The **bending test on a rolled steel I-section** is a standard **destructive laboratory experiment** performed in the Strength of Materials / Structural Engineering Laboratory to:

- Verify the **Euler-Bernoulli beam theory** experimentally.
- Determine the **flexural rigidity (EI)** and **Young's Modulus (E)** of structural steel.
- Validate the **bending stress equation** (Flexure Formula): $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$.
- Compute the **deflection** of the beam at various load stages and compare with theoretical predictions.
- Establish the **load vs. deflection** and **load vs. strain** relationships of the section.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Intent):**
> *To study the flexural behaviour of rolled steel I-section by subjecting it to a gradually increasing transverse load under simply supported end conditions, and to verify the theoretical bending stress distribution, deflection profile, and moment-curvature relationship.*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a **wooden diving board** at the edge of a swimming pool. As you walk towards the tip, the board **bends downward** — the *top surface* of the board is being **stretched (tension)** while the *bottom surface* is being **squeezed (compression)**. Somewhere in between, there is a layer that does **not change length** at all — this is the **neutral axis**.

A **rolled steel I-section** behaves exactly like this diving board, except that:
- Steel is much **stiffer** (E ≈ 2 × 10⁵ N/mm²).
- The **I-shape** is the *most efficient* shape for resisting bending because **most of the material is placed as far as possible from the neutral axis** (in the flanges), which dramatically increases the **moment of inertia (I)** without adding much self-weight.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy Table for Bending**

| Analogy | Top of Member | Neutral Axis | Bottom of Member |
|---|---|---|---|
| Diving board with a person | Stretched (Tension) | No change | Compressed (Compression) |
| Bookshelf loaded with books | Shelf surface in tension | Middle of plank | Compressed against wall |
| Tree branch with snow | Top fibres in tension | Inner core neutral | Bottom fibres in compression |
| I-beam in building | Top flange in compression | Web mid-height | Bottom flange in tension |

> [!TIP]
> **Why an I-section and not a solid rectangle?** A solid rectangle of the same cross-sectional area has a moment of inertia roughly **6 to 10 times smaller** than an I-section. The I-section concentrates material at the flanges (top and bottom), which is exactly where the **maximum bending stresses** occur. This is the principle of **material economy in bending design**.

---

## 1.3 The Rolled Steel I-Section — Standard Indian Designations

The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) classifies hot-rolled steel I-sections under **IS 808:1989 (Reaffirmed 2004)**. The standard designations a student will encounter in the KTU lab are:

| Designation | Full Form | Typical Lab Use |
|---|---|---|
| **ISJB** | Indian Standard Junior Beam | Light loads, short spans |
| **ISLB** | Indian Standard Light Beam | Most common in B.Tech labs |
| **ISMB** | Indian Standard Medium Beam | Heavier experiments |
| **ISWB** | Indian Standard Wide Flange Beam | Wide-flange geometry |
| **ISHB** | Indian Standard Heavy Beam | Heavy load tests |

A typical designation **ISMB 200** means an Indian Standard Medium Beam of nominal depth **200 mm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant to Memorize for KTU Viva:**
> Young's Modulus of Structural Steel, **E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa = 200 kN/mm²**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory

The classical theory used to predict flexural behaviour rests on the following **five axioms** (each tested indirectly during the lab experiment):

1. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the deformed neutral axis** after bending (no warping).
2. The material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law valid: $\sigma = E\varepsilon$).
3. The **neutral axis passes through the centroid** of the cross-section.
4. **Self-weight of the beam is neglected** compared to applied load (or added to it as a UDL).
5. **Deflections are small** compared to the span (so curvature $\kappa \approx \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$ and slope $\theta \approx \dfrac{dy}{dx}$).

> [!NOTE]
> When the load is increased to a point where steel yields, the stress-strain behaviour becomes **non-linear** (elasto-plastic). The lab test usually remains within the **elastic range** so that all formulas below apply.

---

## 2.2 Derivation Outline of the Bending Equation

The famous **bending equation** is built as a chain of three equal ratios:

$$
\dfrac{M}{I} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma}{y} \;=\; \dfrac{E}{R}
$$

where each term has a clear physical meaning. The chain emerges from the following logical sequence:

1. **Geometric compatibility** (from assumption 1) gives the strain distribution: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R}$.
2. **Constitutive law (Hooke's Law)** converts strain to stress: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$.
3. **Static equilibrium** of moments about the neutral axis: $M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^2 \, dA = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R}$.
4. Equating the three expressions yields the master relation: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$.

The detailed step-by-step derivation appears in **Section 3**.

---

## 2.3 Cross-Sectional Geometry of a Symmetrical I-Section

For a doubly-symmetric I-section, the **moment of inertia about the strong axis (x-x axis)** is calculated as:

$$
I_{xx} \;=\; \dfrac{B \cdot D^{3}}{12} \;-\; \dfrac{(B - t_w)\cdot d^{3}}{12}
$$

where

- $D$ = **Overall depth** of the section (mm)
- $B$ = **Overall width of flange** (mm)
- $t_w$ = **Thickness of web** (mm)
- $d$ = **Inner depth between flanges** $= D - 2t_f$ (mm)
- $t_f$ = **Thickness of flange** (mm)

The **elastic section modulus** is then:

$$
Z_{xx} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{xx}}{y_{max}} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{xx}}{D/2} \;=\; \dfrac{2 \cdot I_{xx}}{D}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why $Z_{xx}$ matters:** It directly gives the **maximum bending moment capacity** of the section as $M_{max} = Z_{xx} \cdot \sigma_{allowable}$. For quick KTU calculations, the I-section is supplied in steel tables (SP 6 or IS 808 handbook) so that you can **directly read off** $I_{xx}$ and $Z_{xx}$ without re-deriving them.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table collects every formula you are likely to need in the Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on this experiment. **Print this and stick it to your lab file.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | SI / KTU Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bending stress at fibre distance $y$ | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | N/mm² (MPa) |
| 2 | Maximum bending stress | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z} \;=\; \dfrac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I}$ | N/mm² |
| 3 | Master bending equation (3-ratio chain) | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | — |
| 4 | Radius of curvature at a section | $R = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{M}$ | mm |
| 5 | Slope of beam | $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | radian |
| 6 | Curvature of beam | $\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \approx \dfrac{M}{EI}$ | 1/mm |
| 7 | Section modulus (general) | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | mm³ |
| 8 | Section modulus (symmetric I) | $Z_{xx} = \dfrac{2 I_{xx}}{D}$ | mm³ |
| 9 | Max B.M. for SS beam with central point load $W$ | $M_{max} = \dfrac{W L}{4}$ | N·mm |
| 10 | Max B.M. for SS beam with UDL $w$ | $M_{max} = \dfrac{w L^{2}}{8}$ | N·mm |
| 11 | Max deflection for SS beam, central point load | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{W L^{3}}{48 E I}$ | mm |
| 12 | Max deflection for SS beam, UDL | $\delta_{max} = \dfrac{5 w L^{4}}{384 E I}$ | mm |
| 13 | Bending strain at fibre $y$ | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{y}{R} = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}$ | dimensionless |
| 14 | Flexural rigidity | $EI$ | N·mm² |
| 15 | Strain at extreme fibre (max) | $\varepsilon_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{E} = \dfrac{M}{Z \cdot E}$ | dimensionless |
| 16 | Slope at supports (SS, point load) | $\theta_A = -\theta_B = \dfrac{W L^{2}}{16 E I}$ | rad |
| 17 | Slope at supports (SS, UDL) | $\theta_A = -\theta_B = \dfrac{w L^{3}}{24 E I}$ | rad |
| 18 | Young's Modulus of steel | $E$ | $2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm² |
| 19 | Poisson's ratio of steel | $\nu$ | 0.30 |
| 20 | Density of structural steel | $\rho$ | 7850 kg/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where each formula is used in the lab:**
> - Formulas 1–3 → Verify bending stress at the extreme fibre.
> - Formulas 9–10 → Calculate the theoretical bending moment for a given load.
> - Formulas 11–12 → Predict the theoretical central deflection.
> - Formulas 13–15 → Verify experimental strain gauge readings.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The flexural behaviour of I-sections governs the design of:

- **Building floor beams** in steel-framed structures (offices, warehouses, airports).
- **Bridge girders** in road and railway bridges.
- **Crane girders** in industrial workshops.
- **Purlins and girts** in pre-engineered metal buildings.
- **Railway sleepers** in some advanced composite systems.

Understanding the **moment-curvature relationship** $M/EI = 1/R$ is foundational for advanced topics such as **plastic analysis**, **moment redistribution** in continuous beams, and **finite element analysis (FEA)** of frames.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of the Bending Equation

We derive the famous three-ratio equation $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$ from first principles.

### Step 1 — Consider a small element of the beam

Consider a beam of length $L$ bent into a circular arc of radius $R$. Take an infinitesimally small element subtending an angle $d\theta$ at the centre of curvature.

- **Original length** of the element (along neutral axis): $dx = R \cdot d\theta$.
- **Length of a fibre at distance $y$ from the neutral axis** (above NA, $y > 0$): $dx' = (R + y) \cdot d\theta$.

### Step 2 — Compute the longitudinal strain at distance $y$

The change in length of the fibre at distance $y$ is:

$$
\Delta \ell \;=\; dx' - dx \;=\; (R + y) d\theta - R d\theta \;=\; y \cdot d\theta
$$

The **original length** of the same fibre is $dx = R \cdot d\theta$. Therefore the longitudinal strain is:

$$
\varepsilon \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta \ell}{dx} \;=\; \dfrac{y \cdot d\theta}{R \cdot d\theta} \;=\; \dfrac{y}{R}
$$

This is the **kinematic (compatibility) relation** — strain is **linear in $y$** with $1/R$ as the slope.

### Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law to obtain stress

For linearly elastic material: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$

$$
\sigma \;=\; E \cdot \varepsilon \;=\; E \cdot \dfrac{y}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}
$$

This is the **constitutive (material) relation** — stress is also **linear in $y$**.

### Step 4 — Apply static equilibrium of forces (axial)

For pure bending there is **no net axial force**, so:

$$
\sum F_x \;=\; 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_A \sigma \, dA \;=\; 0
$$

Substitute $\sigma = \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R}$:

$$
\int_A \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R} \, dA \;=\; \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y \, dA \;=\; 0
$$

Since $E/R \neq 0$, we must have $\int_A y \, dA = 0$. By the definition of the **centroid**, this integral vanishes **only if $y$ is measured from the centroidal axis**. Therefore, **the neutral axis coincides with the centroidal axis of the cross-section** — proving assumption 3 of the theory.

### Step 5 — Apply static equilibrium of moments

The internal bending moment $M$ at the section must equal the moment of the stresses about the neutral axis:

$$
M \;=\; \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA \;=\; \int_A \dfrac{E \cdot y}{R} \cdot y \, dA \;=\; \dfrac{E}{R} \int_A y^{2} \, dA
$$

Recognise the **second moment of area** $I = \int_A y^{2} \, dA$. Therefore:

$$
M \;=\; \dfrac{E \cdot I}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{M}{E \cdot I}
$$

### Step 6 — Form the master equation

From Steps 2, 3, and 5, we have three equal ratios:

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{M}{I} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma}{y} \;=\; \dfrac{E}{R}}
$$

This **single equation** encapsulates the entire theory of pure bending.

### Step 7 — Express in differential form (Euler-Bernoulli beam equation)

Since $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}}$ for small deflections:

$$
\dfrac{d^{2}y}{dx^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{M(x)}{E \cdot I}
$$

This **fourth-order linear differential equation** (when written as $\dfrac{d^{4}y}{dx^{4}} = \dfrac{w(x)}{EI}$) is the workhorse of **beam deflection analysis**. Integrating it twice with appropriate boundary conditions gives the **deflection $y(x)$** and **slope $\theta(x)$** along the beam.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem:** A simply supported rolled steel I-section **ISMB 200** of length **L = 1.5 m** is loaded at mid-span by a **central point load W = 30 kN**. Verify the maximum bending stress and the central deflection.
>
> **Given Section Properties (from SP 6 / IS 808):**
> - $D = 200$ mm, $B = 100$ mm, $t_f = 10.8$ mm, $t_w = 5.7$ mm
> - $I_{xx} = 2235.4 \times 10^{4}$ mm⁴
> - $Z_{xx} = 223.54 \times 10^{3}$ mm³
> - $E = 2 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm²

### Sub-step (a) — Maximum Bending Moment

For a simply supported beam with central point load:

$$
M_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot L}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{30 \times 10^{3} \text{ N} \times 1500 \text{ mm}}{4} \;=\; 11.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ N·mm}
$$

### Sub-step (b) — Maximum Bending Stress

$$
\sigma_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{M_{max}}{Z_{xx}} \;=\; \dfrac{11.25 \times 10^{6}}{223.54 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 50.33 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \;(\approx 50.33 \text{ MPa})
$$

### Sub-step (c) — Theoretical Central Deflection

$$
\delta_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot L^{3}}{48 \, E \, I_{xx}} \;=\; \dfrac{30 \times 10^{3} \times (1500)^{3}}{48 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 2235.4 \times 10^{4}}
$$

Numerator: $30 \times 10^{3} \times 3.375 \times 10^{9} = 1.0125 \times 10^{14}$ N·mm³

Denominator: $48 \times 2 \times 10^{5} \times 2.2354 \times 10^{7} = 2.146 \times 10^{14}$ N·mm²

$$
\delta_{max} \;=\; \dfrac{1.0125 \times 10^{14}}{2.146 \times 10^{14}} \;=\; 0.4717 \text{ mm}
$$

### Sub-step (d) — Verification Check

- $\sigma_{max} = 50.33$ MPa $\ll \sigma_{yield} = 250$ MPa → **Elastic behaviour confirmed**. ✓
- $\delta_{max}/L = 0.4717/1500 = 0.000314 < 1/300$ → **Small-deflection assumption valid**. ✓

> [!TIP]
> In your record, you must **tabulate** the experimental deflection (from dial gauge) and theoretical deflection side by side, then compute the **percentage error** $= \dfrac{\vert \delta_{exp} - \delta_{th} \vert}{\delta_{th}} \times 100\%$. A KTU-typical acceptable error is **< 10%**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Deflection & Slope Coefficients for Common Load Cases

The coefficients $a$ and $b$ below are useful for instant KTU problem-solving.

| Loading Case | $\delta_{max}$ (max deflection) | $\theta_{max}$ (max slope) | Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Point load $W$ at centre | $\delta = \dfrac{W L^{3}}{48 EI}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{W L^{2}}{16 EI}$ | at supports |
| UDL $w$ over full span | $\delta = \dfrac{5 w L^{4}}{384 EI}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{w L^{3}}{24 EI}$ | at supports |
| Point load $W$ at distance $a$ from left | $\delta = \dfrac{W a^{2} b^{2}}{3 EI L}$ | — | at load point |
| Couple $M_0$ at one end (cantilever) | $\delta = \dfrac{M_0 L^{2}}{2 EI}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{M_0 L}{EI}$ | at free end |
| UDL on cantilever | $\delta = \dfrac{w L^{4}}{8 EI}$ | $\theta = \dfrac{w L^{3}}{6 EI}$ | at free end |

> [!NOTE]
> Always convert the moment of inertia to a **consistent unit** (e.g., mm⁴) before plugging into these formulas. Mixing mm and m is the **single most common mistake** that students make in KTU exams.

> When you draw these in your lab record manually, use the following conventions:
> - **Top fibre of the I-section** → show arrows pointing **inward** (compression).
> - **Bottom fibre** → show arrows pointing **outward** (tension).
> - **Neutral axis** → a **horizontal dashed line** through the centroid with the label **"N.A. – σ = 0"**.
> - **Bending moment diagram (BMD)** for central point load → **triangle** with peak at mid-span.
> - **Deflection profile** → smooth **parabolic curve** with maximum at mid-span.
