# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define hardness. List the four common methods of hardness measurement and state the principle of the Rockwell hardness test.**

**Model Answer:**

**Hardness:** Hardness is the property of a material which enables it to resist plastic deformation, usually by indentation or scratching. It is an index of the resistance of a material to local plastic deformation.

**Four common methods of hardness measurement:**

1. **Brinell Hardness Test (BHN)** — uses a hardened steel ball indenter, measures diameter of impression.
2. **Rockwell Hardness Test (HR)** — uses a diamond cone or steel ball, measures depth of penetration.
3. **Vickers Hardness Test (HV)** — uses a diamond pyramid indenter, measures diagonal of impression.
4. **Mohs Scratch Test** — qualitative scale based on which mineral scratches the other.

**Principle of Rockwell Hardness Test:**

The Rockwell hardness test measures the **net increase in depth of impression** as the load on an indenter is increased from a minor load (10 kgf) to a major load (60, 100, or 150 kgf depending on the scale), and then the major load is removed. The hardness number is read directly from a calibrated dial gauge, where each scale unit corresponds to 0.002 mm of permanent penetration.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Cue:** Award 1 mark for the correct definition, 1 mark for the four methods, and 1 mark for the principle. Listing only 3 methods forfeits ½ mark.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Explain the principle of the Vickers hardness test. Why is the face angle of the Vickers indenter chosen as 136°?**

**Model Answer:**

**Principle of the Vickers Hardness Test:**

The Vickers hardness test is based on measuring the **resistance of a material to plastic deformation** when a square-based diamond pyramid indenter (with a face angle of 136° between opposite faces) is pressed into the specimen under a known static load $F$ (typically 1 to 100 kgf). After the load is removed, the two diagonals $d_1$ and $d_2$ of the square impression are measured using an optical microscope. The mean diagonal $d$ is then used to compute the **Vickers Hardness Number (HV)** using the formula:

$$ HV = \frac{1.8544 \cdot F}{d^2} $$

where $F$ is in kgf and $d$ is in mm, giving HV in kgf/mm².

**Why the 136° face angle?**

The 136° face angle was **historically chosen** so that the Vickers Hardness Number would be **numerically close to the Brinell Hardness Number** for the typical Brinell load range (above 300 kgf) on steel. This was important because:
1. It allowed engineers to **directly compare** old Brinell data with new Vickers data without numerical conversion.
2. It placed the Vickers geometry near the **optimum angle** for minimizing measurement errors in diagonal length (errors in $d$ are minimized when $\tan(\theta/2) \approx 1$, i.e., $\theta \approx 90°$ to $136°$).
3. The contact area varies as $d^2$, just like the projected area of the Brinell impression at the same load, ensuring a near-constant hardness number across the load range.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Cue:** 1½ marks for the principle (with formula) and 1½ marks for the 136° justification. Mentioning "to match BHN numerically" is the key phrase — examiners look for it.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, has internal choice, and is split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The model answer below provides the full step-by-step solution a 90+ percentile student would write.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a Rockwell hardness testing machine. Discuss the differences between the **Rockwell A, B, and C** scales in terms of indenter, load, and typical applications.

**(b)** A Vickers hardness test is carried out on a **brass specimen** using a load of **10 kgf**. The two diagonals of the indentation are measured as **0.520 mm and 0.530 mm**. Calculate the Vickers Hardness Number. If the same specimen is tested by the Rockwell B method and a permanent depth of penetration of **0.072 mm** is recorded, what is the HRB value? Comment on the consistency of the two readings.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**1. Working Principle of Rockwell Hardness Testing Machine:**

A schematic representation of the Rockwell testing machine is described by the following functional sequence:

1. The **specimen** is placed on the **anvil (1)** which rests on a precision screw.
2. The **minor load of 10 kgf** is applied by rotating the handwheel. This load is transmitted through a system of levers. The minor load seats the indenter and eliminates surface roughness effects. The small pointer on the dial becomes vertical.
3. The **main dial is set to zero** (this corresponds to the initial penetration depth $h_0$ under the minor load).
4. The **major load** (60, 100, or 150 kgf) is applied by releasing a **dashpot-controlled dashpot lever** that gradually transfers the dead-weight load to the indenter. The loading is completed in **4 to 8 seconds**.
5. The major load is **held for a dwell time of 2 to 4 seconds** to allow plastic flow to stabilize.
6. The major load is **removed** by operating the dashpot lever in the reverse direction. The minor load continues to act.
7. The dial reading directly gives the **Rockwell Hardness Number**. For the **diamond-cone scales (A, C, D)**, the reading is on the **black scale**; for the **ball scales (B, E, F)**, the reading is on the **red scale**.
8. The specimen is lowered by rotating the handwheel, and the test is complete.

**2. Comparison of Rockwell A, B, and C Scales:**

| Feature | Rockwell A (HRA) | Rockwell B (HRB) | Rockwell C (HRC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Indenter Type** | Diamond cone (Brale) | Steel ball, 1/16 inch (1.588 mm) | Diamond cone (Brale) |
| **Minor Load** | 10 kgf | 10 kgf | 10 kgf |
| **Major Load** | 60 kgf | 100 kgf | 150 kgf |
| **Total Load** | 70 kgf | 110 kgf | 160 kgf |
| **Scale Dial Color** | Black | Red | Black |
| **Formula Constant N** | 100 | 130 | 100 |
| **Typical Materials** | Tungsten carbide, thin hard steel, cemented carbides | Soft steels, brass, aluminium, annealed copper | Hardened steels, tool steels, martensitic stainless steel |
| **Typical Range** | 60 to 90 HRA | 0 to 100 HRB | 20 to 70 HRC |
| **Specimen Thickness Min.** | Thin samples permissible | Same as HRA | Thicker samples required |

> **Key takeaway:** The choice of scale depends on the **expected hardness** of the material. The diamond-cone indenter is used for hard materials (small impression), while the ball indenter is used for soft materials (larger impression). The "C" scale is the most common industrial scale for hardened steel components.

**(7 marks: 3 marks for the working principle with sketch, 4 marks for the comparison table with applications)**

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Mean diagonal for Vickers test:**

$$ d = \frac{d_1 + d_2}{2} = \frac{0.520 + 0.530}{2} = \frac{1.050}{2} = 0.525 \text{ mm} $$

**Step 2 — Square the mean diagonal:**

$$ d^2 = (0.525)^2 = 0.2756 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 3 — Apply the Vickers formula:**

$$ HV = \frac{1.8544 \cdot F}{d^2} = \frac{1.8544 \times 10}{0.2756} $$

**Step 4 — Numerator evaluation:**

$$ 1.8544 \times 10 = 18.544 $$

**Step 5 — Final division:**

$$ HV = \frac{18.544}{0.2756} \approx 67.30 \text{ kgf/mm}^2 $$

**Step 6 — Compute the Rockwell B hardness:**

For the **Rockwell B scale**, $N = 130$ and $s = 0.002$ mm.

$$ HRB = 130 - \frac{e}{s} = 130 - \frac{0.072}{0.002} = 130 - 36 = 94 $$

**Step 7 — Comment on consistency:**

The Vickers Hardness Number of 67.3 kgf/mm² (i.e., 67.3 HV) corresponds, on the standard ASTM E140 conversion table, to a Rockwell B hardness of approximately **HRB ≈ 33–35** for annealed brass.

However, the *calculated* HRB value from the depth measurement is **94**, which corresponds to a Vickers Hardness of approximately **200 HV** — that is, nearly **3× higher** than the Vickers result.

**Conclusion:** The two readings are **inconsistent**. The most likely reasons are:
1. The Vickers test was performed on a **softer, annealed** region of the brass (perhaps a part of the bar with larger grain size) while the Rockwell B test landed on a **cold-worked** region.
2. The Vickers diagonals were measured inaccurately (microscope calibration error or parallax).
3. The depth reading on the Rockwell machine may have been misread (typical errors: 0.072 vs 0.027 — a factor-of-three off).

> **Recommendation:** Repeat the test on a properly prepared, uniform surface using **both methods on the same spot (or adjacent spots)**, and report the mean of three readings to comply with IS 1586 (minimum 3 indentations per location).

**(7 marks: 3 marks for HV calculation, 2 marks for HRB calculation, 2 marks for the consistency comment)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Describe the Vickers hardness testing procedure in detail. Derive the expression for the Vickers Hardness Number in terms of the applied load and the diagonal of the indentation.

**(b)** A mild steel specimen is tested using the **Vickers method** with a load of **30 kgf**. The mean diagonal length measured is **0.420 mm**. Determine the Vickers Hardness Number in kgf/mm² and convert it to Rockwell C hardness using the standard conversion relation $HRC = 0.1691 \cdot HV - 0.6838$ (valid in the 240–940 HV range). Estimate the ultimate tensile strength of the mild steel.

#### Model Answer — Part (a)

**1. Detailed Vickers Hardness Testing Procedure:**

**A. Specimen Preparation:**
- Cut a representative sample using a hacksaw or abrasive cutter.
- Grind the bottom face flat (to ensure anvil contact).
- Grind the test face progressively with 220, 400, 600, 800, 1000 grit SiC papers.
- For high-precision work, polish with 1 µm diamond paste on a disc polisher.
- Etching is **not required** (unlike metallography) but is permitted.
- Clean the surface with acetone and dry with hot air.

**B. Test Setup:**
- Switch on the Vickers machine and the microscope illumination.
- Select the **load** $F$ based on specimen thickness and expected hardness. Standard loads are 1, 5, 10, 30, 50, or 100 kgf. The **rule of thumb** is that the indentation depth must be less than 1/7 of the specimen thickness. For mild steel, 30 kgf is common.
- Verify the indenter face angle ($136° \pm 0.5°$) and the objective lens cleanliness.

**C. Indentation:**
- Place the specimen on the anvil and **gently** raise it using the elevating screw until the test surface just contacts the indenter tip.
- Apply the **load smoothly over 5 to 10 seconds** (the dashpot control ensures this).
- Hold the load for the **dwell time** — typically **10 to 15 seconds** (longer than Rockwell because plastic flow continues for longer in ductile materials).
- Release the load and lower the anvil.

**D. Measurement:**
- Swing the **optical microscope** over the indentation.
- Use the **10× objective** to focus the diagonal clearly. Adjust the cross-hair to align with one corner of the square indentation, then traverse to the opposite corner and read $d_1$. Rotate the eyepiece 90° and read $d_2$.
- The micrometer drum typically reads in 0.001 mm increments. Estimate to **0.0005 mm** by interpolation.

**E. Calculation and Reporting:**
- Compute $d = (d_1 + d_2)/2$.
- Apply the Vickers formula $HV = 1.8544 \cdot F / d^2$.
- Report HV to **3 significant figures** along with the load used (e.g., **185 HV 30**).

**2. Derivation of the Vickers Hardness Number:**

Let the diamond pyramid indenter have a face angle $\theta = 136°$ between opposite faces, so the half-face angle is $\theta/2 = 68°$. The indentation is a square-based pyramid with the same face angle (the indenter penetrates until plastic flow matches the geometry).

Let $d$ be the mean diagonal of the square impression. The **slant height** $h$ from the apex to the midpoint of a base edge is:

$$ h = \frac{d}{2 \sin(\theta/2)} = \frac{d}{2 \sin(68°)} $$

The four triangular lateral faces of the pyramidal impression each have:
- Base = $d/\sqrt{2}$ (side of the square base)
- Slant height = $h$

Area of one face:

$$ A_{\text{face}} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}} \times h $$

Total lateral area of the impression (which is the contact area used in the hardness definition):

$$ A = 4 \times A_{\text{face}} = 4 \times \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}} \times h = \frac{2 d h}{\sqrt{2}} = d h \sqrt{2} $$

Substituting $h = d / [2 \sin(68°)]$:

$$ A = d \cdot \frac{d}{2 \sin(68°)} \cdot \sqrt{2} = \frac{d^2 \sqrt{2}}{2 \sin(68°)} $$

Using $\sqrt{2}/[2 \sin(68°)] = 1.4142 / [2 \times 0.9272] = 1.4142 / 1.8544 = 0.7624$:

$$ A = \frac{d^2}{1.8544} $$

By definition, the Vickers Hardness Number is the load divided by the contact area:

$$ HV = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{F}{d^2 / 1.8544} = \frac{1.8544 \cdot F}{d^2} $$

**(7 marks: 4 marks for the procedure, 3 marks for the derivation)**

#### Model Answer — Part (b)

**Given data:**
- Load: $F = 30$ kgf
- Mean diagonal: $d = 0.420$ mm

**Step 1 — Compute $d^2$:**

$$ d^2 = (0.420)^2 = 0.1764 \text{ mm}^2 $$

**Step 2 — Apply the Vickers formula:**

$$ HV = \frac{1.8544 \times 30}{0.1764} = \frac{55.632}{0.1764} $$

**Step 3 — Final division:**

$$ HV \approx 315.37 \text{ kgf/mm}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Convert to Rockwell C using the given relation:**

$$ HRC = 0.1691 \times HV - 0.6838 $$

$$ HRC = 0.1691 \times 315.37 - 0.6838 $$

$$ HRC = 53.32 - 0.68 \approx 32.64 $$

**Step 5 — Estimate the ultimate tensile strength:**

For steels, the empirical correlation is $\sigma_u \approx 0.33 \times HV$ in MPa (this holds well in the range 100–500 HV).

$$ \sigma_u = 0.33 \times 315.37 \times 9.81 \approx 0.33 \times 3093 \approx 1021 \text{ MPa} $$

Wait — this is a very high strength for "mild" steel. The likely interpretation is that the specimen is **medium-carbon steel** (e.g., EN8 / AISI 1040) rather than true mild steel (mild steel typically has $HV < 180$ and $\sigma_u < 450$ MPa).

**Step 6 — Comment on the result:**

A measured HV of 315 corresponds more accurately to a **medium-carbon engineering steel** that has been **quenched and tempered** rather than a true mild steel in the as-rolled condition. The student should:
1. Re-confirm the material specification with the lab instructor.
2. Note the discrepancy in the lab record book.
3. Cross-check the diagonal measurement (if the true diagonal is closer to 0.600 mm, the HV drops to ~155, consistent with mild steel).

**(7 marks: 2 marks for HV calculation, 2 marks for HRC conversion, 1.5 marks for tensile strength, 1.5 marks for the comment on material identity)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the constant "1.8544"** in the Vickers formula and writing $HV = F / d^2$ directly. This loses **2 marks** in a 7-mark sub-question.
> 2. **Mixing units**: using $F$ in Newtons with the constant 1.8544 (which is calibrated for kgf) or using mm and cm inconsistently. The KTU key requires the constant **0.1891** for N-based formulas.
> 3. **Confusing the Rockwell constants 100 and 130**: ball scales use 130, diamond scales use 100. A 60 HRC value of $e$ computed as 0.080 mm gives $HRC = 100 - 40 = 60$. Students often write $100 - 80 = 20$ by forgetting to divide by 0.002.
> 4. **Omitting units in the final answer**: writing "HV = 365" instead of "HV = 365.8 kgf/mm²" is a **½ mark penalty**.
> 5. **Not averaging the two diagonals**: Vickers requires the *mean* of $d_1$ and $d_2$. A 5–10% asymmetry indicates the surface was not perpendicular to the indenter axis; such readings must be discarded.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention the dwell time** in the procedure (2–4 s for Rockwell, 10–15 s for Vickers). Examiners often allot 1 mark for this in the procedure section.
> 7. **Confusing the minor load 10 kgf with the major load of the B scale** (100 kgf). The total load on the B scale is 110 kgf, not 100 kgf. This is a common error in numerical problems.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Exam Rapid-Recall Card** — read this section 5 minutes before the viva or paper.

- [x] **Hardness definition**: resistance to **localized plastic deformation** by indentation.
- [x] **Rockwell formula**: $HR = N - e / 0.002$ mm, with **$N = 100$** (diamond scales A, C, D) or **$N = 130$** (ball scales B, E, F).
- [x] **Vickers formula**: $HV = 1.8544 \cdot F / d^2$ (kgf, mm) or $HV = 0.1891 \cdot F / d^2$ (N, mm).
- [x] **Vickers indenter face angle**: $136°$ (chosen to match BHN numerically).
- [x] **Rockwell scales covered in lab**: **A** (60 kgf, diamond), **B** (100 kgf, 1/16" ball), **C** (150 kgf, diamond).
- [x] **Minor load** is always **10 kgf** in standard Rockwell.
- [x] **Dwell time**: **2–4 s** for Rockwell, **10–15 s** for Vickers.
- [x] **Specimen thickness rule** for Vickers: $t \geq 1.5 \times d$ to avoid the anvil-effect.
- [x] **Minimum spacing** between two indentations: **$3d$ centre-to-centre**.
- [x] **Scale-unit conversion**: 1 Rockwell unit = **0.002 mm** of permanent penetration.
- [x] **Standard units**: HV in **kgf/mm²**; in SI, multiply by 9.81 to get **MPa**.
- [x] **Empirical tensile-strength rule for steels**: $\sigma_u \approx 0.33 \times HV$ (MPa).
- [x] **The 136° face angle** is **NOT a coincidence** — it preserves numerical continuity with the older Brinell scale.
- [x] **Readings**: Rockwell A and C on the **black** dial scale; Rockwell B, E, F on the **red** dial scale.
- [x] **Always take at least 3 readings** and report the mean (per IS 1586 / ISO 6508).
- [x] **Cleaning** the specimen with acetone before testing is **mandatory**; oil films give falsely high readings.
- [x] **For brass**: expect HRB 50–85; for **aluminium**: HRB 30–60; for **annealed copper**: HRB 30–45; for **mild steel**: HRB 70–90; for **hardened tool steel**: HRC 55–65.
- [x] **Rockwell test is faster** than Vickers; **Vickers is more accurate** and applicable to a wider hardness range.
- [x] **Mention the dwell time, the load, and the indenter type** in any procedure-based answer for full marks.
- [x] **Sketch the indenter geometry** in descriptive answers — it earns 1 mark by itself.
- [x] **Cross-check units** before writing the final answer; use $\vert$ for absolute value if any modulus appears in the calculation.

---

> [!TIP]
> **End-of-Sheet Note for KTU Students:** The Vickers test is more frequently asked in derivation-style questions (it has a clean geometric derivation), while the Rockwell test dominates in numerical and procedure-based questions (because of the 100-vs-130 confusion trick). Master **both** derivations and the constant 1.8544 origin story for a guaranteed 14-marker.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimation of modulus of rigidity of steel by performing tension and compression tests on spring specimens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL407 - PCCEL407 (Strength of Materials / Material Testing Laboratory)
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Estimation of modulus of rigidity of steel by performing tension and compression tests on spring specimens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Modulus of Rigidity** (also called the **Shear Modulus**, denoted by the symbol **G**) is a fundamental elastic constant of a material that quantifies its resistance to **shear deformation** (angular distortion) when subjected to a tangential (shearing) force. It is mathematically defined as the ratio of **shear stress (τ)** to **shear strain (γ)** within the elastic limit of the material:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

The **SI unit** of modulus of rigidity is **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**, and the commonly used engineering multiple is **GPa (Gigapascal)**. For **mild steel** and most engineering steels, the standard reported value of G is approximately **77 – 80 GPa (≈ 11.2 × 10⁶ psi)**.

In the context of a **helical spring**, when an axial load (tension or compression) is applied along the spring's longitudinal axis, the spring wire does **not** experience direct axial stress. Instead, every cross-section of the coiled wire is subjected to a **torsional moment** (twisting) plus a **direct shear force**, both of which produce shear stresses on the wire cross-section. By measuring the axial deflection of the spring under known axial loads, the modulus of rigidity **G** of the spring material can be back-calculated. This forms the experimental basis of this classic KTU laboratory experiment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment establishes the relationship between **axial spring stiffness (k)** and the **material property G**. The spring acts as a "torsion specimen" — converting an easy-to-apply axial load into a measurable torsional deformation, from which **G** is extracted.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you have a **spiral telephone-cord spring** lying on a table. Now, instead of trying to grab the wire and twist it directly (which is very hard to do precisely), you simply **pull the spring along its length** (or push it together). The spring stretches or compresses by a measurable amount.

What is happening *inside* the wire is fascinating: even though you are applying a force along the axis, every tiny piece of wire is being **twisted** like a tiny torsion bar. The whole spring acts like a **collection of small torsion bars** stacked in series along the coil. Because the relationship between twist and force is well known, by simply measuring how much the spring lengthens or shortens for a given pull or push, you can deduce how *stiff* the wire material is against twisting — and that stiffness *is* the **modulus of rigidity G**.

**Real-world analogy:** Think of a **rope wound around a pole**. Pulling the free end makes the rope unspool — the fibers twist. The spring experiment uses the same geometric principle: axial force → wire torsion → measurable deflection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** A helical spring is essentially a **torsion-testing device** disguised as a compression/tension element. The experiment cleverly uses the spring's geometric amplification (D >> d) to make small wire-level torsions visible as large axial deflections.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Values (Memorize These)

| Material | Modulus of Rigidity (G) in GPa |
|---|---|
| **Mild Steel** | **77 – 80** |
| Stainless Steel | 77 |
| Brass | 36 – 41 |
| Copper | 44 – 48 |
| Aluminium | 25 – 27 |
| Cast Iron | 40 – 50 |

> [!TIP]
> **Physical Constant to Remember:** Theoretical relationship between G, Young's Modulus (E), and Poisson's ratio (ν) is **G = E / [2(1 + ν)]**. For steel with E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3, this yields G = 200 / 2.6 ≈ **76.92 GPa**, confirming the experimental value.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Spring Specimen**
A close-coiled helical spring has the following key geometric parameters:
- **d** = wire diameter
- **D** = mean coil diameter (measured from the **center of the wire on one side to the center of the wire on the opposite side**)
- **N** = number of active coils
- **C** = spring index = D / d (typically 4 to 12 for stable springs; **C < 4 causes buckling**, **C > 12 causes tangling**)

**Step 2 — Force Decomposition at a Wire Cross-Section**
When an axial load **P** is applied, consider any cross-section perpendicular to the helix axis. The internal forces acting on this cross-section are:
- A **direct shear force** = P (acting vertically downward)
- A **torsional moment** = T = P × (D/2) (acting about the centroidal axis of the wire cross-section)
- A **bending moment** = P × (D/2) (acting about a diameter of the wire — usually neglected for small helix angles)

**Step 3 — Shear Stress Distribution**
The **direct shear stress** is uniform:
$$\tau_d = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

The **torsional shear stress** is maximum at the **outer fiber** (radius = d/2):
$$\tau_t = \frac{T \cdot r}{J} = \frac{(P \cdot D/2) \cdot (d/2)}{(\pi d^4 / 32)} = \frac{16 P D}{\pi d^3}$$

The **maximum shear stress** at the **inner fiber** of the coil (where both stresses act in the same direction):
$$\tau_{max} = \tau_d + \tau_t = \frac{8 P D}{\pi d^3} + \frac{4 P}{\pi d^2} \approx \frac{8 P D}{\pi d^3}$$
(approximation valid because **D >> d**, so the torsional term dominates).

**Step 4 — Strain Energy Method for Deflection**
Using **Castigliano's theorem** (or the standard energy method), the total strain energy stored in the spring due to torsion (neglecting the smaller direct shear and bending contributions) is:
$$U = \frac{T^2 \cdot L}{2 G J} \cdot N_{turns}$$
where **L = π D N** is the total wire length.

Differentiating strain energy with respect to load:
$$\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} = \frac{8 P D^3 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 5 — Spring Stiffness and G Extraction**
Defining **spring stiffness k = P / δ**:
$$k = \frac{G d^4}{8 D^3 N}$$

Rearranging to find **G experimentally**:
$$\boxed{G = \frac{8 P D^3 N}{d^4 \, \delta} = \frac{8 k D^3 N}{d^4}}$$

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $G = \tau / \gamma$ | Fundamental definition of modulus of rigidity | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $G = E / [2(1 + \nu)]$ | Relation with Young's modulus & Poisson's ratio | Pa |
| 3 | $\tau_{max} = 8PD / (\pi d^3)$ | Max shear stress in spring wire | Pa |
| 4 | $\delta = 8 P D^3 N / (G d^4)$ | Spring deflection under axial load P | m |
| 5 | $k = P / \delta = G d^4 / (8 D^3 N)$ | Spring stiffness (slope of P vs δ graph) | N/m |
| 6 | $G_{exp} = 8 k D^3 N / d^4$ | **EXPERIMENTAL FORMULA** — primary KTU equation | Pa |
| 7 | $C = D / d$ | Spring index (geometric ratio) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $L = \pi D N$ | Total length of spring wire | m |
| 9 | $U = (T^2 L) / (2 G J)$ | Strain energy in helical spring | J |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FOR KTU EXAMS:** Equation #6 ($G_{exp} = 8 k D^3 N / d^4$) is the **most-tested equation** in viva voce and numerical problems. Memorize the **position of d⁴ in the denominator** — many students mistakenly write d³ or d².

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The helical spring experiment is not just an academic exercise. It has critical applications in:

- **Automotive suspension systems** — coil springs, leaf-spring helpers
- **Valve springs** in IC engines (must be designed with precise G for correct opening/closing)
- **Railway buffer springs** and **railway bogie suspensions**
- **Aerospace landing gear** (often composite or high-G steel springs)
- **Heavy machinery shock absorbers**
- **Seismic isolation devices** in earthquake-resistant buildings
- **Pen springs, door closers, mousetrap mechanisms**

Knowing G accurately ensures that a spring will deliver the **deflection, energy storage, and fatigue life** expected in service.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Spring Deflection from First Principles

**Given:** A close-coiled helical spring with wire diameter **d**, mean coil diameter **D**, and **N** active coils. An axial tensile/compressive load **P** is applied.

**Goal:** Derive the expression for axial deflection $\delta$ in terms of **P**, **G**, **d**, **D**, **N**.

**Step 1 — Establish Internal Torsional Moment**

Consider a small element of the wire at the top of the helix. If we make an imaginary cut through the wire, the lower portion of the spring must be in equilibrium under the applied load P. The internal forces at the cut cross-section are:
- A vertical shear force = **P**
- A torsional moment about the wire's centroidal axis = **T = P × (D/2)**

**Explanation:** The moment arm is **D/2** (radius of the coil), and the force is **P** applied along the central axis. This produces a torque that tends to "unwind" or "tighten" the coil.

**Step 2 — Express the Strain Energy in a Small Wire Segment**

For a small element of wire of length **ds**, subjected to a torque **T**, the strain energy is:
$$dU = \frac{T^2 \, ds}{2 G J}$$

where $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ is the polar moment of inertia of the wire's circular cross-section.

**Step 3 — Integrate Over the Entire Wire Length**

The total length of wire in the spring is **L = π D N** (circumference of one coil × number of coils). Since T and J are constant along the wire:
$$U = \int_0^L \frac{T^2 \, ds}{2 G J} = \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J} = \frac{T^2 \cdot \pi D N}{2 G J}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute T and J**

$$U = \frac{(P D / 2)^2 \cdot \pi D N}{2 G \cdot (\pi d^4 / 32)}$$

$$U = \frac{P^2 D^2 \cdot \pi D N \cdot 32}{4 \cdot 2 G \pi d^4}$$

$$U = \frac{8 P^2 D^3 N}{2 G d^4} = \frac{4 P^2 D^3 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Castigliano's Theorem**

By Castigliano's first theorem, the deflection in the direction of the applied load P is:
$$\delta = \frac{\partial U}{\partial P} = \frac{\partial}{\partial P} \left( \frac{4 P^2 D^3 N}{G d^4} \right) = \frac{8 P D^3 N}{G d^4}$$

**Step 6 — Final Working Equation**

$$\boxed{\delta = \frac{8 P D^3 N}{G d^4}}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange to Solve for G**

$$G = \frac{8 P D^3 N}{d^4 \, \delta}$$

Since in a lab, we plot a **Load (P) vs Deflection (δ)** graph and obtain the slope **k = P/δ**:

$$G = \frac{8 k D^3 N}{d^4}$$

This is the **experimental formula** used to compute G.

### 3.2 Derivation — Relation Between G, E, and ν

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material, the three elastic constants are related by:

$$E = 2 G (1 + \nu)$$

**Derivation sketch:** Consider a square element of side **a** subjected to pure shear τ on all four faces. This creates a state of **pure shear** which can be decomposed into two orthogonal uniaxial stress states of magnitude τ (one tensile along a diagonal, one compressive along the other diagonal). The principal stresses are:
$$\sigma_1 = \tau, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -\tau$$

The principal strains in the tensile direction are:
$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E} [\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_3] = \frac{\tau (1 + \nu)}{E}$$

The shear strain γ is related to principal strain by $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_3 = 2 \varepsilon_1 = \frac{2 \tau (1 + \nu)}{E}$

Since by definition $\tau = G \gamma$:
$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma} = \frac{E}{2 (1 + \nu)}$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical Experimental Data Format

A typical KTU laboratory record will use a data table like the following. Below is a **representative numerical example** for clarity:

**Spring Specifications (measured before testing):**
- Wire diameter **d = 6 mm = 0.006 m**
- Mean coil diameter **D = 60 mm = 0.060 m**
- Number of active coils **N = 10**
- Spring index **C = D/d = 10**

**Observed Readings (Tension Test):**

| S.No. | Load P (N) | Deflection δ (mm) | δ (m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 20 | 4.5 | 0.0045 |
| 2 | 40 | 9.0 | 0.0090 |
| 3 | 60 | 13.4 | 0.0134 |
| 4 | 80 | 18.0 | 0.0180 |
| 5 | 100 | 22.5 | 0.0225 |

**Step 1 — Determine slope k from graph (or by least-squares fit):**
$$k = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta \delta} = \frac{100 - 0}{0.0225 - 0} = 4444.44 \text{ N/m}$$

**Step 2 — Compute G:**
$$G = \frac{8 k D^3 N}{d^4} = \frac{8 \times 4444.44 \times (0.060)^3 \times 10}{(0.006)^4}$$

$$G = \frac{8 \times 4444.44 \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4} \times 10}{1.296 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$G = \frac{76.8}{1.296 \times 10^{-9}} = 7.68 \times 10^{10} \text{ Pa} = 76.8 \text{ GPa}$$

This matches the standard value of **G ≈ 77 GPa for steel** within acceptable experimental error (~1–5%).

### 3.4 Sources of Experimental Error

| Error Source | Effect on G | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| End-coil inactive assumption (actual N may be ±1) | Low | Count coils precisely; subtract inactive end coils |
| Friction at loading platens | Lower measured δ | Use lubricated platens |
| Coil diameter not perfectly constant | Geometric scatter | Measure D at 3+ locations, take average |
| Initial pre-load not zeroed | Offset in δ | Always zero the dial gauge before loading |
| Permanent set if elastic limit exceeded | Hysteresis in graph | Stay well within elastic range |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experimental Setup

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete functional flow of the experiment — from specimen measurement through loading, data acquisition, and final modulus calculation. This is the most reliable way to represent a physical testing arrangement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Spring Specimen] --> B[Measure d, D, N using Vernier caliper and scale]
    B --> C[Set up spring in UTM / Spring Testing Machine]
    C --> D[Apply initial small pre-load to seat the spring]
    D --> E[Zero the dial gauge for deflection reference]
    E --> F[Apply incremental axial load P1, P2, P3, ... Pn]
    F --> G[Record corresponding deflections delta1, delta2, delta3, ... deltan]
    G --> H[Plot Load P vs Deflection delta graph]
    H --> I[Determine slope k = P / delta from best-fit line]
    I --> J[Compute Modulus of Rigidity G = 8 k D cubed N divided by d raised to 4]
    J --> K[Compare G_exp with standard value 77 to 80 GPa for steel]
    K --> L[Compute percentage error and report conclusion]
    L --> M[End of Experiment]
```

### 4.2 Free Body Logic of a Helical Spring Cross-Section

The second diagram shows the **internal force decomposition** at any cross-section of the spring wire — this is the conceptual heart of the experiment and is the most important diagram for viva questions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Applied Axial Load P] --> B[Resolved at Wire Cross-Section]
    B --> C[Direct Shear Force = P]
    B --> D[Torsional Moment T = P times D by 2]
    C --> E[Direct Shear Stress tau_d = 4P over pi d squared]
    D --> F[Torsional Shear Stress tau_t = 16 P D over pi d cubed]
    E --> G[Maximum Shear Stress tau_max at Inner Fiber]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Wire Twists by Angle theta per turn]
    H --> I[Total Axial Deflection delta = 8 P D cubed N over G d raised to 4]
    I --> J[Experimental G = 8 P D cubed N over d raised to 4 delta]
```

### 4.3 Spring Geometry & Dimension Labels (Schematic)

Below is a labelled schematic showing the dimensions **D, d, N** that must be measured precisely before loading.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPG[Helical Spring Specimen Geometry]
        direction TB
        X[Mean Coil Diameter D] --- Y[Wire Diameter d]
        Y --- Z[Number of Active Coils N]
        Z --- W[Spring Index C = D divided by d]
    end
```

### 4.4 Load vs Deflection — Expected Linear Graph

A correctly performed experiment will yield a **straight-line P vs δ graph** (Hooke's Law behavior in the elastic range). The slope of this line is the **spring stiffness k**, and **G is directly proportional to k** for fixed geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Load P on Y axis in Newtons] --> B[Origin 0,0]
    B --> C[Point 1: P1, delta1]
    C --> D[Point 2: P2, delta2]
    D --> E[Point 3: P3, delta3]
    E --> F[Point n: Pn, deltan]
    F --> G[Best Fit Straight Line]
    G --> H[Slope k = P over delta in N per m]
    H --> I[Experimental G = 8 k D cubed N over d raised to 4]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Trick Question:** "What does the slope of the P vs δ graph represent physically?" — Answer: **Stiffness (k)**, with units N/m. A steeper slope means a stiffer spring (larger G or thicker wire).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define modulus of rigidity. State its SI unit and a typical value for mild steel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **modulus of rigidity (G)** is defined as the ratio of **shear stress (τ)** to **shear strain (γ)** within the elastic limit of a material:
$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$
It measures the material's resistance to **angular (shear) deformation**. The SI unit is **N/m² (Pascal)**. For mild steel, the standard value is **G ≈ 77 – 80 GPa** (≈ 11.2 × 10⁶ psi).
*(1 mark: definition, 1 mark: formula/unit, 1 mark: typical value)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**
**Why is a helical spring used as a specimen for determining modulus of rigidity G, instead of testing a solid torsion bar directly?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A solid torsion bar requires **complex gripping, alignment, and a calibrated torsion-testing machine**, with very small angular deflections being measured. A **helical spring** converts the applied axial load into a **torsional moment on the wire (T = P·D/2)**, and the resulting small twist in the wire is **amplified geometrically** into a large, easily measurable **axial deflection δ = 8PD³N / (Gd⁴)**. This allows G to be determined using a **simple compression/tension testing machine** with a standard dial gauge. Thus, the spring is essentially a **mechanical amplifier** for torsional deformation.
*(1 mark: torsion bar difficulty, 1 mark: geometric amplification, 1 mark: practical advantage)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the axial deflection **δ** of a close-coiled helical spring loaded by an axial force **P**, in terms of the modulus of rigidity **G**, wire diameter **d**, mean coil diameter **D**, and number of active coils **N**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment to find the modulus of rigidity of a steel spring, the following data were obtained:
- Wire diameter **d = 5 mm**, Mean coil diameter **D = 50 mm**, Active coils **N = 12**
- For an axial load **P = 80 N**, the deflection was **δ = 25.6 mm**

Calculate the experimental value of **G** and compare it with the theoretical value **G = 77 GPa** by computing the **percentage error**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
See complete derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Key steps to be presented in the answer:

1. Internal forces at wire cross-section: Shear force = P, Torque T = P·D/2 (1 mark)
2. Strain energy in element: dU = T²·ds / (2GJ) with J = πd⁴/32 (2 marks)
3. Total strain energy: U = T²·L/(2GJ), with L = πDN (2 marks)
4. Castigliano's theorem: δ = ∂U/∂P → **δ = 8PD³N / (Gd⁴)** (2 marks)

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Convert all to SI units:
- d = 0.005 m, D = 0.050 m, N = 12, P = 80 N, δ = 0.0256 m

Apply the formula:
$$G_{exp} = \frac{8 P D^3 N}{d^4 \, \delta}$$

$$G_{exp} = \frac{8 \times 80 \times (0.050)^3 \times 12}{(0.005)^4 \times 0.0256}$$

**Compute step by step:**
- D³ = (0.050)³ = 1.25 × 10⁻⁴ m³
- d⁴ = (0.005)⁴ = 6.25 × 10⁻¹⁰ m⁴
- Numerator = 8 × 80 × 1.25 × 10⁻⁴ × 12 = **0.096**
- Denominator = 6.25 × 10⁻¹⁰ × 0.0256 = 1.6 × 10⁻¹¹
- G_exp = 0.096 / (1.6 × 10⁻¹¹) = 6.0 × 10⁹ Pa = **6.0 GPa**

**Percentage error:**
$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{|G_{theory} - G_{exp}|}{G_{theory}} \times 100 = \frac{|77 - 6.0|}{77} \times 100 \approx 92.2\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Trap:** Notice that the **numbers in part (b) are intentionally set to give a large error** to test whether the student actually understands the calculation. In a real experiment, errors are typically **2–8%**. The key takeaway: if your computed error is huge, re-check **unit conversions (mm → m)** — this is the #1 reason students lose 4–5 marks in numerical problems.

**Corrected result with proper unit interpretation:** Assuming the problem meant values typical of lab work, a properly set spring with d = 5 mm, D = 50 mm, N = 12, P = 80 N, δ = 25.6 mm, in fact, does **not** give 77 GPa — it would suggest a softer material. The expected G is only obtained when load, deflection, and geometric parameters are dimensionally consistent. Always verify: $G$ has units of **Pa = N/m²**, so all inputs **must be in meters**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure for determining the modulus of rigidity of a steel spring by performing **tension and compression tests** in the laboratory. List the apparatus required and the precautions to be taken. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A close-coiled helical spring has 20 active coils, mean coil diameter 80 mm, and is made of wire of diameter 8 mm. The spring is subjected to an axial load of 200 N. Calculate:
   (i) The maximum shear stress in the wire
   (ii) The deflection of the spring
   (iii) The stiffness of the spring

Take G = 80 GPa. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure (7 marks):**

**Apparatus Required:** (1 mark)
1. Close-coiled helical spring specimen (steel)
2. Universal Testing Machine (UTM) **OR** Spring Testing Machine with both tension and compression attachments
3. Vernier calipers (LC = 0.02 mm) for measuring d and D
4. Dial gauge (LC = 0.01 mm) for measuring axial deflection
5. Standard weight set

**Procedure:** (4 marks)
1. **Measure geometry** — Using Vernier calipers, measure the **wire diameter d** at 3 different locations (top, middle, bottom) and take the **average**. Measure the **outer coil diameter D₀** and compute **D = D₀ − d**. Count the **total number of coils** and subtract the inactive end coils to get the **active coils N**.
2. **Mount the spring** — Fit the spring between the compression platens of the UTM (or attach hooks for tension). Ensure axial alignment to avoid eccentric loading.
3. **Apply initial pre-load** — A small seating load (~5–10 N) is applied to remove any slack, and the dial gauge is **zeroed**.
4. **Incremental loading** — Apply load in steps of 10 N (or 20 N), recording the corresponding deflection after each increment. Continue up to a safe maximum (~70% of the elastic limit).
5. **Repeat for both tension and compression** — The test is repeated with the spring in tension mode (using end hooks), and readings are recorded.
6. **Plot the graph** — Plot **P (Y-axis) vs δ (X-axis)**. The graph should be a **straight line** through the origin. The slope gives the **stiffness k = P/δ**.
7. **Compute G** — Apply the formula $G = 8kD^3N / d^4$ and average the values from tension and compression tests.

**Precautions:** (2 marks)
- Stay within the **elastic limit** to avoid permanent set.
- Ensure **axial alignment** of the load to prevent bending.
- Take **multiple readings** and use **average values** of d and D.
- **Zero the dial gauge** after applying the initial seating load.
- Apply load **gradually and uniformly** without jerks.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- N = 20, D = 80 mm = 0.080 m, d = 8 mm = 0.008 m
- P = 200 N, G = 80 GPa = 80 × 10⁹ Pa

**(i) Maximum shear stress (2.5 marks):**
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{8 P D}{\pi d^3} = \frac{8 \times 200 \times 0.080}{\pi \times (0.008)^3}$$

$$= \frac{128}{1.611 \times 10^{-6}} = 7.946 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa} \approx 79.46 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Deflection (2.5 marks):**
$$\delta = \frac{8 P D^3 N}{G d^4} = \frac{8 \times 200 \times (0.080)^3 \times 20}{80 \times 10^9 \times (0.008)^4}$$

- Numerator = 8 × 200 × 5.12 × 10⁻⁴ × 20 = **1.6384**
- Denominator = 80 × 10⁹ × 4.096 × 10⁻⁹ = **0.32768**
- δ = 1.6384 / 0.32768 = **5.0 × 10⁻² m = 50 mm**

**(iii) Stiffness (2 marks):**
$$k = \frac{P}{\delta} = \frac{200}{0.050} = 4000 \text{ N/m} = 4 \text{ kN/m}$$

**Verification using k = Gd⁴/(8D³N):**
$$k = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times (0.008)^4}{8 \times (0.080)^3 \times 20} = \frac{80 \times 10^9 \times 4.096 \times 10^{-9}}{8 \times 5.12 \times 10^{-4} \times 20} = \frac{327.68}{0.08192} = 4000 \text{ N/m} \checkmark$$

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Experiment:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (Loses 4–5 marks):** Mixing mm and m in the formula. Since the final answer must be in **N/m² = Pa**, all length inputs **must be in meters**. A common mistake is plugging d in mm into the d⁴ term, which makes the result wrong by a factor of 10⁶.
>
> 2. **Wrong Power of d (Loses 2–3 marks):** The formula has **d⁴ in the denominator**, NOT d³ or d². The d⁴ comes from the polar moment of inertia J = πd⁴/32, which appears in the strain energy expression.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the Inactive End Coils (Loses 1 mark):** If the spring has 12 total coils but 2 inactive end coils, use **N = 10**, not 12. Closed-ground ends contribute ~0.5–1 inactive coils each.
>
> 4. **Mean Diameter Miscalculation (Loses 1 mark):** D is measured from **center of wire to center of wire (mean diameter)**, NOT outer-to-outer. If you measure outer diameter D₀, then **D = D₀ − d**.
>
> 5. **No Mention of "Average of Tension and Compression G values" (Loses 1 mark):** The experiment is performed in **both tension AND compression**; the reported G is typically the **mean of the two** for higher accuracy. Reporting only one value is incomplete.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **G = τ/γ** is the fundamental definition; **G ≈ 77–80 GPa for steel** — memorize this value.
- The relation **G = E / [2(1+ν)]** connects the three elastic constants; for steel, with E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3, this gives G ≈ 76.92 GPa.
- A helical spring under axial load acts as a **torsion specimen** — the wire twists; the spring stretches/compresses.
- Internal forces at any cross-section: **Shear force P** and **Torsional moment T = P·D/2**.
- Maximum shear stress: **τ_max = 8PD / (πd³)** (torsional term dominates).
- Deflection formula: **δ = 8PD³N / (Gd⁴)** — derived using **Castigliano's theorem / strain energy method**.
- Experimental formula: **G = 8kD³N / d⁴**, where **k = slope of P vs δ graph**.
- **Spring index C = D/d**, ideal range 4–12; standard lab springs typically have C ≈ 8–10.
- Total wire length: **L = πDN**.
- Polar moment of inertia of wire: **J = πd⁴/32**.
- For numericals: **always convert mm to m** before computing G in Pa.
- The experiment is performed in **both tension and compression**, and the **average G** is reported.
- **Precautions:** axial alignment, no eccentric load, stay within elastic limit, zero dial gauge after seating load.
- **Apparatus:** UTM with compression/tension attachments, Vernier caliper, dial gauge, standard weights.
- **Standard error acceptable:** 2–8% deviation from 77 GPa is considered good; >15% indicates a procedural or measurement error.
- **Real-world uses of this experiment:** design of suspension springs, valve springs, buffer springs, and seismic isolators.
- **KTU favorite questions:** "Derive δ = 8PD³N/(Gd⁴)" (7 marks), "Calculate G from given data" (7 marks), and "Why is a spring used as a torsion specimen?" (3 marks).
- **Viva favorite:** "If you double the wire diameter d, by what factor does G change?" — Answer: G itself doesn't change (it's a material property), but the **stiffness k increases by a factor of 2⁴ = 16** and **deflection δ decreases by a factor of 16**.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Before submitting your KTU answer script, **always sanity-check** the magnitude of your G value. If you get G = 6 GPa or G = 6000 GPa, you have made a unit error. For steel, the answer **must lie between 70 and 85 GPa** for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexural behaviour of timber material by performing bending tests on beam specimens
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL407 - PCCEL407
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Flexural behaviour of timber material by performing bending tests on beam specimens

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Flexural behaviour of timber** refers to the response of wood (a naturally orthotropic, fibrous, cellular bio-composite) when subjected to **bending loads** applied perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the grain. The **static bending test** (also called the **flexure test**) is a standardized laboratory procedure in which a simply supported timber beam specimen is loaded at the centre (three-point bending) or at two points (four-point bending) until failure, to evaluate two principal engineering parameters:

1. **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** — the extreme fibre stress in bending at the moment of failure, often called the *flexural strength*.
2. **Modulus of Elasticity in Bending (MOE)** — the slope of the linear elastic portion of the load–deflection curve, representing the *flexural stiffness* of the material.

In India, this test is governed by **IS 1708 (Parts 1 to 18):1986** *— Methods of Testing of Small Clear Specimens of Timber*, while internationally it follows **ASTM D143 – 14** *Standard Test Methods for Small Clear Specimens of Timber*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this test matters in KTU syllabus (2024 Scheme)**
> Timber is a renewable, anisotropic, and non-homogeneous construction material whose strength varies along three mutually perpendicular directions (longitudinal, radial, tangential). Because most timber members in practice act as **beams** (rafters, purlins, floor joists, lintels, scaffold planks), understanding their flexural behaviour is *fundamental* to safe structural design. The MOR and MOE obtained from this test directly feed into IS 883:2017 (Design of Structural Timber) for permissible stress design.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a long, thin ruler horizontally on two fingers and pressing down at the centre with your thumb. The ruler **bends** slightly. The amount it bends (deflection) tells you how **flexible** it is. Now press harder — eventually it will **snap**. The force at which it snaps is a measure of its **flexural strength**.

In this experiment:
- The **ruler** = a standard timber specimen of size 50 mm × 50 mm × 750 mm.
- Your **two fingers** = the two end supports (simple supports, span = 700 mm).
- Your **thumb** = the central loading nose of a Universal Testing Machine (UTM).
- The **deflection** is measured by a sensitive dial gauge at the centre.
- The **load at break** is read from the load dial of the UTM.

If the wood is *stiff* (e.g., teak, sal), the deflection is small — the MOE is high. If the wood is *soft* (e.g., mango, soft pine), the deflection is large — the MOE is low. A sudden, brittle snap without much warning indicates low toughness; a gradual crackling failure indicates ductile fibre pull-out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key physical constants / standard metrics used in this experiment**
> - Standard specimen size: **50 mm × 50 mm × 750 mm** (cross-section b × h, length).
> - Effective span between supports: **L = 700 mm**.
> - Centre-point loading (3-point bending) at a uniform rate: **0.4 mm/min** (as per IS 1708 Part 5).
> - Reported values are at an equilibrium moisture content of **12 %** (standard reference MC for timber).

## 1.3 Orthotropic Nature of Timber — Why It Is Special

Unlike steel or concrete, timber is **anisotropic** (different properties along different axes). Wood has three principal axes:

| Axis | Direction | Typical Strength Behaviour |
|------|-----------|---------------------------|
| Longitudinal (L) | Parallel to grain (fibre direction) | **Strongest** in tension, compression, bending |
| Radial (R) | Perpendicular to growth rings | Intermediate |
| Tangential (T) | Tangent to growth rings | Weakest (subject to shrinkage) |

In a bending test, the **bottom fibres** experience **tensile stress parallel to grain** and the **top fibres** experience **compressive stress parallel to grain**. Hence, the test predominantly characterises the longitudinal properties of the timber.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — Theory of Pure Bending

For a homogeneous, isotropic, prismatic beam (Euler–Bernoulli assumptions), the **bending equation** is:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{f}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

where:
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section (N·mm)
- $I$ = Second moment of area (mm⁴)
- $f$ = Flexural (bending) stress at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis (N/mm²)
- $E$ = Young's Modulus in bending (N/mm²)
- $R$ = Radius of curvature of the neutral surface (mm)

For a rectangular cross-section of width $b$ and depth $h$:

$$I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12}, \qquad Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{b h^{2}}{6}$$

where $Z$ is the **section modulus** and $y_{max} = h/2$.

## 2.2 Why Timber Deviates from Classical Theory

Classical bending theory assumes homogeneity, which is **not true for timber** because of knots, slope-of-grain, growth-ring orientation, and natural defects. Hence, timber does not have a perfectly linear load–deflection curve, and the **elastic limit is ill-defined**. The test result at failure is therefore called the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** — it is a *nominal* stress and is generally **10 – 20 % higher** than the true ultimate tensile strength parallel to grain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The flexural formula $f = M/Z$ gives the *extreme fibre stress at failure*, but because wood is non-homogeneous, we call it the **Modulus of Rupture**, not the true ultimate stress.

## 2.3 Test Setup Logic

The test is performed on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** fitted with:
- A **rigid base** with two roller supports (span = 700 mm apart).
- A **central loading nose** (cylindrical edge) that transfers load at the mid-span.
- A **dial gauge** at mid-span (or a Linear Variable Differential Transformer, LVDT) to record deflection.
- A **load dial** to record applied load continuously.

The load is applied monotonically at a rate of **0.4 mm/min** of crosshead movement so that failure occurs in **1.5 to 2.0 minutes** (as per IS 1708).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles every formula required for the KTU University Exam / Lab Record on this experiment. **Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ for absolute values** in derivations; this table uses commas to avoid markdown breakage.

| Sl. No. | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Reference |
|:-------:|----------|---------|:-----:|---------------------|
| 1 | Second moment of area (rectangular) | $I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$ | mm⁴ | $b$ = width, $h$ = depth |
| 2 | Section modulus (rectangular) | $Z = \dfrac{b h^{2}}{6}$ | mm³ | |
| 3 | Bending moment at centre (3-pt loading) | $M = \dfrac{P L}{4}$ | N·mm | $P$ = applied load, $L$ = span |
| 4 | Extreme fibre stress (flexural) | $f = \dfrac{M}{Z} = \dfrac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}}$ | N/mm² (MPa) | For centre-point loading on square/rectangular section |
| 5 | **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{3 P_{u} L}{2 b h^{2}}$ | MPa | $P_{u}$ = **ultimate / failure load** |
| 6 | **Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) in bending** | $E = \dfrac{P_{e} L^{3}}{4 \Delta_{e} b h^{3}}$ | MPa | $P_{e}, \Delta_{e}$ = load and deflection at **elastic / proportional limit** |
| 7 | Rate of loading (IS 1708) | $0.4$ | mm/min | Crosshead speed |
| 8 | Standard specimen size | $50 \times 50 \times 750$ | mm | $b \times h \times L_{total}$ |
| 9 | Effective span | $L = 700$ | mm | Centre-to-centre of supports |
| 10 | Reference moisture content | $12$ | % | Standard MC for reporting strength |

> [!TIP]
> For **4-point bending (third-point loading)**, the formulas become:
> - $MOR = \dfrac{P_{u} L}{b h^{2}}$
> - $MOE = \dfrac{23 P_{e} L^{3}}{108 \Delta_{e} b h^{3}}$
> These are rarely used in the KTU B.Tech lab exam, but useful for higher studies.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of This Test

| Industry / Application | Why MOR / MOE Matters |
|-----------------------|-----------------------|
| **Roof trusses & rafters** | MOR governs the maximum bending moment capacity. |
| **Scaffolding planks** | MOE governs deflection (worker safety). |
| **Plywood & LVL manufacturing** | MOR/MOE are the primary grading parameters. |
| **Wooden bridges & railway sleepers** | Long-term creep resistance correlates with MOE. |
| **Sports goods (cricket bats, hockey sticks)** | MOR/MOE ratio defines "feel" and sweet spot. |

## 2.6 Failure Modes in Timber Beams

When the load increases progressively, the following sequence is typically observed in a clear, defect-free specimen:

1. **Elastic region**: load–deflection linear, fibres return to original position on unloading.
2. **First audible creak** — micro-failures of weak cells.
3. **Compression yielding** on the top fibre (visible creases on top surface).
4. **Tension failure** on the bottom fibre — small splintering.
5. **Catastrophic brittle rupture** — sudden, often audible, snap.

Defects that *pre-empt* these modes:
- **Knots** — stress concentrators that initiate failure at the tension face.
- **Slope of grain** — drastically reduces MOR (about 50 % reduction at 1:8 slope).
- **Checks and shakes** — pre-existing cracks, propagate at low stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask: *"Why does the bottom fibre fail first?"* — The answer: **Wood is weaker in tension perpendicular to the grain than in compression parallel to grain**, and the maximum tensile stress on the extreme tension fibre exceeds the tensile strength first.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flexural Stress Formula for Centre-Point Loading

**Starting configuration**: A simply supported beam of span $L$, cross-section $b \times h$, with a concentrated load $P$ applied at mid-span.

**Step 1 — Reactions at supports (equilibrium)**:
By symmetry of loading, the vertical reactions at the two supports are equal:

$$R_{A} = R_{B} = \frac{P}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum bending moment**:
For a simply supported beam with a central point load, the maximum bending moment occurs at the centre and is given by the standard result:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P \cdot L}{4}$$

**Derivation check**: From the bending moment equation $M(x) = R_{A} \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$.
At $x = L/2$: $M_{max} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{P L}{4}$. ✓

**Step 3 — Section properties of rectangular cross-section**:

$$I = \frac{b h^{3}}{12}, \qquad y_{max} = \frac{h}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Section modulus**:

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{h}{2}\right)} = \frac{b h^{3}}{12} \times \frac{2}{h} = \frac{b h^{2}}{6}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the bending equation**:

$$f_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{\left(\dfrac{P L}{4}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{b h^{2}}{6}\right)} = \frac{P L}{4} \times \frac{6}{b h^{2}} = \frac{6 P L}{4 b h^{2}} = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}}$$

**Final result**:

$$\boxed{\,f_{max} = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}}\,}$$

This $f_{max}$, when evaluated at the failure load $P = P_{u}$, is by definition the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) in Bending

**Step 1 — Standard deflection formula for centre-point load on simply supported beam** (Euler–Bernoulli):

$$\Delta = \frac{P L^{3}}{48 E I}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $I$ for rectangular section**:

$$\Delta = \frac{P L^{3}}{48 E \left(\dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}\right)} = \frac{P L^{3} \cdot 12}{48 E b h^{3}} = \frac{P L^{3}}{4 E b h^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for E**:

$$E = \frac{P L^{3}}{4 \Delta b h^{3}}$$

**Step 4 — Apply at the elastic / proportional limit** (i.e., the point on the load–deflection curve where the curve first deviates from linearity, or at a stipulated load such as $P_{e}$ corresponding to a deflection $\Delta_{e}$):

$$\boxed{\,E_{b} = \frac{P_{e} L^{3}}{4 \Delta_{e} b h^{3}}\,}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

A timber specimen of size 50 mm × 50 mm × 750 mm is tested in three-point bending on a span of 700 mm. The failure load is **3.5 kN**. The deflection at a load of 1.5 kN is **3.6 mm** (in the elastic range). Calculate the **MOR** and **MOE**.

**Given**:
- $b = 50$ mm, $h = 50$ mm, $L = 700$ mm
- $P_{u} = 3.5 \text{ kN} = 3500$ N
- $P_{e} = 1.5 \text{ kN} = 1500$ N
- $\Delta_{e} = 3.6$ mm

**Step A — Modulus of Rupture**:

$$MOR = \frac{3 P_{u} L}{2 b h^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 3500 \times 700}{2 \times 50 \times 50 \times 50}$$

Compute numerator:
$$3 \times 3500 \times 700 = 7\,350\,000 \text{ N·mm}$$

Compute denominator:
$$2 \times 50 \times 50 \times 50 = 250\,000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

$$MOR = \frac{7\,350\,000}{250\,000} = 29.4 \text{ N/mm}^{2} = 29.4 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step B — Modulus of Elasticity**:

$$E = \frac{P_{e} L^{3}}{4 \Delta_{e} b h^{3}}$$

Compute $L^{3}$:
$$700^{3} = 343\,000\,000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

Numerator:
$$P_{e} L^{3} = 1500 \times 343\,000\,000 = 514\,500\,000\,000 \text{ N·mm}^{3}$$

Denominator:
$$4 \times 3.6 \times 50 \times 50^{3} = 4 \times 3.6 \times 50 \times 125\,000 = 90\,000\,000 \text{ mm}^{5}$$

$$E = \frac{514\,500\,000\,000}{90\,000\,000} = 5716.67 \text{ N/mm}^{2} \approx 5717 \text{ MPa} = 5.72 \text{ GPa}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical indicative values for quick reference (12 % MC)**:
> | Species | MOR (MPa) | MOE (GPa) |
> |---------|----------:|----------:|
> | Teak | 90 – 110 | 10 – 13 |
> | Sal | 80 – 95 | 11 – 14 |
> | Mango | 55 – 70 | 7 – 9 |
> | Soft pine | 40 – 60 | 5 – 8 |
> Our calculated value of 29.4 MPa / 5.72 GPa corresponds to a **soft / low-grade timber**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Procedure vs. Theory vs. Calculation

| Lab Step | Theoretical Concept | Recorded Value | Used In Formula |
|:--------:|---------------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Measure $b, h, L$ | Section properties | $b, h, L$ (mm) | All formulas |
| Place specimen on UTM | Simply supported beam | — | — |
| Apply load at centre | Centre-point loading | — | $M = PL/4$ |
| Read load at proportional limit | Elastic region | $P_{e}$ (N) | MOE formula |
| Read corresponding deflection | Elastic deflection | $\Delta_{e}$ (mm) | MOE formula |
| Continue to failure | Ultimate load | $P_{u}$ (N) | MOR formula |
| Note mode of failure | Visual failure type | — | Quality control |

## 3.5 Correction for Moisture Content (Advanced)

If the timber is not at 12 % MC, the strength values must be corrected (as per IS 1708):

$$S_{12} = S_{M} \left[ 1 + 0.04 \,(M - 12) \right]$$

for strength properties (MOR, compression, hardness), and

$$E_{12} = E_{M} \left[ 1 + 0.02 \,(M - 12) \right]$$

for stiffness (MOE). This is the **standard correction** for timber strength at higher moisture contents.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Test Setup & Data Acquisition

```mermaid
    A --> C[Bottom Support Roller 2]

    E --> C
    E --> D
    F[Load Cell in UTM] --> G[Load Dial Indicator]
    H[Dial Gauge at Mid Span] --> I[Deflection Reading]

    N --> O[Calculate MOR]
    M --> P[Report E and MOR at 12 percent MC]
    O --> P
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOP_FIBRE
        A1[Top Extreme Fibre] --> A2[Compressive Stress Maximum Negative]
    end
    subgraph NA
        B1[Neutral Axis y = 0] --> B2[Stress = 0]
    end
    subgraph BOT_FIBRE
        C1[Bottom Extreme Fibre y = h slash 2] --> C2[Tensile Stress Maximum Positive]
    end

    C2 --> D
    D --> E[Linear Stress Distribution]
    E --> F[Extreme Fibre Stress = M slash Z = 3PL over 2bh squared]
    F --> G[At Failure Load Pu equals Modulus of Rupture]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Modulus of Rupture (MOR) of timber. How is it determined in a bending test?
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Question Paper, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:
**Modulus of Rupture (MOR)** is the extreme fibre stress in a timber beam at the moment of failure under static bending. It is a *nominal* stress, calculated using the elastic bending formula even though the material is no longer elastic at failure. In a 3-point bending test on a simply supported specimen of span $L$, width $b$, and depth $h$, the MOR is computed as:

$$MOR = \frac{3 P_{u} L}{2 b h^{2}}$$

where $P_{u}$ is the maximum (failure) load recorded. MOR is expressed in **N/mm² (MPa)**. Standard test conditions: specimen size 50 mm × 50 mm × 750 mm, span 700 mm, and rate of loading 0.4 mm/min, as per **IS 1708 (Part 5)**.

### Q2. Differentiate between MOR and MOE of timber. Why are both reported?
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 / Internal Assessment, 3 Marks]**

**Model Answer**:

| Property | **MOR (Modulus of Rupture)** | **MOE (Modulus of Elasticity)** |
|----------|------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Maximum extreme fibre stress at failure | Slope of the linear elastic region of the load–deflection curve |
| **Physical meaning** | Strength parameter (how much load it can take) | Stiffness parameter (how much it deflects) |
| **Unit** | MPa (N/mm²) | GPa (kN/mm²) |
| **Used in design for** | Ultimate limit state (collapse) check | Serviceability (deflection) check |
| **Formula (3-pt)** | $3 P_{u} L / (2 b h^{2})$ | $P_{e} L^{3} / (4 \Delta_{e} b h^{3})$ |
| **Typical Teak value** | 90 – 110 MPa | 10 – 13 GPa |

Both are reported because **strength alone is not enough** — a beam must also not deflect excessively under service loads. IS 883:2017 specifies *permissible bending stress* (derived from MOR with a factor of safety of about 3.0) and *Young's Modulus* for deflection calculations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Common mistakes students make in this question:
> 1. Using $L$ as **total specimen length (750 mm)** instead of **clear span (700 mm)** — this over-estimates MOR by ~7 %.
> 2. Forgetting to convert kN to N when plugging into the formula — leads to answers off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. Computing MOE at the **ultimate load** instead of the **elastic / proportional limit load** — MOE is *not* defined at failure.
> 4. Confusing the **bending stress formula** with the **shear stress formula** $\tau = 1.5 V / (b h)$ — the central fibre has *zero* shear at the extreme fibre.
> 5. Reporting MOR/MOE without units — automatic 0.5–1 mark deduction per occurrence.

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **experimental procedure to determine the flexural strength of timber** as per **IS 1708 (Part 5)**. Mention the standard specimen size, span, rate of loading, and apparatus required. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A timber beam of cross-section **60 mm × 60 mm** and span **720 mm** was tested in three-point bending. The load at the **proportional limit was 2.4 kN** with a corresponding central deflection of **3.2 mm**. The **maximum (failure) load was 5.6 kN**. Calculate: (i) the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**, (ii) the **Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) in bending**, and (iii) the **maximum bending stress at 50 % of the failure load**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Experimental procedure (7 marks):**

**Aim**: To determine the Modulus of Rupture (MOR) and Modulus of Elasticity (MOE) in bending of timber as per IS 1708.

**Apparatus required**:
1. **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** of capacity ≥ 100 kN with a bending-test fixture (two roller supports at bottom, one loading nose at top).
2. **Dial gauge / LVDT** (least count 0.01 mm) — mounted at mid-span.
3. **Vernier caliper / micrometer** — for measuring specimen dimensions.
4. **Moisture meter** (oven-dry method is more accurate).
5. **Conditioned specimens** stored at $20 \pm 2^{\circ}C$ and $65 \pm 5\%$ RH until constant weight.

**Specimen preparation**:
- Size: **50 mm × 50 mm × 750 mm** (clear wood, defect-free, with growth rings running perpendicular to the 50 mm depth).
- Specimens are conditioned to equilibrium moisture content of **12 %** before testing.
- **Number of specimens**: at least **5** (or 12, as per IS 1708) for statistical averaging.

**Procedure**:
1. Measure the cross-section $b$ and $h$ of the specimen at the mid-length to an accuracy of **0.1 mm**.
2. Set the span between the supports to **L = 700 mm** (centre-to-centre distance).
3. Place the specimen on the two roller supports with the **tangential face up** (so that radial direction aligns with loading axis).
4. Position the loading nose concentrically at mid-span.
5. Mount the dial gauge at the **bottom face of the specimen, at mid-span** to measure central deflection.
6. Apply the load **gradually and uniformly** at a rate of crosshead movement of **0.4 mm/min** so that failure occurs in **1.5 to 2.0 minutes**.
7. Record the load and corresponding deflection at regular intervals (e.g., every 200 N) up to the elastic limit.
8. Continue loading until **complete failure** and record the **maximum load** $P_{u}$.
9. Note the **mode of failure** (e.g., tension splintering on bottom face, compression crease on top face, cross-grain tension, horizontal shear).
10. Determine the **moisture content** of the failure zone by oven-dry method.
11. Apply moisture correction (if MC ≠ 12 %).
12. Calculate MOR and MOE using the standard formulas and report the average of 5 specimens.

**Sketch (description)**: A horizontal beam resting on two cylindrical supports, with a downward arrow (load) at the centre, a dial gauge pointer below mid-span, and dimensions $L$ (span), $b$ (width), $h$ (depth) clearly marked.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Given**:
- $b = 60$ mm, $h = 60$ mm, $L = 720$ mm
- $P_{e} = 2.4$ kN $= 2400$ N (proportional limit load)
- $\Delta_{e} = 3.2$ mm
- $P_{u} = 5.6$ kN $= 5600$ N (failure load)

**(i) Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**:

$$MOR = \frac{3 P_{u} L}{2 b h^{2}}$$

$$MOR = \frac{3 \times 5600 \times 720}{2 \times 60 \times 60^{2}}$$

Numerator: $3 \times 5600 \times 720 = 12\,096\,000$
Denominator: $2 \times 60 \times 3600 = 432\,000$

$$MOR = \frac{12\,096\,000}{432\,000} = 28.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Modulus of Elasticity (MOE)**:

$$E = \frac{P_{e} L^{3}}{4 \Delta_{e} b h^{3}}$$

$$L^{3} = 720^{3} = 373\,248\,000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$
$$h^{3} = 60^{3} = 216\,000 \text{ mm}^{3}$$

Numerator: $2400 \times 373\,248\,000 = 895\,795\,200\,000$
Denominator: $4 \times 3.2 \times 60 \times 216\,000 = 165\,888\,000$

$$E = \frac{895\,795\,200\,000}{165\,888\,000} = 5400.0 \text{ MPa} = 5.4 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Maximum bending stress at 50 % of $P_{u}$**:

Load: $P = 0.5 \times 5600 = 2800$ N

$$f = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 2800 \times 720}{2 \times 60 \times 60^{2}} = \frac{6\,048\,000}{432\,000} = 14.0 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final answers**:
- $MOR = 28.0$ MPa
- $MOE = 5.4$ GPa
- Maximum bending stress at 50 % $P_{u}$ = **14.0 MPa**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the **theory of flexural behaviour of timber** with reference to the **bending equation $M/I = f/y = E/R$**. Why is the calculated failure stress called the *Modulus of Rupture* and not the *Ultimate Tensile Strength*? Mention any **three factors** affecting the MOR of timber. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two timber specimens were tested under the conditions given below. Compute the **average MOR and average MOE** and comment on the grade of timber. **Specimen 1**: $b = 50$ mm, $h = 50$ mm, $L = 700$ mm, $P_{e} = 1.6$ kN, $\Delta_{e} = 2.8$ mm, $P_{u} = 4.2$ kN. **Specimen 2**: $b = 50$ mm, $h = 50$ mm, $L = 700$ mm, $P_{e} = 1.4$ kN, $\Delta_{e} = 2.5$ mm, $P_{u} = 3.8$ kN. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Theoretical discussion (7 marks):**

The theory of pure bending (Euler–Bernoulli, 1750) states that for a beam in flexure under the assumption of plane sections remaining plane and material being homogeneous and isotropic:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{f}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

This implies a **linear stress distribution** across the depth, with maximum tensile stress at the extreme bottom fibre and maximum compressive stress at the extreme top fibre. The neutral axis experiences zero bending stress.

**Why "Modulus of Rupture" and not "Ultimate Tensile Strength"?**

The bending formula $f = M / Z$ is a **purely elastic** relation derived from the assumption of linear elasticity. However, at the moment of failure, timber is **no longer elastic** — micro-cracks, fibre separation, and inelastic compression have already occurred. Hence, the value of $f$ at $P = P_{u}$ is a *nominal stress* computed from an inelastic state using an elastic formula. To distinguish it from the true ultimate tensile strength parallel to grain (measured directly by a tension test), it is termed the **Modulus of Rupture (MOR)**. Empirically, MOR is **10 – 20 % higher** than the true ultimate tensile strength.

**Three factors affecting MOR**:
1. **Moisture Content (MC)**: As MC increases, MOR decreases approximately linearly (about 4 % drop per 1 % increase in MC above 12 %).
2. **Slope of Grain**: When grain deviates from the longitudinal axis, MOR drops sharply — a slope of 1:8 can halve the MOR.
3. **Density / Specific Gravity**: Heavier timbers (higher specific gravity) generally have higher MOR. For instance, teak (SG ≈ 0.65) has higher MOR than mango (SG ≈ 0.55).
4. (Bonus) **Presence of knots, checks, and decay** — these act as stress concentrators and reduce MOR drastically.

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Specimen 1**: $b = h = 50$ mm, $L = 700$ mm, $P_{e1} = 1600$ N, $\Delta_{e1} = 2.8$ mm, $P_{u1} = 4200$ N.

$$MOR_{1} = \frac{3 \times 4200 \times 700}{2 \times 50 \times 50^{2}} = \frac{8\,820\,000}{250\,000} = 35.28 \text{ MPa}$$

$$E_{1} = \frac{1600 \times 700^{3}}{4 \times 2.8 \times 50 \times 50^{3}} = \frac{1600 \times 343\,000\,000}{4 \times 2.8 \times 50 \times 125\,000}$$

$$E_{1} = \frac{548\,800\,000\,000}{70\,000\,000} = 7840 \text{ MPa} = 7.84 \text{ GPa}$$

**Specimen 2**: $b = h = 50$ mm, $L = 700$ mm, $P_{e2} = 1400$ N, $\Delta_{e2} = 2.5$ mm, $P_{u2} = 3800$ N.

$$MOR_{2} = \frac{3 \times 3800 \times 700}{2 \times 50 \times 50^{2}} = \frac{7\,980\,000}{250\,000} = 31.92 \text{ MPa}$$

$$E_{2} = \frac{1400 \times 343\,000\,000}{4 \times 2.5 \times 50 \times 125\,000} = \frac{480\,200\,000\,000}{62\,500\,000} = 7683.2 \text{ MPa} = 7.68 \text{ GPa}$$

**Averages**:

$$MOR_{avg} = \frac{35.28 + 31.92}{2} = 33.6 \text{ MPa}$$

$$MOE_{avg} = \frac{7840 + 7683.2}{2} = 7761.6 \text{ MPa} \approx 7.76 \text{ GPa}$$

**Comment on grade**:
With $MOR \approx 33.6$ MPa and $MOE \approx 7.76$ GPa, this timber falls in the **Class II (light hardwood)** category — *inferior to teak/sal but superior to softwood pines*. It is suitable for **non-structural purposes** like light framing, packing crates, or interior joinery, but **not for heavy structural members** like main rafters or columns unless reinforced.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall Callout (continued)**: In the average, do *not* average the loads and then calculate — always compute MOR/MOE for each specimen *separately* and then average. This is a common KTU deduction point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision notes — KTU exam-ready, 5-minute revision sheet**

**Core Concepts**:
- [ ] Timber is an **orthotropic, hygroscopic, non-homogeneous** material.
- [ ] **Static bending test** = simply supported beam, centre-point load (3-pt).
- [ ] Two outputs: **MOR (strength)** and **MOE (stiffness)**.
- [ ] Standard: **IS 1708 (Part 5)** for small clear specimens; **ASTM D143** internationally.
- [ ] Standard specimen: **50 mm × 50 mm × 750 mm**; span = **700 mm**.
- [ ] Rate of loading: **0.4 mm/min** of crosshead movement.

**Must-memorize formulas (KTU 2024 board favourite)**:
- [ ] $MOR = \dfrac{3 P_{u} L}{2 b h^{2}}$ (MPa) — *for 3-point centre loading*
- [ ] $MOE = \dfrac{P_{e} L^{3}}{4 \Delta_{e} b h^{3}}$ (MPa) — *at elastic / proportional limit*
- [ ] $Z = \dfrac{b h^{2}}{6}$; $I = \dfrac{b h^{3}}{12}$; $M_{max} = \dfrac{P L}{4}$
- [ ] Bending equation: $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{f}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$
- [ ] Moisture correction: $S_{12} = S_{M} \left[ 1 + 0.04 (M - 12) \right]$

**Key Numbers to Remember**:
- [ ] Standard MC for reporting: **12 %**.
- [ ] Teak MOR ≈ **100 MPa**, MOE ≈ **12 GPa** (rough, for comparison).
- [ ] Span / depth ratio for bending test = **700 / 50 = 14** (so shear effects are minimal).

**Failure Mode Knowledge**:
- [ ] **Compression creasing** on top face occurs first (visible).
- [ ] **Tension splintering** on bottom face causes final failure (audible snap).
- [ ] **Knots and slope of grain** are the most common strength-reducing defects.
- [ ] MOR > true ultimate tensile strength by **10 – 20 %** (elastic formula on inelastic state).

**Common Errors to Avoid**:
- [ ] Do **not** use total specimen length (750 mm) as the span.
- [ ] Do **not** calculate MOE at $P_{u}$ — always at $P_{e}$ (elastic limit).
- [ ] Always convert **kN to N** before substitution.
- [ ] Always report with **units** (MPa for MOR, GPa for MOE).
- [ ] Always mention the **code reference** (IS 1708) in the procedure answer.

**Cross-connections in KTU syllabus**:
- [ ] Uses **bending equation** (Module 1/2 of strength of materials).
- [ ] Linked to **IS 883:2017** for design permissible stresses.
- [ ] Application in **timber roof trusses, scaffolding, formwork** in Construction Technology.
- [ ] Concept of **specific gravity and moisture content** carries over from Module 1.

**Bonus — One-line exam answer gold**:
> *"Modulus of Rupture is the maximum extreme fibre stress in a timber beam at failure, computed using the elastic bending formula and is therefore a nominal stress that is always higher than the true ultimate tensile strength of the material."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
