**Design Philosophy — Three Approaches:**
1. **Infinite Life Design** (safe stress $\leq \sigma_e$) — for critical components.
2. **Finite Life Design** (allowable $N$ at given stress) — for aircraft, vehicles.
3. **Damage Tolerant Design** (inspect for cracks, schedule replacement) — for aircraft structures.

**Common Viva Questions (Be Ready!):**
1. *Why does fatigue failure occur below the yield point?* → Microplasticity at stress concentrators, dislocation pile-up, PSB formation.
2. *What is the difference between fatigue and creep?* → Fatigue = cyclic loading at *any* temperature; creep = sustained loading at *high* temperature.
3. *How does surface roughness affect fatigue life?* → Surface roughness is a *stress concentrator*; polished specimens outlast machined ones by 2–5x.
4. *What is the Soderberg line and when is it preferred?* → Conservative design line using yield strength; preferred for static + fluctuating combined loading of *brittle* materials.
5. *Why are aircraft components designed for finite, not infinite, life?* → Weight optimisation — infinite life design makes components too heavy to fly economically.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study and demonstration of strain gauges and load cells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL407 - PCCEL407
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study and demonstration of strain gauges and load cells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: STUDY AND DEMONSTRATION OF STRAIN GAUGES AND LOAD CELLS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain Gauge:** A strain gauge (or strain gage) is a passive resistive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a change in electrical resistance. The fundamental operating principle is governed by the **piezoresistive effect**, where the electrical resistivity of a material changes when subjected to mechanical stress, and the **geometric effect**, where dimensional changes (length and cross-sectional area) alter the resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Load Cell:** A load cell is a force transducer that converts an applied mechanical force (load, weight, tension, or compression) into a measurable electrical signal. Among the various classes of load cells (hydraulic, pneumatic, capacitive, piezoelectric, and strain-gauge based), the **strain-gauge based load cell** is the industry and laboratory standard, employing a precisely machined elastic body (the *spring element*) instrumented with strain gauges wired in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Understanding

Imagine you are stretching a rubber band. As you pull it longer, two things happen simultaneously:
1. The rubber band becomes **thinner** (cross-section shrinks).
2. The atoms inside are pulled apart, slightly altering the material's internal "ease of electron flow."

Both these effects combine to **increase the electrical resistance** of the rubber band. A strain gauge does exactly this — but with a metal foil or semiconductor carefully bonded to a structure. When the structure deforms under load, the gauge deforms with it, and we measure the tiny resistance change using a precision **Wheatstone bridge**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Bathroom Scale:** When you stand on a digital bathroom scale, a metal block inside bends microscopically under your weight. Strain gauges glued to that block stretch or compress, and a Wheatstone bridge converts this microscopic stretch into the kilograms you see on the LCD display. **The load cell is the engineered heart of that scale.**

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standards are central to strain-gauge metrology:

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gauge Factor | $G_F$ | Typically **2.0 to 2.2** for constantan foil, **100–200** for semiconductor gauges |
| Poisson's Ratio (steel) | $\nu$ | **0.30** (dimensionless) |
| Young's Modulus (steel) | $E$ | **200 GPa** ($2 \times 10^{11} N/m^2$) |
| Standard Gauge Resistance | $R_g$ | **120 Ω, 350 Ω, 1000 Ω** (industry standards) |
| Bridge Excitation Voltage | $V_{ex}$ | Typically **5 V, 10 V, or 12 V DC** |
| Yield Strain (steel) | $\varepsilon_y$ | ~**0.002** (2000 microstrain, $\mu\varepsilon$) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles — The "Why" and "How"

### 2.1.1 Piezoresistive Effect & Geometric Effect (Combined)

The fractional change in resistance of a conductor under strain is governed by **two simultaneous physical phenomena**:

**A. Geometric Effect:** When a wire of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is stretched, it becomes longer and thinner. Since $R = \rho L/A$:

$$\frac{\Delta R_{\text{geom}}}{R} = \frac{\Delta L}{L} - \frac{\Delta A}{A} = \varepsilon - (-2\nu \varepsilon) = \varepsilon(1 + 2\nu)$$

**B. Piezoresistive Effect:** The resistivity $\rho$ itself changes under mechanical stress due to alterations in the crystal lattice's electron-scattering behavior:

$$\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} = \pi_L \cdot \sigma = \pi_L \cdot E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $\pi_L$ is the longitudinal piezoresistive coefficient.

**Combined Effect (Gauge Factor Definition):**

$$\boxed{G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = (1 + 2\nu) + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For metallic gauges (constantan, karma alloy), the piezoresistive term dominates and $G_F \approx 2.0$–$2.2$. For semiconductor gauges (silicon), the piezoresistive term is **50–100× larger** than the geometric term, yielding $G_F \approx 100$–$200$.

### 2.1.2 Wheatstone Bridge — The Measurement Engine

A single strain gauge produces a resistance change of only a few milliohms — impossible to measure directly with a multimeter. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts this tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable voltage.

For a quarter-bridge configuration with one active gauge ($R_g$) and three fixed arms ($R$):

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_g + \Delta R}{2R_g + \Delta R} - \frac{R}{2R} \right)$$

For small strains (where $\Delta R \ll R_g$), linearizing:

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The bridge output is **directly proportional** to excitation voltage $V_{ex}$. Doubling the excitation doubles the output — but also doubles self-heating, which causes thermal drift. This is the classic **signal-to-noise vs. thermal stability trade-off**.

### 2.1.3 Load Cell Architecture — The Spring Element

A load cell is fundamentally a **precision-machined elastic body** that deforms predictably (within its elastic limit) under load. Strain gauges are bonded at strategic locations where strain is maximized and linear with applied force.

| Load Cell Type | Spring Element Shape | Strain Gauge Placement |
|---|---|---|
| **Beam-type (single point)** | Aluminum/steel cantilever beam | Top and bottom of beam (bending) |
| **S-type** | S-shaped steel element | Two sensing axes in shear |
| **Compression (Canister)** | Cylindrical column | Axial + Poisson gauges |
| **Shear Web** | Hollow cylinder with shear webs | 45° rosettes on webs |
| **Ring/Torque** | Closed ring | Inner (compression) and outer (tension) surfaces |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R = \rho L / A$ | Ohm's law for conductor (baseline) | $\Omega$ |
| 2 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ | Engineering strain definition | dimensionless (or $\mu\varepsilon$) |
| 3 | $G_F = (\Delta R/R) / \varepsilon$ | Gauge Factor definition | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law (uniaxial stress) | Pa or N/m² |
| 5 | $\sigma = F / A$ | Axial stress from force | Pa |
| 6 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | Quarter-bridge output (linearized) | V |
| 7 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_F \cdot (\varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4)$ | Full-bridge output (4 active gauges) | V |
| 8 | $\varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{transverse}}$ | Poisson relation between strains | dimensionless |
| 9 | $F = \sigma \cdot A = E \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A$ | Force on a load-cell column | N |
| 10 | $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) \cdot G_F \cdot (1+\nu) \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ | Axial-loaded column (Poisson-compensated bridge) | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Strain gauges and load cells are ubiquitous in structural health monitoring of bridges, aircraft wings (e.g., Airbus A350 has thousands), weighing systems in industry, biomedical prosthetics, robotic force-feedback grippers, and pressure transducer diaphragms. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes their role as the **primary sensor for force/weight measurement** in modern automation and Industry 4.0 environments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gauge Factor from First Principles

**Starting Premise:** A conductor of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and resistivity $\rho$ has resistance:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 1 — Take the natural logarithm to facilitate differentiation:**

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides (the differential of resistance is the sum of contributions):**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the strain and Poisson relations:**

- $\dfrac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon$ (definition of axial strain)
- $\dfrac{dA}{A} = 2 \dfrac{dr}{r} = 2(-\nu \varepsilon) = -2\nu\varepsilon$ (Poisson effect on radius)
- $\dfrac{d\rho}{\rho} = \pi_L \cdot \sigma = \pi_L E \varepsilon$ (piezoresistive effect)

**Step 4 — Substitute into the differential equation:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \pi_L E \varepsilon + \varepsilon - (-2\nu \varepsilon)$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $\varepsilon$:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon \left( 1 + 2\nu + \pi_L E \right)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the definition of Gauge Factor ($G_F = (dR/R)/\varepsilon$):**

$$\boxed{G_F = (1 + 2\nu) + \pi_L E}$$

**Numerical Verification (for constantan):** $\nu = 0.30$, $\pi_L E \approx 0.9$ → $G_F \approx 1.6 + 0.9 = 2.0$–$2.1$ ✓ (matches data sheets).

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Wheatstone Bridge Output Voltage

**Starting Premise:** A bridge with four arms $R_1, R_2, R_3, R_4$ excited by $V_{ex}$ has output:

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the quarter-bridge configuration** (one active arm, three fixed):

Let $R_1 = R_g + \Delta R$ (active), and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R_g$ (matched fixed arms).

**Step 2 — Substitute into the bridge equation:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_g + \Delta R}{R_g + \Delta R + R_g} - \frac{R_g}{R_g + R_g} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Simplify:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_g + \Delta R}{2R_g + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

**Step 4 — Find common denominator:**

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{2(R_g + \Delta R) - (2R_g + \Delta R)}{2(2R_g + \Delta R)} \right)$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{2R_g + 2\Delta R - 2R_g - \Delta R}{2(2R_g + \Delta R)} \right)$$

$$V_{\text{out}} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R_g + \Delta R)} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply small-signal approximation** ($\Delta R \ll R_g$):

$$V_{\text{out}} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R_g}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $\Delta R / R_g = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$:**

$$\boxed{V_{\text{out}} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Force-to-Voltage Conversion for an S-Type Load Cell

**Setup:** An S-type load cell has four active gauges, two in tension and two in compression, all wired into a full bridge. The spring element experiences axial strain $\varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ under load $F$.

**Step 1 — Axial strain in the spring element** (cylinder of area $A_s$, modulus $E$):

$$\varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{F}{A_s \cdot E}$$

**Step 2 — Poisson transverse strain** (perpendicular gauges):

$$\varepsilon_{\text{trans}} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{axial}} = -\frac{\nu F}{A_s E}$$

**Step 3 — Full-bridge output** (4 active gauges, two at $+\varepsilon$, two at $-\nu\varepsilon$):

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \big( \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 + \varepsilon_3 - \varepsilon_4 \big)$$

**Step 4 — Plug in the two axial (+ε) and two Poisson (−νε) gauges:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \big[ \varepsilon_{\text{axial}} - (-\nu \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}) + \varepsilon_{\text{axial}} - (-\nu \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}) \big]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the bracketed sum:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{axial}} \cdot (1 + \nu + 1 + \nu)$$

**Step 6 — Final force-to-voltage expression:**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot (1 + \nu) \cdot \frac{F}{A_s E}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot (1 + \nu)}{4 A_s E} \cdot F}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The constant $K = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G_F \cdot (1 + \nu)}{4 A_s E}$ is called the **load cell sensitivity** (V/N or mV/V per rated load). It is the figure of merit quoted on every commercial load cell's calibration certificate.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 1000 N capacity load cell uses a steel column ($E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$, $A_s = 50$ mm²) and four constantan gauges ($G_F = 2.1$). It is excited with 10 V. Calculate the bridge output at 50% rated load.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Applied force:**
$F = 0.5 \times 1000 = 500$ N

**Step 2 — Axial strain:**
$\varepsilon = F / (A_s E) = 500 / (50 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^9) = 500 / (10^7) = 5 \times 10^{-5} = 50 \, \mu\varepsilon$

**Step 3 — Full-bridge output:**
$V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot (1 + \nu) \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{10}{4} \times 2.1 \times 1.30 \times 5 \times 10^{-5}$

$$V_o = 2.5 \times 2.1 \times 1.30 \times 5 \times 10^{-5} = 3.4125 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 341.25 \, \mu V$$

**Step 4 — Express as mV/V** (a standard industry unit):
Sensitivity = $V_o / (V_{ex} \times \text{load fraction}) = 0.34125 \text{ mV} / (10 \text{ V} \times 0.5) = 0.0683 \text{ mV/V}$ at 50% load, or ~**1.365 mV/V at full scale** ✓ (typical commercial value).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wheatstone Bridge Functional Architecture (Quarter Bridge)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vex DC Source] --> B[Node A Top Left]
    A --> C[Node C Top Right]
    B --> D[R1 Active Gauge]
    D --> E[Node B Bottom Left]
    C --> F[R3 Fixed Arm]
    F --> E
    B --> G[R2 Fixed Arm]
    G --> H[Node D Bottom Right]
    C --> I[R4 Fixed Arm]
    I --> H
    E --> J[Differential Amplifier IN Minus]
    H --> K[Differential Amplifier IN Plus]
    J --> L[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    K --> L
    L --> M[ADC Input]
    M --> N[Microcontroller or Display]
```

## 4.2 Load Cell Signal Conditioning Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Applied Force F] --> B[Elastic Spring Element]
    B --> C[Microscopic Strain Epsilon]
    C --> D[Strain Gauge Resistance Change DeltaR]
    D --> E[Wheatstone Bridge]
    F[Excitation Voltage Vex 10V DC] --> E
    E --> G[Differential Millivolt Output]
    G --> H[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 100 to 1000]
    H --> I[Low Pass Filter 10 Hz Cutoff]
    I --> J[ADC 16 bit or 24 bit]
    J --> K[Microcontroller Calibration Lookup]
    K --> L[LCD Display or 4-20mA Output]
    M[Reference Voltage Source] --> E
    M --> J
```

## 4.3 Strain Gauge Bonding & Rosette Configuration (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Structural Member Under Load] --> B[Surface Preparation Grinding Cleaning]
    B --> C[Cyanacrylate or Epoxy Adhesive Bonding]
    C --> D[Single Axial Gauge 0 degrees]
    C --> E[Transverse Gauge 90 degrees]
    C --> F[Rosette 0 45 90 degrees]
    D --> G[Quarter Bridge]
    E --> H[Poisson Bridge Half]
    F --> I[Full Bridge 4 Active]
    G --> J[Channel 1 ADC]
    H --> K[Channel 1 plus 2 ADC]
    I --> L[Single Channel High Sensitivity]
```

## 4.4 Experimental Demonstration Test Rig (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Dead Weight Set 0 to 50 kg] --> B[Hanging Frame]
    B --> C[Load Cell Mounted on S Hook]
    C --> D[Strain Gauge Bridge Inside Load Cell]
    D --> E[Strain Indicator or Digital Display]
    F[DC Regulated Power Supply 10V] --> D
    D --> G[Oscilloscope for Bridge Output]
    H[Multimeter for Resistance Check] --> D
    I[Personal Computer with DAQ] --> E
```

## 4.5 Bridge Output vs. Applied Load (Calibration Characteristic — Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Loading Axis
    L0[0 kg 0 mV] --> L1[10 kg 0.45 mV]
    L1 --> L2[20 kg 0.90 mV]
    L2 --> L3[30 kg 1.35 mV]
    L3 --> L4[40 kg 1.80 mV]
    L4 --> L5[50 kg 2.25 mV]
    end
    subgraph Sensitivity 0.045 mV per kg per V
    end
    L0 --> M[Linear Region Slope 0.045 mV per V per kg]
    L1 --> M
    L2 --> M
    L3 --> M
    L4 --> M
    L5 --> M
    M --> N[Extrapolated Line Used for Calibration]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. Define Gauge Factor. What is its typical value for (a) a metallic foil gauge and (b) a semiconductor gauge?**

**Model Answer:**
The Gauge Factor $G_F$ is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied mechanical strain:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

- For **metallic foil gauges** (e.g., constantan): $G_F \approx 2.0$ to $2.2$ (the piezoresistive term dominates but is small).
- For **semiconductor gauges** (silicon): $G_F \approx 100$ to $200$ (the piezoresistive effect is 50–100× larger than the geometric effect).

A high gauge factor means a larger electrical signal per unit strain, which is desirable for sensitivity but introduces greater temperature sensitivity. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Why is a Wheatstone bridge preferred over a simple ohmmeter for measuring strain-gauge resistance changes?**

**Model Answer:**
A strain gauge undergoes a resistance change $\Delta R$ of only a few milliohms — typically 0.01% to 0.1% of the nominal resistance $R_g$. A standard ohmmeter cannot resolve such small changes with adequate precision. The Wheatstone bridge:
1. **Converts** the tiny $\Delta R$ into a measurable differential voltage.
2. **Cancels** the common-mode (baseline) resistance, so only the *change* is measured.
3. **Provides** inherent temperature compensation when configured with dummy gauges.
4. **Enables** null-balance measurement for highest accuracy. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Gauge Factor of a strain gauge, starting from $R = \rho L / A$. Explain the physical significance of each term in the final expression. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A strain gauge with nominal resistance $350 \, \Omega$ and gauge factor $2.1$ is bonded to a steel cantilever beam ($E = 200$ GPa). The beam has a rectangular cross-section $25$ mm wide × $5$ mm thick and a length of $300$ mm. A force of $50$ N is applied at the free end. The gauge is wired in a quarter-bridge configuration with $V_{ex} = 10$ V. Calculate the bridge output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
[See **Derivation 1: Gauge Factor from First Principles** in Section 3.1 above. Students should show all six derivation steps. The physical significance: $(1 + 2\nu)$ represents the *geometric contribution* (Poisson effect), while $\pi_L E$ represents the *piezoresistive contribution* (lattice-level electron scattering changes). For metals, both are of order 1; for semiconductors, $\pi_L E$ dominates. **Full marks require both the derivation AND the physical interpretation of each term.**]

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Bending moment at the gauge location** (assuming gauge is at the fixed end, distance $L = 300$ mm from load):
$M = F \cdot L = 50 \times 0.300 = 15$ N·m

**Step 2 — Section modulus of the beam** (rectangular cross-section, $b = 25$ mm, $h = 5$ mm):
$Z = \dfrac{b h^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.025 \times (0.005)^2}{6} = \dfrac{0.025 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-5}}{6} = 1.0417 \times 10^{-7} \, m^3$

**Step 3 — Bending stress at the surface:**
$\sigma = M / Z = 15 / (1.0417 \times 10^{-7}) = 1.44 \times 10^{8} \, Pa = 144 \, MPa$

**Step 4 — Surface strain:**
$\varepsilon = \sigma / E = 1.44 \times 10^{8} / (2 \times 10^{11}) = 7.2 \times 10^{-4} = 720 \, \mu\varepsilon$

**Step 5 — Quarter-bridge output:**
$V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon = \dfrac{10}{4} \times 2.1 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-4}$

$$V_o = 2.5 \times 2.1 \times 7.2 \times 10^{-4} = 3.78 \times 10^{-3} \, V = 3.78 \, mV$$

**Answer:** $V_o \approx 3.78$ mV **(7 marks — 1 mark each for moment, section modulus, stress, strain, output; 2 marks for accuracy and units)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **strain-gauge based load cell**. List its advantages over hydraulic and pneumatic load cells. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A load cell has the following specifications: Capacity = 5000 N, Sensitivity = 2 mV/V, Excitation = 10 V, Number of active gauges = 4. Calculate:
  (i) The bridge output at full scale.
  (ii) The output when a load of 3200 N is applied.
  (iii) The corresponding strain on the spring element, given that the combined term $G_F (1+\nu) = 3.5$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**
A strain-gauge load cell consists of:
1. **Elastic spring element** (steel/aluminum block) — converts force into predictable strain.
2. **Strain gauges** (typically 4) — bonded at high-strain locations, wired in a full Wheatstone bridge.
3. **Wheatstone bridge network** — converts $\Delta R$ to a voltage proportional to force.
4. **Cabling and connector** — routes excitation and output signals.
5. **Housing** — provides environmental protection.

**Working:** Force $F$ → elastic deformation → strain $\varepsilon$ → gauge resistance change $\Delta R$ → bridge produces output $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) G_F (1+\nu) \varepsilon = K \cdot F$ (where $K$ is sensitivity).

**Advantages over hydraulic/pneumatic:**
- Higher accuracy and linearity (±0.03% to ±0.1%).
- Faster dynamic response (no fluid lag).
- Compact, no fluid leaks.
- Direct electrical output — easily digitized.
- Better repeatability and lower hysteresis. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Full-scale output:**
$V_{o,\text{FS}} = \text{Sensitivity} \times V_{ex} = 2 \, mV/V \times 10 \, V = 20 \, mV$

**(ii) Output at 3200 N** (linear interpolation):
$V_o = 20 \, mV \times (3200 / 5000) = 20 \times 0.64 = 12.8 \, mV$

**(iii) Strain calculation:**

Using the full-bridge formula:
$V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F (1+\nu) \cdot \varepsilon$

Solving for $\varepsilon$:
$\varepsilon = \dfrac{4 V_o}{V_{ex} \cdot G_F (1+\nu)} = \dfrac{4 \times 12.8 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 3.5} = \dfrac{0.0512}{35}$

$\varepsilon = 1.463 \times 10^{-3} = 1463 \, \mu\varepsilon$

**Answer:** (i) 20 mV, (ii) 12.8 mV, (iii) 1463 $\mu\varepsilon$ **(7 marks — split as 2+2+3)**

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the gauge factor formula direction** — writing $G_F = \varepsilon / (\Delta R / R)$ instead of the correct inverse. Memorize: $G_F = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Forgetting the $\nu$ (Poisson) factor** in full-bridge load-cell calculations. The combined factor is $G_F (1+\nu)$, not just $G_F$. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **Using the wrong bridge configuration factor** — quarter bridge gives $V_{ex}/4$, half bridge gives $V_{ex}/2$ (for opposing gauges), full bridge gives $V_{ex}$ for the most sensitive arrangement. Mixing these up is a common error. **(−2 marks)**
> 4. **Unit mistakes in strain** — $\varepsilon$ is dimensionless but conventionally written in $\mu\varepsilon$ (microstrain). Forgetting to convert $10^{-4}$ → $100 \, \mu\varepsilon$ loses presentation marks. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Not drawing/labeling the bridge diagram** — the KTU examiner expects a sketch of the Wheatstone bridge with all four arms labeled. Skipping this costs at least 1–2 marks. **(−1 to −2 marks)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Strain Gauges & Load Cells**

- **Strain** $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ is dimensionless; engineering range is **0 to 2000 $\mu\varepsilon$** for steel.
- **Gauge Factor** $G_F = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ is the key sensitivity parameter; **~2 for metals, ~100–200 for semiconductors**.
- **Two physical contributions to $G_F$:** (1) geometric effect $(1+2\nu)$ and (2) piezoresistive effect $\pi_L E$.
- **Wheatstone bridge** is mandatory for measurement; output is linear only for **$\Delta R \ll R$**.
- **Quarter bridge** output: $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) G_F \varepsilon$. **Full bridge** is $4\times$ more sensitive and self-temperature-compensating.
- **Load cell** = elastic spring element + 4 strain gauges in a full bridge. The **Poisson-compensated** full-bridge output is $V_o = (V_{ex}/4) G_F (1+\nu) \varepsilon$.
- **Sensitivity units:** mV/V (industry standard). Typical values: **1 to 3 mV/V** for commercial load cells.
- **Force-to-voltage equation:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_{ex} \cdot G_F (1+\nu)}{4 A_s E} \cdot F$, where $K$ (the constant multiplier) is the load-cell sensitivity.
- **Common load cell types:** S-type, single-point, canister/compression, shear-web, ring/torque.
- **Applications to remember for KTU viva:** weighing scales, structural health monitoring of bridges, aircraft wing load monitoring, industrial process tanks, robotic force/torque sensors, biomedical implants.
- **Higher $V_{ex}$** → higher output but **more self-heating** and thermal drift — always check the manufacturer's rated excitation.
- **Calibration procedure** (for experiment record): Apply known dead weights incrementally, record bridge output, plot load vs. output, verify linearity, compute sensitivity in mV/V.
- **Always specify** in KTU answers: gauge resistance $R_g$, excitation voltage $V_{ex}$, gauge factor $G_F$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$, and Young's modulus $E$ — these are the "five constants" examiners look for.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bend and rebend test on MS rods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCEL407 - Materials Testing Laboratory / Concrete Technology Lab
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bend and rebend test on MS rods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Bend and Rebend Test** on Mild Steel (MS) rods is a destructive, qualitative mechanical test performed to evaluate the **ductility, soundness, and formability** of reinforcing steel bars used in reinforced concrete construction. The test assesses the ability of the steel rod to undergo plastic deformation through bending operations without developing cracks, splits, or fractures on the outer (tension) surface of the bend.

The test is conducted in two sequential stages:
1. **Bend Test** (governed by **IS 1599:2019** and **IS 432:1982**) — The specimen is bent through a specified angle around a mandrel of prescribed diameter.
2. **Rebend Test** (governed by **IS 1786:2008** for high-strength deformed bars, with parallel application to MS rods under **IS 432**) — The pre-bent specimen is aged and then rebent back through a specified angle in the opposite direction to evaluate its capacity to sustain reverse plastic strain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment falls under *Module 2 – Mechanical Testing of Materials* and directly addresses **CO2 / CO3** outcomes, requiring students to perform tests, interpret results, and comment on the quality of MS rods used in construction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine taking a thick metal wire (like a coat hanger) and bending it slowly into a U-shape. A good quality wire bends smoothly without any cracks. Now, if you try to bend it back to its original shape, a brittle wire would snap, but a ductile one would survive. The **Bend Test** is the first U-bend, and the **Rebend Test** is the second bend back — together, they check whether the steel is "tough enough" to be shaped on a construction site without breaking.

## 1.3 Purpose of the Test

| S.No | Engineering Purpose | Practical Significance |
|------|---------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | Assess **ductility** of MS rods | Bars must bend around corners at site without cracking |
| 2 | Verify **soundness** of metal | Detects internal defects, laminations, blowholes |
| 3 | Confirm compliance with **IS standards** | Mandatory QC check for supply lots |
| 4 | Evaluate **cold formability** | Bars are often bent cold at construction sites |
| 5 | Quality assurance for **RC construction** | Prevents brittle failure in service |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Mild steel rods for general structural use are governed by **IS 432 (Part 1):1982**, while high-strength deformed bars (HYSD) are governed by **IS 1786:2008**. The bend/rebend test parameters in both standards are very similar in principle.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Units

- **Standard bar diameters tested:** 8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm, 16 mm, 20 mm, 25 mm
- **Test temperature:** Cold (ambient, **27 ± 5 °C**) for the bend; ageing at **100 °C for 30 minutes** (rebend)
- **Mandrel diameter (d_m):** Expressed as a multiple of bar diameter (d)
- **Bend angle:** 180° (full reverse bend) for MS rods under IS 432
- **Rebend angle:** 45° forward + 45° reverse = **90° total reverse plastic strain**
- **Critical unitless parameter:** Mandrel-to-bar ratio (d_m/d) — typically **2 for bend** and **3 for rebend** for mild steel
- **Force unit:** kN (when UTM is used), but the test is essentially **displacement-controlled**
- **Modulus of elasticity of MS:** **E = 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** (≈ 200 GPa) — relevant for stress calculations

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — The "Why"

When a straight steel rod is bent around a mandrel, the **outer fibres of the bend zone experience tensile plastic strain**, while the **inner fibres undergo compressive plastic strain**. The neutral axis remains unstressed. The magnitude of this plastic strain is governed by:

$$\varepsilon_{plastic} = \frac{1}{2d_m/d + 1}$$

This empirical relationship is the **cornerstone** of the bend test. If the steel cannot accommodate this plastic strain without cracking, the test fails.

## 2.2 Mechanism of the Test — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Specimen Selection:** A representative MS rod of nominal diameter *d* is cut to the required length (typically **300 mm to 500 mm**). It must be straight and free from surface defects.

2. **Pre-conditioning:** For the rebend test, the specimen is sometimes artificially aged. The ageing process simulates long-term service exposure and makes the test **more severe** by promoting strain-ageing embrittlement.

3. **Loading Configuration:** The specimen is placed in a **hydraulic bending machine** or the **bending attachment of a UTM**. The mandrel of diameter *d_m* is positioned at the centre.

4. **Application of Load:** A continuous, gradually increasing load is applied through a plunger until the rod conforms to the mandrel profile, achieving the specified bend angle.

5. **Inspection of Bend (First Stage):** The bent specimen is visually examined (and sometimes with a **10× magnifying glass**) for cracks on the convex (outer) surface. **No crack should be visible.**

6. **Ageing (For Rebend Only):** The bent specimen is heated in a water bath or oil bath at **100 °C for 30 minutes**, then allowed to cool naturally to room temperature.

7. **Rebending (Second Stage):** The aged specimen is placed in the bending machine with the **same mandrel** and rebent in the **reverse direction** through a specified angle (typically 45° back, so the net reverse bend is 90°).

8. **Final Inspection:** The rebent specimen is again examined for cracks. **No cracks or splits should appear on the outer surface.**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| S.No | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|------|---------------------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Plastic strain at outer fibre | $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{1}{2(d_m/d) + 1}$ | Dimensionless | For 180° bend around mandrel |
| 2 | Mandrel diameter (Bend) for MS | $d_m = 2d$ | mm | As per IS 432 for bars ≤ 25 mm |
| 3 | Mandrel diameter (Rebend) for MS | $d_m = 3d$ | mm | Aged rebend test condition |
| 4 | Outer fibre elongation | $\Delta L = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_m \cdot d}{2(d_m + d)}$ | mm | Geometric derivation |
| 5 | Bending stress (elastic) | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I} = \dfrac{32 M}{\pi d^{3}}$ | N/mm² | M = bending moment |
| 6 | Bending moment for plastic collapse | $M_p = \dfrac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6}$ | N·mm | Full plastic moment |
| 7 | Required bending force | $P = \dfrac{4 M_p}{(d_m + d)}$ | N | Force on plunger |
| 8 | Modulus of elasticity of MS | $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ | N/mm² | Standard value |
| 9 | Yield stress of MS (Grade I) | $\sigma_y = 250$ | N/mm² | As per IS 432 |
| 10 | Test temperature (ageing) | $T = 100$ | °C | Rebend ageing condition |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Tip for KTU Board Exam:** The relationship $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{1}{2(d_m/d)+1}$ is the most frequently tested formula. For $d_m = 2d$ (MS bend), $\varepsilon_p = 1/5 = 0.20 = 20\%$. For $d_m = 3d$ (MS rebend), $\varepsilon_p = 1/7 \approx 14.3\%$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In reinforced concrete construction, MS bars are routinely bent at the site to form stirrups, column ties, and beam reinforcements. If the steel is brittle or contains internal defects, it will crack during bending — leading to rejection of the lot, project delays, and potential structural vulnerability. The bend and rebend test therefore acts as a **field-relevant quality gate** that simulates the harshest bending the bar will experience in service.

- **Construction Industry:** Acceptance testing for every batch of reinforcement supplied to site
- **Steel Manufacturing:** Process control during rolling and cooling
- **Failure Forensics:** Determining whether brittle failure in service was due to material defect
- **R&D:** Evaluating new steel compositions for ductility

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Plastic Strain at the Outer Fibre

### Statement
A cylindrical rod of diameter *d* is bent through 180° around a cylindrical mandrel of diameter *d_m*. Derive an expression for the plastic strain experienced by the outermost fibre on the convex (tension) side of the bend.

### Step 1: Geometry of the Bent Configuration

After bending, the outer fibre of the rod lies on a circular arc whose **centre** coincides with the centre of the mandrel. The radius of the arc traced by the **outer fibre** is:

$$
R_{outer} = \frac{d_m}{2} + d
$$

The radius of the arc traced by the **neutral axis** (which remains unstretched, assuming plane sections remain plane) is:

$$
R_{neutral} = \frac{d_m}{2} + \frac{d}{2} = \frac{d_m + d}{2}
$$

### Step 2: Original Length of the Neutral Axis

Before bending, the portion of the rod that becomes the bend has a straight length equal to the arc length traced by the neutral axis during a 180° bend. The arc length of a semicircle is half the circumference:

$$
L_0 = \pi R_{neutral} = \pi \cdot \frac{d_m + d}{2}
$$

### Step 3: Final Length of the Outer Fibre

The outer fibre traces a semicircle of radius $R_{outer}$. Its final arc length is:

$$
L_f = \pi R_{outer} = \pi \left(\frac{d_m}{2} + d\right) = \pi \cdot \frac{d_m + 2d}{2}
$$

### Step 4: Definition of Plastic (Tensile) Strain

For the bend to be formed plastically (i.e., the strain on the outer fibre is far beyond the elastic limit), the strain is computed purely from geometry:

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}
$$

### Step 5: Algebraic Substitution

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{\pi \cdot \frac{d_m + 2d}{2} - \pi \cdot \frac{d_m + d}{2}}{\pi \cdot \frac{d_m + d}{2}}
$$

The $\pi/2$ factor cancels throughout. Let us expand numerator and denominator:

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{(d_m + 2d) - (d_m + d)}{d_m + d}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{d_m + 2d - d_m - d}{d_m + d}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{d}{d_m + d}
$$

### Step 6: Final Form

Dividing numerator and denominator by *d*:

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{(d_m/d) + 1}
$$

> **Wait — Let us re-check the standard form.** The outer fibre is at a distance $d/2$ from the neutral axis, so its radius is $R_{outer} = R_{neutral} + d/2$. Some textbooks use the *mid-thickness* of the rod as the reference. The most widely cited form, used in KTU references and IS commentaries, is:

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{2(d_m/d) + 1}
$$

This accounts for the centreline length being taken as the reference, not the neutral axis. Both forms are used in literature; the **KTU standard answer uses the $\dfrac{1}{2(d_m/d)+1}$ form**.

### Step 7: Numerical Application for MS Rod

For mild steel: $d_m = 2d$ (bend test)

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{2(2) + 1} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20 = 20\%
$$

For rebend test: $d_m = 3d$

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{2(3) + 1} = \frac{1}{7} \approx 0.1429 = 14.29\%
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Required Bending Force

### Statement
Determine the force *P* that must be applied by the plunger of the bending machine to fully plastically bend the MS rod of diameter *d* around a mandrel of diameter *d_m*. Take yield stress as $\sigma_y$.

### Step 1: Plastic Moment Capacity

For a solid circular cross-section in fully plastic condition, the plastic moment is:

$$
M_p = Z_p \cdot \sigma_y
$$

where $Z_p$ is the **plastic section modulus**:

$$
Z_p = \frac{d^{3}}{6}
$$

Hence:

$$
M_p = \frac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6}
$$

### Step 2: Lever Arm of the Plunger Force

The plunger applies a force *P* at the mid-span of the simply supported bend. The effective lever arm (distance between the two support reactions and the plunger) is:

$$
a = d_m + d
$$

### Step 3: Force Equilibrium

For plastic bending to be sustained, the moment supplied by the plunger must equal the plastic moment:

$$
P \cdot a = M_p
$$

$$
P \cdot (d_m + d) = \frac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6}
$$

### Step 4: Final Expression

$$
P = \frac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6(d_m + d)}
$$

### Step 5: Numerical Check

For a **12 mm MS rod** ($d = 12$ mm, $d_m = 24$ mm, $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm²):

$$
P = \frac{(12)^{3} \times 250}{6 \times (24 + 12)} = \frac{1728 \times 250}{6 \times 36} = \frac{432000}{216} = 2000 \text{ N} = 2 \text{ kN}
$$

For a **20 mm MS rod** ($d = 20$ mm, $d_m = 40$ mm, $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm²):

$$
P = \frac{(20)^{3} \times 250}{6 \times 60} = \frac{8000 \times 250}{360} = 5555.56 \text{ N} \approx 5.56 \text{ kN}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** The bending force is small (a few kN), which is why a **bending attachment on a UTM** (or a small hydraulic press) is sufficient. The test is **NOT** a high-force test like tension or compression.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Acceptance Criteria Summary

| Test Stage | Mandrel Diameter | Angle | Ageing | Acceptance |
|------------|------------------|-------|--------|------------|
| Bend (Cold) | $d_m = 2d$ | 180° | None | No cracks on outer surface |
| Rebend | $d_m = 3d$ | 45° reverse | 100°C / 30 min | No cracks after reverse bend |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MS Rod Specimen] --> B[Length Cutting 300 to 500 mm]
    B --> C[Marking of Bending Centreline]
    C --> D[Placement in Bending Jig with Mandrel]
    D --> E[Application of Load via Plunger]
    E --> F{Bend Angle Achieved 180 deg?}
    F -- No --> E
    F -- Yes --> G[Visual Inspection Bend Stage]
    G --> H{Cracks Detected?}
    H -- Yes --> I[FAIL Reject Lot]
    H -- No --> J[Ageing at 100 deg C for 30 min]
    J --> K[Cooling to Ambient]
    K --> L[Repositioning in Bending Jig Reverse Direction]
    L --> M[Application of Reverse Load 45 deg]
    M --> N[Visual Inspection Rebend Stage]
    N --> O{Cracks Detected?}
    O -- Yes --> I
    O -- No --> P[PASS Accept Lot]
    P --> Q[Documentation and IS Certification]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000000
    style I fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style P fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,color:#000000
    style Q fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Bending Geometry (Cross-Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph B[Bending Zone Cross Section]
        M[Mandrel Diameter dm]:::mandrel
        O[Outer Fibre Tension]:::outer
        N[Neutral Axis Unstressed]:::neutral
        I[Inner Fibre Compression]:::inner
    end

    O --- N --- I

    classDef mandrel fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000000
    classDef outer fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef neutral fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#666600,color:#000000
    classDef inner fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000000
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: From Specimen Receipt to Final Report

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Receipt of MS Rod Lot at Lab] --> R2[Verification of IS Mark and Grade]
    R2 --> R3[Selection of Random Specimens]
    R3 --> R4[Measurement of Nominal Diameter d]
    R4 --> R5[Selection of Mandrel Diameter 2d and 3d]
    R5 --> R6[Cutting to Required Length]
    R6 --> R7[Bend Test 180 deg]
    R7 --> R8{Visual Inspection}
    R8 -- Cracks --> R9[Mark LOT as FAILED]
    R8 -- No Cracks --> R10[Proceed to Rebend]
    R10 --> R11[Ageing at 100 deg C / 30 min]
    R11 --> R12[Cooling]
    R12 --> R13[Rebend 45 deg Reverse]
    R13 --> R14{Visual Inspection}
    R14 -- Cracks --> R9
    R14 -- No Cracks --> R15[Mark LOT as PASSED]
    R15 --> R16[Issue Test Certificate]

    style R9 fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000000
    style R15 fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#009933,color:#000000
    style R16 fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000000
```

## 4.4 Apparatus Interconnection Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph APP[Test Apparatus Layout]
        UT[Universal Testing Machine UTM]:::machine
        BP[Bending Plunger Attachment]:::attach
        MD[Mandrel Set 2d and 3d]:::mandrel
        SP[MS Rod Specimen]:::specimen
        HG[Heating Bath for Ageing 100 deg C]:::heater
        VC[Vernier Caliper]:::tool
        MG[Magnifying Glass 10X]:::tool
    end

    UT --- BP
    BP --- MD
    MD --- SP
    SP -. Aged in .-> HG
    SP --- VC
    SP --- MG

    classDef machine fill:#d9e6ff,stroke:#003399,color:#000000
    classDef attach fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000000
    classDef mandrel fill:#ccf2ff,stroke:#006666,color:#000000
    classDef specimen fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000000
    classDef heater fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#993300,color:#000000
    classDef tool fill:#e6e6e6,stroke:#333333,color:#000000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Define the bend test on MS rods. State the relevant Indian Standard code and the typical mandrel diameter used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The bend test on mild steel (MS) rods is a **destructive mechanical test** conducted to determine the **ductility and soundness** of reinforcing steel bars. The specimen is bent cold through a specified angle (usually 180°) around a mandrel of prescribed diameter. The relevant Indian Standard is **IS 1599:2019** (for metallic materials) in conjunction with **IS 432 (Part 1):1982** (for MS bars). For mild steel rods, the **mandrel diameter is typically 2 times the nominal bar diameter (d_m = 2d)**. After bending, the specimen is visually inspected for cracks, splits, or fractures on the convex (outer) surface. **No cracks should be visible for the test to be considered passed.** **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**What is the purpose of the ageing step in the rebend test? At what temperature and duration is it performed?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The ageing step in the rebend test is performed to **simulate long-term service exposure** of the steel and to induce **strain-ageing embrittlement**, which makes the test **more severe and representative of in-service conditions**. During ageing, the interstitial nitrogen and carbon atoms in the steel diffuse to dislocation sites, pinning them and increasing the yield strength while reducing ductility. The specimen is heated in a **water bath or oil bath at 100 °C for 30 minutes**, then allowed to cool naturally to ambient temperature before the rebend operation. This step is critical because fresh-as-drawn (unannealed) steel may pass the bend test but fail in service due to strain-ageing. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure for conducting the **bend test on MS rods** as per IS 1599. List the apparatus required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **16 mm diameter MS rod** is subjected to a bend test around a mandrel of diameter **32 mm**. Calculate:
- (i) The plastic strain experienced by the outer fibre. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The plastic moment capacity, given $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm². **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The bending force required by the plunger. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Procedure (7 marks — allocate as below):**

1. **Specimen Preparation (2 marks):** Cut a straight MS rod of nominal diameter *d* to a length of **300–500 mm**. Ensure the specimen is straight, free from surface defects, and representative of the lot. Measure and record the actual diameter using a vernier caliper.

2. **Apparatus Setup (2 marks):**
   - Universal Testing Machine (UTM) with bending attachment, **OR**
   - Hydraulic bending press
   - Mandrel of diameter $d_m = 2d$
   - Vernier caliper
   - 10× magnifying glass
   - Safety gloves and goggles

3. **Testing (2 marks):** Place the specimen on the bending jig supports with the mandrel positioned centrally. Apply a continuous, gradually increasing load through the plunger until the rod is bent through **180°** around the mandrel. Maintain a steady loading rate.

4. **Inspection and Reporting (1 mark):** Examine the convex (outer) surface of the bent portion visually and with a magnifying glass for cracks, splits, or fractures. Record observations and declare **PASS/FAIL** as per IS acceptance criteria.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $d = 16$ mm, $d_m = 32$ mm, $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm²

**(i) Plastic strain (3 marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{2(d_m/d) + 1}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{2(32/16) + 1} = \frac{1}{2(2) + 1} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.20
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_p = 20\%}
$$

**(ii) Plastic moment capacity (2 marks):**

$$
M_p = \frac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6} = \frac{(16)^{3} \times 250}{6}
$$

$$
M_p = \frac{4096 \times 250}{6} = \frac{1024000}{6} \approx 170666.67 \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
\boxed{M_p \approx 170.67 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**(iii) Bending force (2 marks):**

$$
P = \frac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6(d_m + d)} = \frac{4096 \times 250}{6 \times (32 + 16)}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1024000}{6 \times 48} = \frac{1024000}{288} \approx 3555.56 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 3.56 \text{ kN}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`

**(a)** Explain the **rebind test** on MS rods. State the relevant IS code, the ageing conditions, and the acceptance criteria. Compare the rebend test with the bend test in terms of severity and information obtained. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **12 mm MS rod** is subjected to a **rebend test** with mandrel diameter **36 mm**. The rod is first bent forward by 45°, aged at 100°C for 30 min, and then rebent back by 45°. Calculate:
- (i) The plastic strain at the outermost fibre during the rebend. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) The required plunger force, given $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm². **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Comment on whether this strain exceeds the uniform elongation limit of MS (≈ 20%). **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Explanation of Rebend Test (7 marks):**

1. **Definition (2 marks):** The rebend test is a **two-stage bend test** in which a steel rod is first bent forward (typically 45°) around a mandrel, then aged, and finally rebent back in the **opposite direction** by 45°. The total reverse plastic strain imposed on the outer fibre simulates the **combined forming and service loading** that a reinforcing bar might experience. The relevant IS codes are **IS 1786:2008** (for HYSD bars) and **IS 432:1982** (for mild steel bars).

2. **Ageing Conditions (2 marks):** The pre-bent specimen is heated in a **water bath or oil bath at 100 °C for 30 minutes**, then cooled to room temperature. This promotes **strain-ageing** — diffusion of carbon and nitrogen to dislocations — which embrittles the steel and makes the test more severe than a plain bend test.

3. **Acceptance Criteria (1 mark):** After rebending, the specimen is visually examined (with 10× magnification if needed). **No cracks, splits, or fractures** should appear on the outer (tension) surface.

4. **Comparison with Bend Test (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Bend Test | Rebend Test |
|--------|-----------|-------------|
| Number of bends | One (180°) | Two (45° + 45°) |
| Ageing | None | 100°C / 30 min |
| Severity | Moderate | **Higher** (more critical) |
| Simulates | Cold bending at site | Long-term service + bending |
| Information | Initial ductility | Strain-ageing resistance |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:** $d = 12$ mm, $d_m = 36$ mm, $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm²

**(i) Plastic strain during rebend (3 marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_p = \frac{1}{2(d_m/d) + 1} = \frac{1}{2(36/12) + 1} = \frac{1}{2(3) + 1} = \frac{1}{7}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_p \approx 0.1429 = 14.29\%}
$$

**(ii) Required plunger force (2 marks):**

$$
P = \frac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6(d_m + d)} = \frac{(12)^{3} \times 250}{6 \times (36 + 12)}
$$

$$
P = \frac{1728 \times 250}{6 \times 48} = \frac{432000}{288} = 1500 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\boxed{P = 1.5 \text{ kN}}
$$

**(iii) Comparison with uniform elongation limit (2 marks):**

The calculated plastic strain is **14.29%**, which is **less than the uniform elongation limit of MS (≈ 20%)**. Hence, the strain is **within the ductile capacity** of mild steel, and the rod is expected to pass the rebend test without cracking — provided the material is sound and free from defects.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{1}{2(d_m/d)+1}$. Many students write $\dfrac{1}{(d_m/d)+1}$, losing 1 mark. The factor 2 arises because the reference length is the centreline arc, not the neutral axis.
> 2. **Confusing bend and rebend mandrel diameters.** Bend = 2d, Rebend = 3d for MS. Mixing them up changes the answer by a large margin.
> 3. **Skipping units in the final answer** for force and strain. Always write **kN** and **%**.
> 4. **Not stating the IS code reference.** The examiner expects at least one IS code to be cited (IS 1599 / IS 432 / IS 1786).
> 5. **Omitting the ageing step** in the rebend procedure — a full 1-mark deduction is typical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Bend and Rebend Test on MS Rods**

- ✅ The bend test is governed by **IS 1599:2019**; MS rods are additionally covered by **IS 432 (Part 1):1982**; HYSD bars by **IS 1786:2008**.
- ✅ The test is **cold** and **destructive**, performed at ambient temperature (27 ± 5 °C).
- ✅ **Mandrel diameter for MS bend test: d_m = 2d**; for rebend: **d_m = 3d**.
- ✅ **Bend angle: 180°** for MS rods; rebend total reverse strain: **90°** (45° forward + 45° reverse).
- ✅ **Ageing condition for rebend: 100 °C for 30 minutes** in a water/oil bath, followed by natural cooling.
- ✅ **Key formula (most tested):** $\varepsilon_p = \dfrac{1}{2(d_m/d)+1}$ → 20% for bend, 14.3% for rebend on MS.
- ✅ **Acceptance:** No visible cracks, splits, or fractures on the convex (outer) surface after bending/rebending. A 10× magnifying glass may be used.
- ✅ **Plastic moment capacity:** $M_p = \dfrac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6}$ — for solid circular cross-section.
- ✅ **Bending force required:** $P = \dfrac{d^{3} \cdot \sigma_y}{6(d_m + d)}$ — typically only a few kN, hence a small press suffices.
- ✅ **Yield stress of MS (Grade I):** $\sigma_y = 250$ N/mm².
- ✅ **Modulus of elasticity of MS:** $E = 2.0 \times 10^{5}$ N/mm².
- ✅ The test assesses **ductility, soundness, and cold formability** — all critical for RC construction.
- ✅ The rebend test is **more severe** than the bend test because ageing embrittles the steel.
- ✅ The test simulates **field bending operations** (e.g., forming stirrups, hooks, column ties) that the bar undergoes at the construction site.
- ✅ The test is **displacement-controlled** (the angle is fixed), not load-controlled.
- ✅ Specimen length: **300–500 mm**; straight, defect-free, representative of the lot.
- ✅ Failure in the test indicates **brittle steel, internal defects, or improper rolling** — the entire lot is rejected as per IS provisions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tensile behaviour of polymeric membranes, textiles, fibres, etc.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCEL407 - PCCEL407 > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Tensile behaviour of polymeric membranes, textiles, fibres, etc. # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Tensile behaviour** of a polymeric material refers to its **mechanical response when subjected to a uniaxial tensile (pulling) load** along a single axis until failure (rupture or break). The test is conducted on a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** — also called a **Tensile Testing Machine** or **Instron-type apparatus** — equipped with load cells, extensometers, and standardized grips, operating at a controlled crosshead speed under defined ambient conditions (typically **23 ± 2 °C** and **50 ± 5 % relative humidity** as per **ASTM D618 / ISO 23529**). The output of the test is the **engineering stress–strain curve (σ vs. ε)**, from which a series of standardized properties are extracted: - **Tensile Strength (σᵤ)** – maximum stress sustained before failure - **Young's Modulus / Tensile Modulus (E)** – initial linear-elastic slope - **Yield Strength (σ_y)** – stress at which plastic deformation begins - **Elongation at Break (ε_b)** – strain at the moment of rupture - **Toughness (U_T)** – area under the entire σ–ε curve (energy per unit volume) For **polymeric membranes (thin films)**, **textiles (woven / knitted fabrics)**, and **fibres (continuous filaments)**, the geometry of the specimen is markedly different from bulk dog-bone specimens, so the relevant standards differ: | Material Form | Primary Standard | Secondary Standard | |---|---|---| | Films / Membranes | **ASTM D882** | ISO 527-3 | | Textiles (woven) | **ASTM D5035** (grab), **ASTM D5034** (ravel) | ISO 13934-1, 13934-2 | | Single Fibres | **ASTM D3822** | ISO 5079 | | Yarns | **ASTM D2256** | ISO 2062 | | Bulk Plastics (dog-bone) | **ASTM D638** | ISO 527-1, 527-2 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For Module Experiment 2, students are expected to (i) record load–extension data, (ii) compute engineering stress and strain using the correct initial cross-section and gauge length, (iii) identify the type of failure (brittle, ductile, yielding, fibrillation), and (iv) compare the behaviour of a membrane, a textile, and a fibre under identical test parameters. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **stretching a rubber band** with your two hands. As you pull slowly: - At first, the band resists with a small but proportional force — this is the **elastic region**. - Then it becomes very easy to stretch — this is the **yielding / plastic region**. - Finally, just before it snaps, you see a narrow region become thin and white — this is **necking and stress whitening** in polymers. A **membrane** behaves like a *very thin* rubber band — its strength is reported per unit width (e.g., N/cm) rather than per unit area, because thickness is hard to measure precisely below ~50 µm. A **textile** (e.g., a cotton or polyester fabric strip) behaves like a *net* — when pulled, individual yarns share and redistribute the load, and failure occurs by **sequential yarn rupture** rather than a single clean break. A **fibre** (e.g., a single strand of nylon, silk, or carbon) behaves like a *thin steel wire on a microscopic scale* — it is highly anisotropic, has a near-linear elastic response, and fails at very high stresses with little elongation (for stiff fibres) or very high elongation (for elastomeric fibres like spandex). > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Takeaway:** The *shape* of the σ–ε curve is the "fingerprint" of the material. A stiff brittle fibre (e.g., glass) shows a steep, short curve; a ductile membrane (e.g., LDPE) shows a long, low curve with a yield point; a textile strip shows a *jagged* curve due to progressive yarn failure. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants (in bold) - **Standard Atmosphere:** 23 °C and 50 % RH (ASTM D618 / ISO 23529) - **Standard Gravitational Acceleration:** **g = 9.80665 m s⁻²** - **Typical crosshead speed for films:** 5–500 mm min⁻¹ (most commonly **50 mm min⁻¹** for ASTM D882) - **Typical crosshead speed for fibres:** depends on linear density, but often **10–300 mm min⁻¹** at a constant rate of extension - **Typical specimen gauge length:** films 50–250 mm; fibres 10–500 mm; textiles 75–300 mm - **Young's Modulus units:** **MPa** or **GPa** (1 GPa = 1000 MPa) - **Linear density unit for fibres:** **tex** (g per 1000 m) or **denier** (g per 9000 m) - **Specific strength (modulus / density):** **N·m·kg⁻¹** or **m²·s⁻²** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Tensile Test A tensile test is performed by clamping a specimen between two grips of a UTM, then separating the grips at a **constant crosshead velocity (v)**. The load cell records the instantaneous force **F(t)**, and the extensometer (or the crosshead displacement, corrected for machine compliance) records the elongation **ΔL(t)**. The test consists of four sequential stages: 1. **Grip seating / slack take-up** — initial non-linear region from slack in the specimen; **not** to be used for modulus calculation. 2. **Linear elastic region** — Hooke's law applies, σ = E·ε. 3. **Yielding / plastic region** — irreversible deformation; strain continues to grow with little or no rise in stress. 4. **Failure region** — neck propagation, fibrillation, or catastrophic rupture. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** Always compute **Young's Modulus** as the slope of the *initial linear portion* (typically between **ε = 0.0005 and ε = 0.0025** for plastics per ASTM D638, or as specified per material standard). Using the secant line from origin to peak gives a *secant modulus*, not the true elastic modulus. ## 2.2 Engineering vs. True Stress & Strain Because the cross-section of the specimen reduces as it is stretched (especially after necking), two definitions exist: - **Engineering Stress:** σ = F / A₀ (uses the *original* cross-section) - **True Stress:** σ_true = F / A_i (uses the *instantaneous* cross-section) - **Engineering Strain:** ε = (L − L₀) / L₀ - **True Strain:** ε_true = ln(L / L₀) Assuming **incompressibility** (volume constancy, valid for most polymers up to moderate strains): $$A_i \cdot L_i = A_0 \cdot L_0 \implies A_i = A_0 \cdot \frac{L_0}{L_i} = A_0 \cdot \frac{1}{1+\varepsilon}$$ This gives the **bridging relation** between true and engineering values: $$\sigma_{true} = \sigma \cdot (1+\varepsilon), \qquad \varepsilon_{true} = \ln(1+\varepsilon)$$ ## 2.3 Fibre-Specific Quantities For fibres and yarns, **linear density (T)** is used instead of cross-sectional area (because the cross-section is rarely circular and is hard to measure directly): $$\text{Stress (specific)} = \frac{F}{T} \quad \text{[units: N/tex, cN/dtex, gf/denier]}$$ The standard conversion is: $$1 \;\text{N/tex} = 10 \;\text{cN/dtex} = 11.33 \;\text{gf/denier}$$ **Tenacity** is the *specific tensile strength* of a fibre, i.e., its breaking force divided by its linear density. The corresponding **specific modulus (E/L)** is the tensile modulus divided by the density of the fibre material. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol / Unit | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Engineering Stress | σ = F / A₀ | σ in **MPa**, F in **N**, A₀ in **mm²** | F is load, A₀ is original CSA | | 2 | Engineering Strain | ε = ΔL / L₀ = (L − L₀)/L₀ | ε dimensionless (or %) | L₀ is original gauge length | | 3 | Young's Modulus | E = σ / ε (in linear region) | E in **MPa** or **GPa** | Use secant in elastic zone only | | 4 | Yield Strength | σ_y at onset of plastic flow | **MPa** | Offset 0.2 % method often used | | 5 | Ultimate Tensile Strength | σ_u = max(F) / A₀ | **MPa** | Peak of σ–ε curve | | 6 | Elongation at Break | ε_b = (L_break − L₀) / L₀ | % or dimensionless | L_break measured after fracture | | 7 | Toughness | U_T = ∫σ dε | **MJ m⁻³** | Numerical integration of curve | | 8 | True Stress | σ_true = σ(1 + ε) | **MPa** | Assumes constant volume | | 9 | True Strain | ε_true = ln(1 + ε) | dimensionless | Better for large strains | | 10 | Poisson's Ratio | ν = −ε_lateral / ε_axial | dimensionless | Typically 0.3–0.49 for polymers | | 11 | Specific Stress (fibre) | σ_s = F / T | **N/tex** or **cN/dtex** | T is linear density | | 12 | Tex to denier | 1 denier = 1/9 tex | — | Conversion constant | | 13 | Specific Modulus | E_s = E / ρ | **N·m·kg⁻¹** | ρ is material density | | 14 | Tex definition | 1 tex = 1 g / 1000 m | **g km⁻¹** | Mass per unit length | | 15 | Load to stress (film) | σ = F / (w·t) | w = width, t = thickness | Used for ASTM D882 thin films | > [!NOTE] > **Boundary / Validity Conditions:** > - Linear-elastic modulus is only valid while σ < σ_y. > - True stress formula assumes **incompressible deformation** (ν → 0.5, valid for most elastomers; for glassy polymers, ν ≈ 0.35–0.40 introduces a small error). > - Fibre linear density must be **pre-conditioned and tested under standard atmosphere**. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Tensile testing is the *first-line quality-control* test in the polymer, textile, packaging, and composite industries. Typical applications: - **Packaging films (LDPE, BOPP, PET):** Confirm seal strength, tear resistance, and elongation for forming operations. - **Geotextiles (PP, PET):** Grab tensile strength and elongation determine suitability for soil reinforcement. - **Tyre cord (nylon 6,6; polyester; steel):** Specific tenacity and fatigue life dictate ply design. - **Body armour (Kevlar, UHMWPE):** Specific energy absorption (toughness per unit density) is the design metric. - **Filtration membranes (PVDF, PTFE, PES):** Burst strength and tensile modulus ensure survival under back-pressure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The reason polymers show *such varied* tensile responses is their **semi-crystalline morphology** (crystallinity %, spherulite size, tie-molecule density) and **glass-transition temperature (T_g)** relative to test temperature. A polymer tested *above* T_g is rubbery (low E, high ε); tested *below* T_g, it is glassy (high E, brittle). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: From Load–Extension Data to a Complete Stress–Strain Curve **Given (simulated raw data from a UTM for a PET membrane strip):** | Time (s) | Load F (N) | Extension ΔL (mm) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0.0 | 0.000 | | 2 | 4.2 | 0.040 | | 4 | 8.1 | 0.080 | | 6 | 12.0 | 0.120 | | 8 | 15.5 | 0.165 | | 10 | 18.2 | 0.220 | | 12 | 19.6 | 0.310 | | 14 | 19.4 | 0.450 | | 16 | 18.5 | 0.700 | | 18 | 17.2 | 1.050 | | 20 | 15.8 | 1.450 (rupture) | **Specimen geometry (PET membrane):** - Width: w = 10.0 mm - Thickness: t = 0.050 mm - Original cross-section: A₀ = w × t = 10.0 × 0.050 = **0.50 mm²** - Original gauge length: L₀ = 50.0 mm ### Step 1 — Convert extension to engineering strain Using the relation ε = ΔL / L₀, with L₀ = 50.0 mm: $$\varepsilon_i = \frac{\Delta L_i}{50.0\;\text{mm}}$$ For the first data point (ΔL = 0.040 mm): $$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{0.040}{50.0} = 0.00080 = 0.080\,\%$$ For the rupture point (ΔL = 1.450 mm): $$\varepsilon_b = \frac{1.450}{50.0} = 0.0290 = 2.90\,\%$$ > **Interpretation logic:** Engineering strain is *unitless* and is usually reported in %. A PET membrane is moderately ductile, so a strain of ~3 % at break is consistent with semi-crystalline PET below its T_g (≈ 70 °C). ### Step 2 — Convert load to engineering stress Using σ = F / A₀, with A₀ = 0.50 mm²: $$\sigma_i = \frac{F_i}{0.50\;\text{mm}^2}$$ For the first data point (F = 4.2 N): $$\sigma_1 = \frac{4.2}{0.50} = 8.4\;\text{MPa}$$ For the maximum-load point (F = 19.6 N, at t = 12 s): $$\sigma_u = \frac{19.6}{0.50} = 39.2\;\text{MPa}$$ > **Interpretation logic:** A 0.05 mm thick PET film with 39.2 MPa UTS is consistent with reported values (PET film UTS ≈ 55–75 MPa for unoriented; 200+ MPa for biaxially oriented). ### Step 3 — Compute Young's Modulus (linear region: t = 0 to 6 s) The first four data points lie on a straight line. Using a two-point linear fit between (ΔL = 0.000, F = 0.0) and (ΔL = 0.120, F = 12.0): $$\Delta\sigma = 12.0 / 0.50 - 0 = 24.0\;\text{MPa}$$ $$\Delta\varepsilon = 0.120/50.0 - 0 = 0.0024$$ $$E = \frac{\Delta\sigma}{\Delta\varepsilon} = \frac{24.0}{0.0024} = 10\,000\;\text{MPa} = 10.0\;\text{GPa}$$ > **Interpretation logic:** PET's tensile modulus is ~2–4 GPa for isotropic film; the higher value (10 GPa) suggests the film is *partially oriented* (e.g., machine-direction oriented, MDO PET), which is typical for packaging films. ### Step 4 — Compute Toughness (area under σ–ε curve, trapezoidal rule) Using trapezoidal integration over the 10 non-zero data points: $$U_T = \sum_{i=1}^{9} \frac{1}{2}(\sigma_{i+1}+\sigma_i)(\varepsilon_{i+1}-\varepsilon_i)$$ Computing each trapezoid: | Interval (s) | σ_i (MPa) | σ_{i+1} (MPa) | ε_{i+1}−ε_i | Area (MJ m⁻³) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 0–2 | 0.0 | 8.4 | 0.00080 | 0.00336 | | 2–4 | 8.4 | 16.2 | 0.00080 | 0.00984 | | 4–6 | 16.2 | 24.0 | 0.00080 | 0.01608 | | 6–8 | 24.0 | 31.0 | 0.00090 | 0.02475 | | 8–10 | 31.0 | 36.4 | 0.00110 | 0.03707 | | 10–12 | 36.4 | 39.2 | 0.00180 | 0.06804 | | 12–14 | 39.2 | 38.8 | 0.00280 | 0.10920 | | 14–16 | 38.8 | 37.0 | 0.00500 | 0.18950 | | 16–18 | 37.0 | 34.4 | 0.00700 | 0.24990 | Summing (excluding the last rupture interval, which goes to zero stress — handled separately by adding the triangular tail 0.5·34.4·0.0080 = 0.1376 MJ m⁻³): $$U_T \approx 0.7077 + 0.1376 = 0.845\;\text{MJ m}^{-3}$$ > **Interpretation logic:** Toughness ~0.85 MJ m⁻³ indicates moderate energy absorption — characteristic of a toughened / oriented PET film rather than a brittle PS (which would be <0.1 MJ m⁻³). ### Step 5 — Convert to True Stress & Strain at the Ultimate Point At σ_u = 39.2 MPa, ε_u = 0.220 / 50.0 = 0.0044: $$\sigma_{true,u} = 39.2 \times (1 + 0.0044) = 39.37\;\text{MPa}$$ $$\varepsilon_{true,u} = \ln(1 + 0.0044) = 0.00439$$ At rupture (ε_b = 0.0290): $$\sigma_{true,b} = 0 \times (1 + 0.0290) = 0\;\text{MPa (by definition at break)}$$ $$\varepsilon_{true,b} = \ln(1.0290) = 0.0286 = 2.86\,\%$$ > **Interpretation logic:** At low strains (<1 %), engineering and true stress differ by <1 % — so for stiff, low-elongation polymers the two are essentially interchangeable. The deviation matters only for elastomers and necking ductile polymers. ### Step 6 — Apply the Poisson's-Ratio Volume-Constancy Check For a polymer with ν = 0.38 at small strains, the volume change ΔV/V₀ is: $$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = (1+\varepsilon_x)(1-\nu\varepsilon_x)(1-\nu\varepsilon_x) - 1$$ At ε_x = 0.0024 (top of elastic region): $$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = (1.0024)(1-0.00091)^2 - 1 \approx +0.00058 = +0.058\,\%$$ > **Interpretation logic:** A volume expansion of ~0.06 % is consistent with the *dilatant* (volume-increasing) behaviour typical of glassy polymers under elastic load — confirming the assumption of *near*-incompressibility is reasonable at low strains. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Classifying Polymer Tensile Behaviour | Material Class | Typical E (GPa) | σ_u (MPa) | ε_b (%) | Curve Shape | Failure Mode | Examples | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | Glassy brittle | 2.0 – 3.5 | 30 – 80 | 1 – 5 | Steep, linear, sudden break | Crazing → brittle fracture | PS, PMMA, cured epoxy | | Glassy ductile | 1.5 – 3.0 | 40 – 90 | 20 – 100 | Yield + cold drawing + necking | Ductile with stress whitening | PC, PET (amorphous), ABS | | Semi-crystalline ductile | 0.5 – 2.5 | 15 – 70 | 50 – 1000 | Yield, necking, fibrillation | Ductile with fibril pull-out | LDPE, HDPE, PP, PA6 | | Elastomer | 0.001 – 0.05 | 5 – 30 | 100 – 1000 | S-shaped, no yield | Elastic recovery | NR, SBR, silicone | | High-performance fibre | 50 – 600 | 2000 – 7000 | 0.5 – 5 | Linear, no yield | Brittle fibril failure | Carbon, Kevlar, UHMWPE | | Textile fabric (woven) | 0.5 – 5 (apparent) | 20 – 200 N/cm | 5 – 30 | Jagged, stepwise | Sequential yarn rupture | Cotton, PET woven | | Membrane (thin film) | 1 – 10 | 30 – 250 | 5 – 200 | Depends on orientation | Tear or break | BOPP, PVDF, PES | --- ## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU 2024 Numerical Style **Problem:** A nylon 6 fibre of linear density 2.5 tex is tested on a tensile machine. The fibre breaks at a load of 45 cN. Calculate the tenacity in cN/tex and in N/tex. If the fibre density is 1.14 g cm⁻³, compute the tensile strength in MPa (assume circular cross-section). **Step 1 — Tenacity in cN/tex** $$\text{Tenacity} = \frac{F}{T} = \frac{45\;\text{cN}}{2.5\;\text{tex}} = 18.0\;\text{cN/tex}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to N/tex** $$18.0\;\text{cN/tex} = \frac{18.0}{100}\;\text{N/tex} = 0.180\;\text{N/tex}$$ **Step 3 — Cross-sectional area from tex and density** The cross-sectional area of a circular fibre of linear density T and density ρ is: $$A = \frac{T}{\rho} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-6}\;\text{kg m}^{-1}}{1.14 \times 10^{3}\;\text{kg m}^{-3}} = 2.193 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{m}^2 = 2.193 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mm}^2$$ **Step 4 — Tensile strength in MPa** $$\sigma_u = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{45 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{N}}{2.193 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{mm}^2} = 205.2\;\text{N/mm}^2 = 205.2\;\text{MPa}$$ > **Interpretation:** Nylon 6 tenacity of 18 cN/tex and tensile strength ~205 MPa are *normal values* for a *partially oriented, drawn industrial yarn*. A high-tenacity (HT) nylon 6 yarn typically reaches 80–90 cN/tex, indicating our fibre is a regular textile-grade yarn. --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example — Textile Strip Test (Grab Method) **Problem:** A polyester-cotton (65/35) woven fabric strip of dimensions 100 mm × 150 mm (grip area 25 mm × 150 mm) is tested per ASTM D5035 grab method. The breaking load is 480 N and the elongation at break is 18 %. If the fabric mass per unit area is 220 g m⁻², calculate the (i) grab tensile strength, (ii) specific strength in N·m·g⁻¹, and (iii) toughness (assume a triangular approximation of the σ–ε curve). **Step 1 — Grab tensile strength** For grab method, strength is reported as **load per unit width** (since the full cross-section of the fabric is not uniformly stressed): $$\sigma_{grab} = \frac{F_{max}}{w} = \frac{480\;\text{N}}{25\;\text{mm}} = 19.2\;\text{N/mm} = 1920\;\text{N/cm}$$ **Step 2 — Specific strength (per unit areal density)** $$\sigma_{spec} = \frac{F_{max}}{w \cdot \rho_A} = \frac{480\;\text{N}}{25 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{m} \times 220\;\text{g m}^{-2}} = \frac{480}{5.5} = 87.3\;\text{N/g} = 87\,300\;\text{N·m·kg}^{-1}$$ > **Interpretation:** ~87 kN·m/kg is in the typical range for a woven poly-cotton fabric. **Step 3 — Toughness (triangular approximation)** $$U_T \approx \frac{1}{2} \cdot \sigma_{grab} \cdot \varepsilon_b = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 19.2\;\text{N/mm} \cdot 0.18 = 1.728\;\text{N/mm} = 1.728\;\text{MJ m}^{-3}$$ > **Interpretation:** A real textile curve is *not* triangular — it has a long low-modulus toe region (from yarn crimp removal) and a jagged top. The triangular approximation *overestimates* the true toughness by 20–40 %, so the actual toughness is ~1.0–1.4 MJ m⁻³. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Tensile Testing Workflow for Polymeric Specimens The following Mermaid diagram describes the complete functional workflow of a tensile test on a polymer film, textile, or fibre, including specimen preparation, machine configuration, data acquisition, and analysis pipeline. ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA[Specimen Selection
Membrane OR Textile OR Fibre] --> nodeB[Geometry Measurement
width w, thickness t, gauge length L0] nodeB --> nodeC[Pre-conditioning Chamber
23 C and 50 percent RH for 24 h] nodeC --> nodeD[Grip Installation
Type varies by form:
Pneumatic flat grips for films
Capstan or wrap grips for fibres
Flat rubber-lined grips for textiles] nodeD --> nodeE[Load Cell Calibration
Force range Fs selected for expected peak] nodeE --> nodeF[Extensometer Attachment
Contact or non-contact laser type] nodeF --> nodeG[Crosshead Motion
Constant velocity v mm per min] nodeG --> nodeH[Data Acquisition
Time series of F t and delta L t] nodeH --> nodeI[Signal Processing
Subtract grip slack
Subtract machine compliance] nodeI --> nodeJ[Compute Engineering Stress and Strain
sigma equals F over A0
epsilon equals delta L over L0] nodeJ --> nodeK[Plot Sigma versus Epsilon Curve
Identify elastic region, yield, peak, break] nodeK --> nodeL[Extract Modulus, Yield, UTS, Elongation, Toughness] nodeL --> nodeM[Failure Mode Analysis
Brittle, ductile, fibrillation, necking] nodeM --> nodeN[Report and Compare to Standard
ASTM D882, D5035, D3822, etc.] ``` ## 4.2 Schematic — Typical σ–ε Curve Anatomy for a Ductile Semi-Crystalline Polymer The following Mermaid block emulates a labelled stress–strain curve, identifying each feature that a KTU 2024 student is expected to recognize. ```mermaid flowchart LR pA[Origin
Strain equals 0, Stress equals 0] --> pB[Linear Elastic Region
Slope equals Young Modulus E] pB --> pC[Proportional Limit
End of strict Hookean behaviour] pC --> pD[Elastic Limit
Full recovery upon unloading] pD --> pE[Yield Point
Stress equals sigma y
Onset of plastic flow] pE --> pF[Strain Softening
Stress dips slightly with neck formation] pF --> pG[Neck Propagation
Constant or rising stress] pG --> pH[Strain Hardening
Stress rises due to chain orientation] pH --> pI[Ultimate Tensile Strength
Stress equals sigma u equals peak] pI --> pJ[Failure Point
Strain equals epsilon b equals elongation at break] pJ --> pK[Rupture
Energy released equals toughness U sub T] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Diagram — Selecting the Correct Tensile Test Standard ```mermaid flowchart TD qA[Polymer Specimen Type] --> qB{Is it a thin film or membrane? thickness below 1 mm} qB -- Yes --> qC[Use ASTM D882
ISO 527-3] qB -- No --> qD{Is it a single filament fibre?} qD -- Yes --> qE[Use ASTM D3822
ISO 5079] qD -- No --> qF{Is it a yarn bundle?} qF -- Yes --> qG[Use ASTM D2256
ISO 2062] qF -- No --> qH{Is it a fabric or textile?} qH -- Yes --> qI{Test method?} qI -- Grab --> qJ[ASTM D5035] qI -- Ravel Strip --> qK[ASTM D5034] qI -- Cut Strip --> qL[ISO 13934-2] qH -- No --> qM[Bulk Plastic Dog-bone
Use ASTM D638
ISO 527-1 or 527-2] ``` ## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Relationship Between Material Structure and Tensile Response ```mermaid flowchart TD rA[Polymer Microstructure] --> rB{Chain Architecture} rB -- Linear flexible --> rC[High elongation, moderate E
Example LDPE] rB -- Rigid aromatic backbone --> rD[High E, low elongation, brittle
Example Kevlar PEEK] rB -- Crosslinked network --> rE[Elastomeric, recoverable
Example NR SBR Silicone] rA --> rF{Crystallinity} rF -- Amorphous glassy below Tg --> rG[Glass-like response, brittle or ductile
Example PS PMMA PC] rF -- Semi-crystalline --> rH[Yields and draws, strain hardens
Example HDPE PP Nylon] rF -- Amorphous rubbery above Tg --> rI[Rubbery plateau
Example NR at room temperature] rA --> rJ{Orientation State} rJ -- Isotropic --> rK[Symmetric tensile response in all directions] rJ -- Uniaxially oriented --> rL[Strong along draw direction weak transverse] rJ -- Biaxially oriented --> rM[BOPET BOPP film behaviour] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2] **Differentiate between "engineering stress" and "true stress" in a tensile test. Under what condition is the volume assumed constant for the conversion between the two?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Engineering stress (σ)** is defined as the instantaneous tensile load divided by the *original* (undeformed) cross-sectional area: σ = F / A₀. (1 mark) - **True stress (σ_true)** is the load divided by the *instantaneous* cross-sectional area A_i: σ_true = F / A_i. (1 mark) - **Conversion formula (assuming constant volume):** σ_true = σ (1 + ε), where ε is engineering strain. (1 mark) The volume is assumed constant when the material deforms *incompressibly* — typically when ν → 0.5 (rubbery / elastomeric state) or, to a good approximation, for many polymers at small strains (ε < 0.05). --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module Experiment 2] **Why is "tenacity" (in cN/tex or N/tex) used instead of "tensile strength" (in MPa) for characterising single fibres?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The cross-sectional area of a single fibre is very small (typically 10–100 µm diameter), irregular (often non-circular), and difficult to measure accurately. (1 mark) - **Linear density (tex or denier)** is far easier to measure precisely (by weighing a known length of fibre). (1 mark) - Tenacity = breaking load ÷ linear density, and is a direct measure of the load-bearing capacity per unit mass, allowing fair comparison between fibres of different densities and cross-sections. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) > Choose **ONE** of the following two alternatives. ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern] **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat labelled engineering stress–strain curve for a ductile semi-crystalline polymer, identify and briefly explain the following features: (i) proportional limit, (ii) yield point, (iii) strain-softening region, (iv) strain-hardening region, (v) ultimate tensile strength, and (vi) elongation at break. **(b) [7 marks]** A polyethylene (PE) film strip of original dimensions 100 mm × 10 mm × 0.05 mm (L × w × t) is tested in tension at a crosshead speed of 50 mm/min.
